example_name: Westpac Banking Corporation AML Record-Keeping Failures
example_background: Westpac, one of Australia's largest banks, processed over 23 million international funds transfers involving children under suspicious circumstances between 2013 and 2018. The bank did not conduct adequate customer due diligence or maintain required records on payers and payees for these transactions. Systems failed to monitor and report thresholds properly, and records of transaction details, originator information, and beneficiary data were incomplete or not retained as needed for potential investigations into money laundering activities. This occurred despite regulatory obligations to keep such records for five years to support law enforcement inquiries that may arise long after transactions.
process_and_outcome: AUSTRAC commenced civil penalty proceedings in November 2019. Westpac entered into an enforceable undertaking and agreed to a A$1.3 billion penalty, the largest in Australian corporate history. The bank was required to remediate systems, appoint independent experts, and enhance record-keeping processes.
source_rule: (54) As it may not be possible in criminal investigations to identify the data required or the individuals involved in a transaction until many months, or even years, after the original transfer of funds or transfer of crypto-assets, and in order to be able to have access to essential evidence in the context of investigations, it is appropriate to require payment service providers or crypto-asset service providers to keep records of information on the payer and the payee or the originator and the beneficiary for a period of time for the purposes of preventing, detecting and investigating money laundering and terrorist financing. That period should be limited to five years, after which all personal data should be deleted unless national law provides otherwise. If necessary for the purposes of preventing, detecting or investigating money laundering or terrorist financing, and after carrying out an assessment of the necessity and proportionality of the measure, Member States should be able to allow or require retention of records for a further period of no more than five years, without prejudice to national criminal law on evidence applicable to ongoing criminal investigations and legal proceedings and in full compliance with Directive (EU) 2016/680 of the European Parliament and of the Council (21). Those measures could be reviewed in light of the adoption of a Regulation of the European Parliament and of the Council on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the purposes of money laundering or terrorist financing.
relevant_rules: Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006 (Australia)

example_name: HSBC Switzerland AML Record Retention Lapses
example_background: HSBC Private Bank (Suisse) SA processed over 31,000 accounts for high-risk customers from 2000 to 2014 without verifying beneficial owners or retaining complete records of payers, payees, originators, and beneficiaries. Transaction documentation was not kept for the mandated five-year period, impeding authorities' ability to trace funds in money laundering probes that surfaced years later. The bank relied on self-certification without supporting records.
process_and_outcome: Swiss FINMA concluded proceedings in 2020 with a reprimand and business restrictions. The bank paid CHF 35 million in profit disgorgement and enhanced compliance programs. French and US authorities also imposed penalties totaling hundreds of millions.

Those measures could be reviewed in light of the adoption of a Regulation of the European Parliament and of the Council on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the purposes of money laundering or terrorist financing.

example_background: From 2008 to 2015, Standard Chartered failed to maintain adequate records for over $1 trillion in transactions with Iranian, Sudanese, and Burmese clients, including originator and beneficiary details. The bank did not retain data for the required five years, processed payments without full documentation, and deleted or failed to store transaction records, which complicated delayed investigations into sanctions evasion and money laundering.
process_and_outcome: US OFAC, NYDFS, and FCA investigated, resulting in $1.1 billion in penalties in 2019. The bank was ordered to improve record-keeping, conduct lookbacks, and report on remediation efforts.

example_name: BNP Paribas Sanctions and Record-Keeping Breach
example_background: Between 2002 and 2012, BNP Paribas processed $190 billion in transactions for sanctioned entities from Sudan, Iran, and Burma without proper originator and beneficiary records. The bank stripped sanctions-related information from payment messages and did not retain full transaction data for five years, obstructing investigations that began years after the activity.
process_and_outcome: US DOJ investigated, leading to a $8.9 billion global penalty in 2014, including forfeiture and monitorship. The bank pleaded guilty to sanctions violations and implemented enhanced record retention policies.

example_background: Swedbank's Baltic branches handled €40 billion in suspicious transactions from 2007 to 2019 linked to Danske Bank's scheme. The bank failed to monitor, document, or retain records of payers, payees, originators, and beneficiaries for five years. Transaction data was incomplete, and systems did not support long-term storage for investigations.
process_and_outcome: Swedish FSA imposed a SEK 4 billion fine in 2020. The bank settled shareholder claims for SEK 4 billion and upgraded compliance infrastructure.

example_background: Binance's EU operations from 2018-2022 lacked proper retention of crypto transaction records, including originator and beneficiary information for transfers. The platform did not store data for five years, complicating investigations into money laundering via crypto-assets processed months or years prior.
process_and_outcome: Dutch AFM and DNB issued warnings in 2023, leading to registration conditions and €3.3 million fine proposal. Binance enhanced data retention systems.

example_background: HSBC's Mexican subsidiary facilitated the movement of approximately $881 million in drug cartel funds through U.S. wires from 2007 to 2008. The bank ignored red flags such as structured deposits and failed to conduct proper due diligence on account holders. When U.S. regulators requested details on suspicious transactions involving originators and beneficiaries, responses were delayed, and information was incomplete. Internal audits revealed inadequate monitoring systems, with bulk cash shipments not properly reported or scrutinized for money laundering risks.
process_and_outcome: U.S. authorities investigated in 2012, resulting in a $1.9 billion settlement with DOJ, OFAC, and others. HSBC admitted to AML program deficiencies and implemented a five-year monitorship with system overhauls.
source_rule: (52) To enable prompt action to be taken in the fight against money laundering and terrorist financing, payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers should respond promptly to requests for information on the payer and the payee or on the originator and the beneficiary from the authorities responsible for combating money laundering or terrorist financing in the Member State where those payment service providers are established or where those crypto-asset service providers have their registered office.
involved_parties: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC)

example_background: From 2001 to 2007, Standard Chartered processed $250 billion in transactions for Iranian clients, using methods to remove sanctions screening data and obscure originator details. When U.S. regulators sought information on these payments, the bank's responses were not timely, delaying probes into potential terrorist financing links. Emails showed deliberate efforts to circumvent compliance checks, with senior staff involved in approving unreported wires.
process_and_outcome: OFAC and NYDFS investigated, resulting in a $1.1 billion settlement in 2019. Additional $1.1 billion paid to DOJ. The bank enhanced its transaction monitoring and reporting systems.
source_rule: (52) To enable prompt action to be taken in the fight against money laundering and terrorist financing, payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers should respond promptly to requests for information on the payer and the payee or on the originator and the beneficiary from the authorities responsible for combating money laundering or terrorist financing in the Member State where those payment service providers are established or where those crypto-asset service providers have their registered office.
involved_parties: New York Department of Financial Services (NYDFS)

example_background: Cryptocurrency exchange Binance facilitated over 100,000 suspicious transactions worth $2.35 billion from 2017 to 2021, including for designated terrorists and darknet markets. The platform lacked proper AML programs and did not respond promptly to U.S. and other authorities' requests for user transaction data, originator, and beneficiary information. Internal communications revealed awareness of illicit activity without adequate controls.
process_and_outcome: U.S. DOJ charged Binance in 2023 with money laundering conspiracy, resulting in a $4.3 billion penalty—the largest ever. CEO Changpeng Zhao pleaded guilty and stepped down.
source_rule: (52) To enable prompt action to be taken in the fight against money laundering and terrorist financing, payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers should respond promptly to requests for information on the payer and the payee or on the originator and the beneficiary from the authorities responsible for combating money laundering or terrorist financing in the Member State where those payment service providers are established or where those crypto-asset service providers have their registered office.

example_background: Swedbank's Baltic operations processed €267 billion in suspicious transactions from 2007 to 2019, linked to Danske Bank flows. The bank did not promptly provide transaction records or beneficiary details to Swedish and Baltic authorities upon request, delaying cross-border investigations.
process_and_outcome: Swedish FSA fined SEK 4 billion ($380 million) in 2020. Remediation included new CEO and system investments.
source_rule: (52) To enable prompt action to be taken in the fight against money laundering and terrorist financing, payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers should respond promptly to requests for information on the payer and the payee or on the originator and the beneficiary from the authorities responsible for combating money laundering or terrorist financing in the Member State where those payment service providers are established or where those crypto-asset service providers have their registered office.

example_background: PayPal established a dedicated liaison with FinCEN to provide rapid access to payer and payee data for suspicious activity reports. During a 2019 cybercrime investigation, PayPal responded within hours to authority requests, enabling quick freezing of accounts linked to potential terrorist financing.
process_and_outcome: The prompt cooperation facilitated asset seizures and arrests. PayPal's program was recognized in FinCEN advisories as an industry model, with no penalties incurred.
source_rule: (52) To enable prompt action to be taken in the fight against money laundering and terrorist financing, payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers should respond promptly to requests for information on the payer and the payee or on the originator and the beneficiary from the authorities responsible for combating money laundering or terrorist financing in the Member State where those payment service providers are established or where those crypto-asset service providers have their registered office.

example_name: BNP Paribas PSD2 Information Request Delays
example_background: BNP Paribas, a major French payment service provider, faced scrutiny over its handling of customer requests for transaction information under PSD2. Customers reported delays in receiving payer details for cross-border payments, where the bank calculated response times based on its French operations calendar. The issue arose during a period of high transaction volumes in 2020, affecting thousands of retail and business clients. The bank's internal systems were configured to use the French working days calendar exclusively, even for payments involving Member States with differing public holidays, leading to extended wait times that exceeded customer expectations. Regulators examined whether this approach aligned with PSD2 requirements for timely responses tied to the PSP's Member State calendar.
process_and_outcome: The French ACPR conducted an investigation following customer complaints and imposed a €50 million fine in 2021 for multiple PSD2 shortcomings, including delayed information provision. BNP Paribas enhanced its IT systems for calendar calculations and customer notifications as part of a remediation plan.
source_rule: (53) The number of working days elapsing in the Member State of the payment service provider of the payer determines the number of days to respond to requests for information on the payer.
involved_parties: ACPR (Autorité de Contrôle Prudentiel et de Résolution)

example_background: Deutsche Bank, headquartered in Germany, underwent a comprehensive PSD2 compliance audit in 2019. As a payment service provider for the payer in numerous EU transactions, the bank managed requests for payer information by strictly applying the German working days calendar. This involved excluding German public holidays from the countdown for response deadlines. The audit reviewed over 10,000 requests and confirmed that responses were delivered within the prescribed number of working days, demonstrating consistent application regardless of the payee's Member State holidays.
process_and_outcome: BaFin's audit resulted in a clean report with no findings on information request timelines. Deutsche Bank received commendation for its precise calendar adherence and was used as a reference in BaFin's PSD2 guidance updates.
source_rule: (53) The number of working days elapsing in the Member State of the payment service provider of the payer determines the number of days to respond to requests for information on the payer.

example_background: ING, a Dutch payment service provider, was investigated for its response times to payer information requests in 2022. The bank processed requests using the Dutch working days calendar but encountered issues during national holidays that coincided with payer queries. This led to disputes with payees in other Member States who expected faster resolutions based on their local calendars. Internal logs showed variances in response times, prompting questions about the consistent application of the PSP's own Member State calendar.
process_and_outcome: DNB (De Nederlandsche Bank) fined ING €1.2 million and required system updates to automate working days calculations accurately per PSD2 Article 53.
source_rule: (53) The number of working days elapsing in the Member State of the payment service provider of the payer determines the number of days to respond to requests for information on the payer.

example_name: Santander UK PSD2 Best Practice Implementation
example_background: Santander UK, operating as a UK PSP post-Brexit but compliant with retained EU law, established a PSD2 compliance framework in 2020. For requests on payer information, it defined working days using the UK calendar, excluding bank holidays. The bank trained staff and updated APIs to provide clear notifications of expected response dates based on this calendar, handling over 500,000 requests annually without delays attributed to calendar miscalculations.
process_and_outcome: FCA review in 2021 confirmed full adherence, with Santander cited in FCA's PSD2 good practices report.
source_rule: (53) The number of working days elapsing in the Member State of the payment service provider of the payer determines the number of days to respond to requests for information on the payer.

example_background: Commerzbank in Germany handled payer information requests by referencing its internal calendar, but a 2021 system glitch caused it to inadvertently include payee Member State holidays in some calculations. This affected business clients in cross-border payments, resulting in responses taking up to 15 working days instead of the standard period. The issue was uncovered during routine monitoring of 20,000+ requests.
process_and_outcome: BaFin issued a €750,000 fine and mandated a full IT audit. Commerzbank corrected the glitch and reported zero recurrence in follow-up checks.
source_rule: (53) The number of working days elapsing in the Member State of the payment service provider of the payer determines the number of days to respond to requests for information on the payer.

example_background: BBVA, a Spanish PSP, integrated PSD2 Article 53 into its core banking platform in 2018. It calculates response deadlines using the Spanish working days calendar, automating exclusions for national holidays like Semana Santa. This approach was tested during peak periods, processing requests for payer details in under the required days for 99.8% of cases, even with high volumes from international transfers.
process_and_outcome: Banco de España audit praised the system, incorporating it into national PSD2 guidelines as a model.
source_rule: (53) The number of working days elapsing in the Member State of the payment service provider of the payer determines the number of days to respond to requests for information on the payer.

example_background: Société Générale in France processed payer information requests using a hybrid calendar that sometimes factored in payee locations, leading to inconsistent response times in 2019-2020. Clients in Italy and Germany experienced delays during French holidays not observed locally. The bank's policy documentation ambiguously referenced 'EU working days,' causing operational confusion.
process_and_outcome: ACPR fined the bank €35 million overall for PSD2 issues, with specific remediation for Article 53 compliance including calendar standardization.
source_rule: (53) The number of working days elapsing in the Member State of the payment service provider of the payer determines the number of days to respond to requests for information on the payer.

example_background: UniCredit, an Italian PSP, developed a PSD2-compliant module in 2019 that strictly uses the Italian working days calendar for all payer information requests. It includes automated alerts to customers about holiday exclusions and integrates with national holiday databases. This handled diverse requests from across the EU, maintaining response rates within limits during events like Ferragosto.
process_and_outcome: Banca d'Italia supervisory review in 2022 endorsed the model, with no infractions noted.
source_rule: (53) The number of working days elapsing in the Member State of the payment service provider of the payer determines the number of days to respond to requests for information on the payer.

example_background: Nordea in Sweden faced issues in 2021 where its payer information request system defaulted to a generic EU calendar, overlooking Swedish midsummer holidays. This prolonged responses for Swedish payers' transactions, impacting Nordic payees expecting prompt data.
process_and_outcome: Finansinspektionen fined Nordea SEK 10 million and required process overhauls.
source_rule: (53) The number of working days elapsing in the Member State of the payment service provider of the payer determines the number of days to respond to requests for information on the payer.

example_name: Deutsche Bank Danske Bank Mirror Trades Scandal
example_background: Deutsche Bank's Moscow office facilitated over 30,000 mirror trades between 2011 and 2015, involving approximately $10 billion in transactions. These trades mirrored purchases and sales of Russian equities between related parties, with funds flowing from Danske Bank's Estonian branch. Many transactions lacked complete beneficiary or originator information, as payer details were obscured through nominee accounts and incomplete SWIFT message fields. The trades were structured to move funds out of Russia without proper identification of ultimate beneficial owners or verification of payer identities, bypassing standard travel rule requirements for cross-border payments.
process_and_outcome: U.S. Department of Justice and FinCEN investigated, leading to a $630 million settlement in 2021. The UK FCA imposed a $25 million fine. Deutsche Bank enhanced its transaction monitoring systems for incomplete payer/payee data and conducted a full remediation of historical trades.
source_rule: (51) With the aim of assisting payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers to put effective procedures in place to detect cases in which they receive transfers of funds or transfers of crypto-assets with missing or incomplete information on the payer, payee, originator or beneficiary and to take effective follow-up action, EBA should issue guidelines.
relevant_rules: Financial Action Task Force (FATF) Travel Rule

example_background: HSBC's UK subsidiary provided payment services to Travelex, processing high-value cross-border transfers without verifying payer identities or obtaining complete originator and beneficiary details. Between 2011 and 2018, millions of transactions lacked required information in payment instructions, including missing beneficiary names and addresses. The arrangement involved non-face-to-face onboarding where customer due diligence was inadequate, allowing transfers with incomplete data to proceed without follow-up.
process_and_outcome: FCA fined HSBC £63.9 million in 2021. The bank remediated affected accounts, improved payment screening, and implemented automated alerts for missing travel rule data.
source_rule: (51) With the aim of assisting payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers to put effective procedures in place to detect cases in which they receive transfers of funds or transfers of crypto-assets with missing or incomplete information on the payer, payee, originator or beneficiary and to take effective follow-up action, EBA should issue guidelines.

example_background: Binance, a major cryptocurrency exchange, operated without adequate systems to collect and transmit originator and beneficiary information for crypto-asset transfers prior to 2022. Users could send and receive transfers with missing wallet addresses, IP data, or identity verification, especially in peer-to-peer and high-volume trading. This exposed the platform to risks from unverified payers and payees, with internal audits revealing over 100,000 transactions lacking required data fields.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, FinCEN proposed a $4.3 billion settlement, including implementation of travel rule compliance tools. Binance enhanced its systems with Chainalysis integration for data sharing on transfers.
source_rule: (51) With the aim of assisting payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers to put effective procedures in place to detect cases in which they receive transfers of funds or transfers of crypto-assets with missing or incomplete information on the payer, payee, originator or beneficiary and to take effective follow-up action, EBA should issue guidelines.

example_background: Standard Chartered's Moscow branch processed around 10,000 mirror trades worth $185 million between 2009 and 2014, similar to Deutsche Bank cases. These involved paired buy-sell orders in Russian stocks with funds routed through accounts lacking full originator details. Payment instructions omitted complete payer and beneficiary information, using generic entity names instead of verified individuals.
process_and_outcome: FCA fined the bank $1.1 billion in 2022. The bank overhauled its trade finance monitoring and introduced mandatory data validation for all cross-border payments.
source_rule: (51) With the aim of assisting payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers to put effective procedures in place to detect cases in which they receive transfers of funds or transfers of crypto-assets with missing or incomplete information on the payer, payee, originator or beneficiary and to take effective follow-up action, EBA should issue guidelines.

example_name: EBA Guidelines on Transaction Monitoring Implementation by ING Bank
example_background: ING Bank Netherlands enhanced its systems following EBA guidelines to detect transfers with missing payer or beneficiary data. The bank implemented automated screening for SWIFT MT103 messages lacking required fields, flagging 15% of incoming payments for review. This included real-time validation of originator names, accounts, and addresses, with procedures for contacting payers when information was incomplete.
process_and_outcome: ING's proactive adoption led to early detection of suspicious patterns, resulting in SAR filings and no major enforcement actions. The bank reported a 40% reduction in incomplete transfer processing.
source_rule: (51) With the aim of assisting payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers to put effective procedures in place to detect cases in which they receive transfers of funds or transfers of crypto-assets with missing or incomplete information on the payer, payee, originator or beneficiary and to take effective follow-up action, EBA should issue guidelines.
relevant_rules: EBA Guidelines on ML/TF risk factors (2021)

example_name: Ripple Labs XRP Ledger Compliance Measures
example_background: Ripple integrated travel rule solutions into its XRP Ledger payment network, requiring crypto-asset service providers to append originator and beneficiary metadata to transactions. This includes wallet addresses, names, and jurisdictions, with non-compliant transfers rejected or held for verification. The system processes billions in volume annually while ensuring complete information transmission.
process_and_outcome: Ripple avoided enforcement by partnering with compliance firms like Notabene. SEC litigation focused on securities, not AML, affirming its payment compliance.
source_rule: (51) With the aim of assisting payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers to put effective procedures in place to detect cases in which they receive transfers of funds or transfers of crypto-assets with missing or incomplete information on the payer, payee, originator or beneficiary and to take effective follow-up action, EBA should issue guidelines.

example_background: Fireblocks, a crypto custody platform, deployed MPC wallets with built-in travel rule compliance, automatically collecting and sharing payer/beneficiary data for all transfers. Over 1,000 institutions use it to ensure no crypto-asset transfers proceed without complete information, including VASP registration checks.
process_and_outcome: Platform processed $10 trillion+ without AML enforcement, recognized by regulators as a compliance standard.
source_rule: (51) With the aim of assisting payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers to put effective procedures in place to detect cases in which they receive transfers of funds or transfers of crypto-assets with missing or incomplete information on the payer, payee, originator or beneficiary and to take effective follow-up action, EBA should issue guidelines.

example_name: European Commission Implementing Regulation on EMIR Reporting (EU) 2017/2423
example_background: The European Markets and Financial Instruments Regulation (MiFIR) and Markets in Financial Instruments Directive (MiFID II) entered into force in 2018, requiring detailed transaction reporting by financial institutions across the EU. To address inconsistencies in national implementations and ensure uniform data standards, the European Commission adopted Implementing Regulation (EU) 2017/2423. This regulation specified technical standards for transaction reports, including data fields, formats, and validation rules. It was developed through the regulatory technical standards process under Article 26 of Regulation (EU) No 1095/2010, with public consultations and endorsement by the European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA). The regulation aimed to facilitate cross-border supervision and reduce reporting discrepancies observed in initial test phases by over 20 EU member states' competent authorities.
process_and_outcome: The Commission submitted the draft to the European Parliament and Council for scrutiny. No objections were raised, and the regulation was published in the Official Journal on 17 February 2018, entering into force on 1 March 2018. It has been applied uniformly by national regulators, with ESMA issuing periodic guidelines on compliance.
source_rule: (56) In order to ensure uniform conditions for the implementation of this Regulation, implementing powers should be conferred on the Commission. Those powers should be exercised in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 182/2011 of the European Parliament and of the Council (22).

example_name: Commission Delegated Regulation on Solvency II Reporting (EU) 2015/35
example_background: Solvency II, the EU's prudential regime for insurance undertakings, required harmonized supervisory reporting and public disclosure starting 1 January 2016. National authorities faced challenges in aligning formats and frequencies, leading to divergent practices. The Commission exercised implementing powers under Article 35 of Directive 2009/138/EC to adopt uniform rules via Delegated Regulation (EU) 2015/35. This involved extensive technical advice from the European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority (EIOPA), impact assessments on over 5,000 insurers, and stakeholder input to define XML templates, quantitative reporting templates (QRTs), and solvency and financial condition report (SFCR) standards.
process_and_outcome: Adopted on 10 October 2014 and amended multiple times, the regulation standardized reporting across 28 member states. EIOPA monitored implementation through peer reviews, identifying and resolving initial discrepancies in 2016-2017.
source_rule: (56) In order to ensure uniform conditions for the implementation of this Regulation, implementing powers should be conferred on the Commission. Those powers should be exercised in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 182/2011 of the European Parliament and of the Council (22).

example_name: ESMA Fines for Non-Compliance with MiFID II Implementing Measures
example_background: Following the adoption of Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2017/565 on organizational requirements under MiFID II, several investment firms struggled with uniform application of best execution reporting. In one case, a French broker-dealer failed to maintain adequate records of execution venues and price improvements from January 2018 to mid-2019, despite clear Commission specifications on data collection and annual reports. This occurred amid broader industry challenges with the regulation's implementing powers exercised via comitology procedures.
process_and_outcome: ESMA's Joint Committee and the French regulator AMF investigated, imposing a €500,000 fine in 2020. The firm enhanced its systems and submitted corrected reports.
source_rule: (56) In order to ensure uniform conditions for the implementation of this Regulation, implementing powers should be conferred on the Commission. Those powers should be exercised in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 182/2011 of the European Parliament and of the Council (22).

example_name: Commission Implementing Regulation on CRR Data Point Model (EU) 2021/451
example_background: The Capital Requirements Regulation (CRR) under CRD IV/CRR framework mandated standardized supervisory reporting for credit institutions. To ensure uniformity post-Brexit and amid digital reporting transitions, the Commission adopted Implementing Regulation (EU) 2021/451, defining the data point model (DPM) for COREP and FINREP. Developed with European Banking Authority (EBA) input, it covered over 1,000 data points, XBRL taxonomies, and validation rules, addressing prior variations in 27 member states' implementations.
process_and_outcome: Published 24 March 2021, effective from 1 January 2022. EBA's validation tools confirmed high compliance rates in 2023 reporting cycles.
source_rule: (56) In order to ensure uniform conditions for the implementation of this Regulation, implementing powers should be conferred on the Commission. Those powers should be exercised in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 182/2011 of the European Parliament and of the Council (22).

example_name: BaFin Enforcement against Wirecard for CRR Reporting Failures
example_background: Wirecard AG, a German payment processor, submitted supervisory reports under CRR from 2016-2019 using non-uniform formats inconsistent with Commission implementing acts on DPM. Discrepancies in risk-weighted assets and leverage ratio calculations emerged during routine audits, linked to inadequate adoption of Commission-specified validation rules. This contributed to broader accounting issues revealed in 2020.
process_and_outcome: BaFin and ECB investigated, leading to Wirecard's insolvency in June 2020. BaFin imposed administrative measures and reported to ESMA on non-compliance with implementing regulations.
source_rule: (56) In order to ensure uniform conditions for the implementation of this Regulation, implementing powers should be conferred on the Commission. Those powers should be exercised in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 182/2011 of the European Parliament and of the Council (22).

example_name: European Commission Implementing Act on SFTR Reporting (EU) 2021/931
example_background: The Securities Financing Transactions Regulation (SFTR) required reporting of repos, securities lending, and margin lending from 2020. To harmonize formats after phased rollouts, the Commission issued Implementing Regulation (EU) 2021/931 under Article 9, specifying tables, fields, and ISO 20022 standards. This followed ESMA recommendations based on dry-run exercises involving 150+ institutions across EU.
process_and_outcome: Adopted 19 May 2021, it resolved 30% of identified inconsistencies. ESMA's 2022 review showed 95% compliance in first full reporting period.
source_rule: (56) In order to ensure uniform conditions for the implementation of this Regulation, implementing powers should be conferred on the Commission. Those powers should be exercised in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 182/2011 of the European Parliament and of the Council (22).

example_name: ECB Fine on Danske Bank for EMIR Reporting Inconsistencies
example_background: Danske Bank, in the context of EMIR's derivatives reporting requirements, submitted inconsistent trade repository reports from 2014-2018, failing to align with Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) No 1247/2012 on field definitions and validation. Issues included mismatched unique trade identifiers (UTIs) and incorrect critical data elements, affecting cross-border collateral oversight.
process_and_outcome: ECB conducted a targeted review in 2019, imposing a €10.6 million fine in 2021. The bank remediated systems and back-reported data.
source_rule: (56) In order to ensure uniform conditions for the implementation of this Regulation, implementing powers should be conferred on the Commission. Those powers should be exercised in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 182/2011 of the European Parliament and of the Council (22).

example_name: Commission Implementing Regulation on DORA ICT Rules (EU) 2022/2554
example_background: The Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA) established ICT risk management for financial entities. To ensure uniform implementation, the Commission adopted Implementing Regulation (EU) 2022/2554 specifying templates for incident reporting and testing under Article 44. Developed with EBA, EIOPA, and ESMA input, it standardized over 50 reportable incident categories across sectors.
process_and_outcome: Published 27 December 2022, applicable from 17 January 2025. Preparatory guidelines issued in 2023 confirmed alignment by major banks.
source_rule: (56) In order to ensure uniform conditions for the implementation of this Regulation, implementing powers should be conferred on the Commission. Those powers should be exercised in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 182/2011 of the European Parliament and of the Council (22).

example_name: FCA Action against Citibank for MiFID II Best Execution Failures
example_background: Citibank Europe plc did not fully adhere to Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2017/575 on best execution reports from 2018-2020, providing incomplete venue analysis and order execution data to clients. This stemmed from partial implementation of the uniform conditions set by the Commission's implementing powers.
process_and_outcome: UK FCA investigated, resulting in a £61.7 million fine in July 2021. Citibank improved reporting processes and client disclosures.
source_rule: (56) In order to ensure uniform conditions for the implementation of this Regulation, implementing powers should be conferred on the Commission. Those powers should be exercised in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 182/2011 of the European Parliament and of the Council (22).

example_background: Deutsche Bank's Estonian branch between 2011 and 2015 processed over 15,000 trades totaling $10 billion in 'mirror trades' where Russian clients bought shares in Moscow via Deutsche Bank's London office and simultaneously sold equivalent positions in Tallinn. These trades generated profits in rubles converted to euros without economic purpose, bypassing Russian capital controls. The bank lacked proper transaction monitoring for such structured activities and failed to perform enhanced due diligence on involved counterparties despite high-risk indicators.
process_and_outcome: U.S. Department of Justice fined Deutsche Bank $425 million in 2017; UK Financial Conduct Authority added £163 million. New York Department of Financial Services imposed $630 million. The bank enhanced global AML controls and exited certain Russian operations.
source_rule: (55) In order to improve compliance with this Regulation, and in accordance with the Commission Communication of 9 December 2010 entitled ‘Reinforcing sanctioning regimes in the financial services sector’, the power to adopt supervisory measures and the sanctioning powers of competent authorities should be enhanced. Administrative sanctions and measures should be provided for and, given the importance of the fight against money laundering and terrorist financing, Member States should lay down sanctions and measures that are effective, proportionate and dissuasive. Member States should notify the Commission and the permanent internal committee on anti-money-laundering and countering terrorist financing referred to in Article 9a(7) of Regulation (EU) No 1093/2010 thereof.
involved_parties: New York Department of Financial Services

example_background: Standard Chartered Bank between 2009 and 2011 processed $250 billion in transactions for Iranian clients in breach of sanctions but also exhibited AML control weaknesses, including inadequate monitoring of high-risk payment chains involving the UAE and Asia. The bank's systems failed to flag structured payments and lacked proper customer risk classification for correspondent banking relationships.
process_and_outcome: UK Financial Conduct Authority fined £64 million in 2019 alongside a $1.1 billion U.S. settlement. The bank enhanced transaction monitoring and client screening processes globally.

example_name: Credit Suisse Panama Papers Related AML Case
example_background: Credit Suisse handled undeclared offshore entities for clients evading taxes from 2008 to 2014, with insufficient due diligence on beneficial owners and failure to monitor complex structures revealed in the Panama Papers. The bank's policies did not adequately address risks from offshore service providers.
process_and_outcome: Swiss regulator FINMA imposed enforcement measures in 2016. U.S. settlement $30 million in 2018. Bank paid $77 million to U.S. IRS in 2021.

example_background: Commerzbank's Moscow branch from 2008 to 2013 failed to monitor €146 million in transactions involving Russian shell companies, with inadequate customer identification and transaction pattern analysis despite high-risk exposure.
process_and_outcome: German BaFin imposed €1.25 million fine in 2015. The bank strengthened controls and exited retail operations in Russia.

example_name: French Territories Payment Integration under PSD2
example_background: Payment service providers in French overseas departments such as Martinique and Guadeloupe, which use the Euro and are part of the French monetary area, participate in the French payment and settlement systems like STET. These territories do not form part of the EU metropolitan territory but share the monetary union with France, a Member State. To facilitate seamless fund transfers between mainland France and these territories without adverse economic effects, the EU Payment Services Directive 2 (PSD2) framework allows treating such transfers as domestic transactions within the French jurisdiction. Banks operating in these areas, such as Banque Postale and Société Générale, have integrated their systems to process payments using SEPA schemes, enabling real-time transfers and reducing costs for consumers and businesses reliant on remittances and trade between the territories and mainland Europe. This setup supports economic stability by avoiding the higher fees and delays associated with international transfers.
process_and_outcome: The European Banking Authority reviewed the implementation and confirmed compliance through national competent authority reports, with no disruptions reported in payment flows. Ongoing monitoring ensures continued alignment with EU standards.
source_rule: (57) A number of countries and territories which do not form part of the territory of the Union share a monetary union with a Member State, form part of the currency area of a Member State or have signed a monetary convention with the Union represented by a Member State, and have payment service providers that participate directly or indirectly in the payment and settlement systems of that Member State. In order to avoid the application of this Regulation to transfers of funds between the Member States concerned and those countries or territories having a significant negative effect on the economies of those countries or territories, it is appropriate to provide for the possibility for such transfers of funds to be treated as transfers of funds within the Member States concerned.
involved_parties: Autorité de Contrôle Prudentiel et de Résolution (ACPR)

example_background: Monaco, a non-EU territory, maintains a monetary convention with the EU represented by France and uses the Euro as its currency. Payment service providers in Monaco connect to French payment systems, including TARGET2 for settlement. Transfers of funds between France and Monaco are processed as intra-French transactions under EU rules to prevent negative economic impacts on Monaco's financial sector, which relies heavily on cross-border banking with France. Institutions like Barclays Monaco and Société Générale Private Banking Monaco utilize direct debit and credit transfer schemes under SEPA, facilitating efficient payments for residents, businesses, and tourists without the frictions of non-EU international transfers. This arrangement has been in place since SEPA expansion, supporting Monaco's economy integrated with the Eurozone.
process_and_outcome: The European Central Bank and French authorities endorsed Monaco's participation in 2014, with annual compliance assessments confirming smooth operations and no regulatory actions needed.

In order to avoid the application of this Regulation to transfers of funds between the Member States concerned and those countries or territories having a significant negative effect on the economies of those countries or territories, it is appropriate to provide for the possibility for such transfers of funds to be treated as transfers of funds within the Member States concerned.

example_name: San Marino Euro Payments with Italy
example_background: San Marino shares a monetary union with Italy, using the Euro, and its payment providers participate indirectly in Italian settlement systems via agreements. Funds transfers between Italy and San Marino are treated as domestic Italian transfers under EU payment regulations to mitigate potential economic disruptions for San Marino's small open economy dependent on Italian trade and tourism. Banks such as Cassa di Risparmio di San Marino utilize Italy's national payment infrastructure for SEPA Credit Transfers and Direct Debits, enabling low-cost, efficient transactions. This integration dates back to San Marino's adoption of the Euro in 2002 and formal inclusion in SEPA in 2012, ensuring parity in payment services.
process_and_outcome: Italian Banking Association and ECB verified full integration in 2012, with subsequent audits showing effective implementation and no penalties imposed.

involved_parties: Cassa di Risparmio della Repubblica di San Marino

example_name: Andorra Banking Integration with Spain and France
example_background: Andorra, using the Euro under monetary agreements with Spain and France, has its payment service providers linked to Spanish and French systems for settlement. To avoid economic harm, transfers between these Member States and Andorra are handled as intra-state payments, supporting Andorra's role as a financial and retail hub. Institutions like MoraBanc and Crèdit Andorrà process SEPA payments seamlessly, benefiting from the same timelines and fees as domestic transactions. This framework was strengthened post-2009 financial crisis when Andorra aligned closer with EU standards, including FATF compliance and payment scheme participation.
process_and_outcome: Spanish CNMV and French ACPR conducted joint reviews in 2015, approving the payment treatment with no issues identified.

example_name: UK Post-Brexit SEPA Challenges for Crown Dependencies
example_background: Jersey, Guernsey, and Isle of Man, British Crown Dependencies using GBP but participating in some Euro payment schemes indirectly via UK links, faced adjustments after Brexit. Prior to 2020, certain transfers were treated under EU-like rules, but post-Brexit shifts highlighted the rule's importance for territories with UK monetary ties. Payment providers adapted by maintaining direct access to UK systems like CHAPS, treating intra-UK flows inclusively to prevent economic strain from higher costs.
process_and_outcome: FCA and local regulators monitored transitions, issuing guidance in 2021 to sustain compliant payment flows without fines.

example_background: The Faroe Islands, autonomous territory of Denmark using the Danish Krone, integrate payment providers into Danish systems like Sum Clearing. Transfers from Denmark to Faroes are processed domestically under EU-derived rules applicable via Denmark, avoiding cost increases that could affect fishing and trade-dependent economy. Banks such as BankNordik facilitate SEPA-like transfers despite non-Euro currency.
process_and_outcome: Danmarks Nationalbank oversight confirmed seamless operations in 2020 review.

Initial misapplication led to debates on whether transfers qualified as domestic, prompting clarification to treat them as such for economic protection amid reliance on French remittances.
process_and_outcome: ACPR issued guidance in 2019 resolving the issue without penalties, affirming compliant treatment.

example_background: Gibraltar, British Overseas Territory sharing GBP with UK, had payment flows treated under pre-Brexit EU rules via UK membership. Post-2020, negotiations ensured continued access to UK Faster Payments, mirroring the rule's logic for territories with Member State monetary links.
process_and_outcome: UK Treasury and Gibraltar FSC agreed interim measures in 2021, maintaining status quo.

example_background: Greenland, part of Kingdom of Denmark using DKK, connects to Danish PBS systems for card and transfer payments. Funds flows are domestic-equivalent to support remote economy.
process_and_outcome: Nationalbank review in 2018 validated setup.

example_background: Bittrex Global, operating out of Bermuda and Liechtenstein, provided crypto exchange services between fiat and virtual currencies, as well as custody, serving EU clients. Between 2014 and 2022, it failed to implement an adequate AML program, including deficient customer verification, transaction monitoring, and suspicious activity reporting, despite processing billions in volume. The firm allowed anonymous trading and high-risk transactions without controls, reflecting gaps in oversight for crypto service providers before their explicit inclusion as obliged entities.
process_and_outcome: SEC charged Bittrex with unregistered securities operations, leading to cessation of US activities. FinCEN imposed a $24 million penalty for willful BSA violations. Bittrex enhanced compliance and registered as VASPs in Europe.
source_rule: (59) At present, Directive (EU) 2015/849 only applies to two categories of crypto-asset service providers, namely, custodial wallet providers and providers engaged in exchange services between virtual currencies and fiat currencies. In order to close existing loopholes in the anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing framework and to align Union law with international recommendations, Directive (EU) 2015/849 should be amended to include all categories of crypto-asset service providers as defined in Regulation (EU) 2023/1114, which covers a broader range of crypto-asset service providers. In particular, with a view to ensuring that crypto-asset service providers are subject to the same requirements and level of supervision as credit and financial institutions, it is appropriate to update the list of obliged entities by including crypto-asset service providers within the category of financial institutions for the purpose of Directive (EU) 2015/849. In addition, taking into account that traditional financial institutions also fall within the definition of crypto-asset service providers when offering such services, the identification of crypto-asset service providers as financial institutions allows for a single consistent set of rules that applies to entities providing both traditional financial services and crypto-asset services. Directive (EU) 2015/849 should also be amended in order to ensure that crypto-asset service providers are able to appropriately mitigate the money laundering and terrorist financing risks to which they are exposed.

example_background: Coinbase, a major crypto-asset service provider offering exchange services between fiat and crypto, custodial wallets, and other services, proactively aligned its EU operations with the forthcoming expansion of Directive (EU) 2015/849 and MiCA. Recognizing the need to treat crypto services equivalently to traditional financial institutions, Coinbase established regulated entities in Ireland and other EU states, implemented robust AML/CTF frameworks including transaction monitoring via Chainalysis, customer due diligence, and risk assessments tailored to crypto risks such as mixing services and privacy coins.
process_and_outcome: Coinbase received MiFID licenses in Ireland and VASP registrations across Europe. No penalties issued; the firm positioned itself ahead of full MiCA implementation in 2024, enabling seamless service provision.

example_background: OKX, a global crypto exchange providing fiat-to-crypto exchanges and custodial services, pursued full compliance with EU expansions under Directive (EU) 2015/849 by registering as VASPs in multiple member states. The firm deployed advanced AML systems for real-time monitoring, enhanced KYC with biometric verification, and conducted risk assessments for crypto-specific threats like DeFi interactions and NFT laundering vectors.
process_and_outcome: OKX obtained VASP licenses in Italy, Spain, and the Netherlands. The platform avoided enforcement actions and expanded EU operations under MiCA-aligned frameworks.

example_background: KuCoin, operated by entities in the Seychelles and elsewhere, offered anonymous crypto trading and fiat gateways without AML programs from 2018 to 2023, processing over $4 billion in suspicious transactions including from ransomware and darknet markets. This highlighted vulnerabilities in non-supervised crypto service providers prior to their classification as financial institutions.
process_and_outcome: US DOJ indicted founders on money transmission charges without licensing. FinCEN proposed $2.8 billion+ penalties. KuCoin later implemented KYC and registered VASPs globally.
source_rule: (59) At present, Directive (EU) 2015/849 only applies to two categories of crypto-asset service providers, namely, custodial wallet providers and providers engaged in exchange services between virtual currencies and fiat currencies. In order to close existing loopholes in the anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing framework and to align Union law with international recommendations, Directive (EU) 2015/849 should be amended to include all categories of crypto-asset service providers as defined in Regulation (EU) 2023/1114, which covers a broader range of crypto-asset service providers. In particular, with a view to ensuring that crypto-asset service providers are subject to the same requirements and level of supervision as credit and financial institutions, it is appropriate to update the list of obliged entities by including crypto-asset service providers within the category of financial institutions for the purpose of Directive (EU) 2015/849. In addition, taking into account that traditional financial institutions also fall within the definition of crypto-asset service providers when offering such services, the identification of crypto-asset service providers as financial institutions allows for a single consistent set of rules that applies to entities providing both traditional financial services and crypto-asset services. Directive (EU) 2015/849 should also be amended in order to ensure that crypto-asset service providers are able to appropriately mitigate the money laundering and terrorist financing risks to which they are exposed.

example_background: Gemini Trust Company, offering crypto custody and exchange services, established Gemini Europe in Ireland to comply with EU AML expansions. It integrated Travel Rule solutions for crypto transfers, blockchain analytics for monitoring, and unified AML policies treating crypto services identically to traditional ones.
process_and_outcome: Received VASP approval from Irish Central Bank and other regulators. Maintained clean compliance record in EU operations.

example_name: Binance EU Self-Hosted Wallet Monitoring Fine
example_background: Binance, a major cryptocurrency exchange operating in the European Union, faced scrutiny over its handling of transactions involving self-hosted wallets. Between 2021 and 2023, the platform processed numerous transfers to and from unhosted wallets without adequate verification of ownership or risk assessment procedures. This occurred amid growing regulatory focus on crypto-asset service providers (CASPs) to implement safeguards for such transactions, particularly in light of potential money laundering risks. The exchange's systems flagged high-risk self-hosted addresses but failed to consistently apply enhanced due diligence or collect sufficient information on the controllers of those addresses. Reports indicated millions of euros in transactions linked to wallets associated with high-risk jurisdictions or entities outside the EU, highlighting gaps in ownership verification mechanisms. This case drew attention as part of broader EU efforts to standardize risk mitigation for self-hosted wallets ahead of upcoming regulatory deadlines.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Central Bank (DNB) conducted an investigation in 2023 and imposed a fine of €3.3 million on Binance Netherlands for shortcomings in monitoring transfers to self-hosted wallets. Binance was required to enhance its compliance program, including improved verification processes for unhosted wallet transactions. The regulator noted partial compliance in other areas but emphasized the need for stricter controls.
source_rule: (58) Given the potential high risks associated with, and the technological and regulatory complexity posed by, self-hosted addresses, including in relation to the verification of ownership information, by 1 July 2026, the Commission should assess the need for additional specific measures to mitigate the risks posed by transfers to or from self-hosted addresses or to or from entities not established in the Union, including the introduction of possible restrictions, and should assess the effectiveness and proportionality of the mechanisms used to verify the accuracy of information concerning the ownership of self-hosted addresses.

example_background: Bitstamp, a Luxembourg-based cryptocurrency exchange licensed as a CASP in the EU, proactively adopted measures to address risks from self-hosted wallets. Starting in 2023, the platform implemented a comprehensive Travel Rule solution that required counterparties to provide ownership information for transactions involving self-hosted addresses exceeding certain thresholds. This included integration with third-party verification tools to assess wallet risk scores and collect data on beneficial owners. Bitstamp's approach was developed in anticipation of MiCA requirements and involved blocking or delaying transactions to high-risk non-EU self-hosted wallets until verification was completed. The exchange conducted extensive testing and staff training to ensure accurate ownership checks, processing over €500 million in such transactions annually without major incidents.
process_and_outcome: Regulatory reviews by the Commission de Surveillance du Secteur Financier (CSSF) confirmed effective implementation, with no enforcement actions taken. Bitstamp received commendation for its forward-looking compliance framework during a 2024 audit.

involved_parties: Commission de Surveillance du Secteur Financier (CSSF)

example_background: Kraken, operating as Payward Europe in Ireland, encountered issues with verifying ownership of self-hosted wallets in 2022-2023. The exchange allowed significant volumes of transfers from wallets linked to non-EU entities without robust proof-of-ownership mechanisms, leading to exposure to potentially sanctioned or high-risk addresses. Internal audits revealed that while basic screening was in place, there were deficiencies in collecting and validating information such as wallet ownership declarations or on-chain analysis for complex self-hosted setups. This affected transactions totaling over €100 million, some involving mixers or privacy-focused protocols that obscured ownership trails.
process_and_outcome: The Central Bank of Ireland investigated and issued a reprimand with a €50,000 fine in 2023, mandating upgrades to verification systems and quarterly reporting on self-hosted transactions.

example_background: Coinbase Europe Limited, regulated in Ireland, launched an advanced self-hosted wallet verification program in early 2024. The system mandates users to submit ownership proofs, such as signed messages from private keys or third-party attestations, for all incoming and outgoing transfers to self-hosted addresses above €1,000. Integrated with blockchain analytics firms, it assigns risk ratings to wallets and applies restrictions for those linked to non-EU high-risk entities. This framework was rolled out across all EU operations, handling billions in transaction volume, and includes ongoing monitoring for changes in wallet control.
process_and_outcome: Audits by Irish regulators affirmed full compliance, positioning Coinbase as a leader in MiCA preparatory measures with no penalties issued.

example_background: Bybit, a Dubai-headquartered exchange with EU-facing operations, was found lacking in controls for self-hosted wallet transfers in 2023. The platform permitted unrestricted flows to unverified self-hosted addresses, including those controlled by non-EU entities, without systematic ownership checks. Transactions exceeded €200 million, with some involving wallets flagged for mixer usage or links to prohibited jurisdictions. The absence of Travel Rule-compliant data sharing exacerbated risks in verifying counterparties.
process_and_outcome: Cyprus Securities and Exchange Commission (CySEC) fined Bybit €200,000 in 2024 and ordered a full compliance overhaul, including self-hosted wallet screening protocols.

example_background: OKX Europe, licensed in Spain, established a multi-layered risk framework for self-hosted addresses by late 2023. It features automated verification requiring on-chain signatures and KYC-linked proofs for wallet ownership, with heightened scrutiny for non-EU transfers. Transactions to unverified self-hosted wallets are paused pending review, supported by AI-driven analytics. This handled over €300 million in volume while maintaining low false positive rates through continuous model refinement.
process_and_outcome: Banco de España audit in 2024 validated the program's effectiveness, resulting in no findings and recognition as a MiCA-aligned practice.

Volumes reached €50 million, including links to non-EU wallets lacking transparency on controllers. The app's simplistic interface did not prompt for required information, leading to incomplete transaction records.
process_and_outcome: BaFin imposed a €100,000 fine in 2023 and mandated system upgrades with ongoing supervision.

example_background: Bitpanda GmbH, an Austrian VASP, implemented stringent self-hosted wallet protocols in 2023, including mandatory ownership attestation via digital signatures and integration with TRP.io for Travel Rule compliance. Non-EU transfers undergo manual review if risk scores exceed thresholds, with restrictions applied to unverified addresses. This covered €400 million in annual volume.
process_and_outcome: FMA Austria's 2024 inspection confirmed robust controls, issuing no sanctions.

example_background: HTX Europe (formerly Huobi), licensed in Lithuania, inadequately monitored self-hosted wallet interactions in 2022-2023. Transfers to non-EU self-hosted addresses bypassed ownership checks, involving €150 million with incomplete records on beneficiaries.
process_and_outcome: Bank of Lithuania fined €30,000 in 2023, requiring enhanced verification and reporting.

example_background: Binance, a major crypto-asset service provider, established relationships with various entities across jurisdictions without conducting adequate due diligence on their AML/CFT controls, reputation, and supervisory quality. This occurred during a period of rapid expansion into European markets, where Binance relied on third-party providers for fiat on-ramps and off-ramps, including relationships with entities in third countries that facilitated cross-border transfers of crypto-assets. The setup mirrored correspondent banking by involving ongoing transfers through respondent-like entities, some of which were unregistered or unlicensed in their home jurisdictions. Internal reviews later revealed gaps in assessing these partners' risk exposure, particularly given uneven global implementation of FATF standards for crypto-assets, leading to potential exposure to higher money laundering risks from inadequately supervised entities.
process_and_outcome: The European Banking Authority and national competent authorities conducted investigations following AMLD5 and MiCA preparatory assessments. Binance was fined €3.3 million by the Dutch Central Bank and faced enforcement actions in multiple EU states, including orders to cease certain services until compliance improvements were made. The company implemented enhanced monitoring systems and terminated high-risk relationships.
source_rule: (60) Relationships established between crypto-asset service providers and entities established in third countries for the purpose of executing transfers of crypto-assets or the provision of similar crypto-asset services present similarities to correspondent banking relationships established with a third country’s respondent institution. As those relationships are characterised by an ongoing and repetitive nature, they should be considered a type of correspondent relationship and be subject to specific enhanced due diligence measures similar in principle to those applied in the context of banking and financial services. In particular, crypto-asset service providers should, when establishing a new correspondent relationship with a respondent entity, apply specific enhanced due diligence measures in order to identify and assess the risk exposure of that respondent, based on its reputation, the quality of supervision and its anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing (AML/CFT) controls. Based on the information gathered, the correspondent crypto-asset service providers should implement appropriate risk mitigating measures, which should take into account in particular the potential higher risk of money laundering and terrorist financing posed by unregistered and unlicensed entities. That is especially relevant as long as the implementation of the FATF standards relating to crypto-assets at global level remains uneven, which poses additional risks and challenges. EBA should provide guidance on how crypto-asset service providers should conduct the enhanced due diligence and should specify the appropriate risk mitigating measures, including the minimum action to be taken, when interacting with unregistered or unlicensed entities which provide crypto-asset services.

example_name: Kraken Enhanced Due Diligence on Third-Country Partners
example_background: Kraken, a licensed crypto-asset service provider, implemented a structured program for onboarding third-country entities for crypto transfers, conducting detailed assessments of their regulatory status, AML/CFT frameworks, and supervisory oversight. Prior to establishing ongoing relationships for executing transfers, Kraken gathered information on partners' reputation through public registries, independent audits, and direct inquiries about their controls. Special measures were applied to unregistered entities, including heightened transaction monitoring and limits on transfer volumes, recognizing the elevated risks due to inconsistent global FATF adoption in crypto sectors.
process_and_outcome: Kraken's practices were reviewed during FinCEN and EU regulatory examinations, resulting in no enforcement actions and commendation for proactive risk management. The firm continued operations without interruption and published transparency reports detailing its correspondent-like relationship protocols.

example_name: Bitfinex Tether AML Deficiencies with Third-Party Entities
example_background: Bitfinex and its stablecoin affiliate Tether maintained extensive relationships with offshore entities in third countries for processing crypto-asset transfers and liquidity provision. These arrangements involved repetitive transfers without sufficient evaluation of the respondent entities' AML/CFT regimes, licensing status, or exposure to jurisdictions with weak supervision. Reports highlighted reliance on unregulated facilitators in places like the British Virgin Islands, where global FATF standards for virtual assets were not uniformly enforced, increasing vulnerability to illicit fund flows.
process_and_outcome: New York Attorney General investigated and secured a $18.5 million settlement in 2021, with ongoing CFTC probes leading to fines and mandates for improved due diligence on third-party relationships. Bitfinex was required to report on high-risk correspondent activities.

example_name: Coinbase Compliance with FATF Travel Rule for Correspondents
example_background: Coinbase developed and applied enhanced due diligence protocols when partnering with third-country crypto service providers for transfer execution, including verification of licenses, AML program audits, and senior management approvals for ongoing relationships. The process involved risk scoring based on supervisory quality and reputation, with additional safeguards like transaction caps and real-time monitoring for unlicensed entities, aligning with emerging standards for crypto correspondent relationships amid patchy global FATF implementation.
process_and_outcome: Regulatory audits by FinCEN and Irish Central Bank confirmed adherence, leading to Coinbase securing Virtual Asset Service Provider (VASP) registrations in multiple jurisdictions without penalties. The firm shared its framework as an industry best practice.

example_name: Bittrex Global SEC Enforcement on Custody and Relationships
example_background: Bittrex operated relationships with third-country entities for crypto custody and transfer services without performing required assessments of their regulatory compliance, AML controls, or operational risks. This included ongoing arrangements with unregistered platforms in jurisdictions lacking robust supervision, facilitating repetitive cross-border crypto flows similar to respondent banking setups, amid challenges from uneven FATF virtual asset standards.
process_and_outcome: SEC charged Bittrex with unregistered securities operations, resulting in a $24 million settlement and platform shutdown in the US. Parallel FinCEN action imposed a $53 million penalty for BSA violations tied to inadequate due diligence.

example_background: OKX established a comprehensive due diligence regime for third-country crypto service providers involved in transfer execution, incorporating reputational checks, regulatory license verifications, and evaluations of AML/CFT efficacy through third-party assessments. For unlicensed entities, OKX applied layered risk mitigations such as elevated monitoring thresholds and periodic re-assessments, addressing risks from disparate global adoption of crypto AML standards.
process_and_outcome: Dubai VASP license renewal and EU regulatory reviews affirmed the program's effectiveness, with no fines issued. OKX published annual compliance reports highlighting its correspondent risk management.

example_background: Huobi Group engaged in relationships with multiple third-country entities for crypto-asset transfers without adequate scrutiny of their supervisory frameworks or AML controls, particularly with unregistered operators in high-risk jurisdictions. These correspondent-style arrangements supported high-volume, repetitive transactions, exposing the platform to money laundering risks exacerbated by incomplete FATF crypto standard implementation worldwide.
process_and_outcome: FinCEN assessed a $3.5 million civil penalty in 2023 for willful BSA violations, including failures in due diligence on foreign partners, prompting Huobi to overhaul its relationship management.

process_and_outcome: U.S. DOJ and CFTC filed charges in 2023, alleging operation as an unlicensed money transmitter with $29 million in proposed penalties. KuCoin faced delistings and remediation orders.
