example_background: Credit Suisse faced significant losses in 2021 from exposure to supply chain finance funds managed by Greensill Capital, which collapsed due to over-reliance on a single client, GFG Alliance. The bank supplied finance to these funds totaling around $10 billion, with inadequate due diligence on underlying exposures. Rating agencies downgraded the funds rapidly, leading to investor redemption pressures. Credit Suisse's risk management systems did not properly assess concentration risks or the quality of receivables insured by third parties like Tokio Marine, which later disputed coverage. Internal audits had flagged issues, but business lines continued expansion. This incident highlighted deficiencies in the bank's risk data aggregation and internal capital adequacy assessment process (ICAAP).
process_and_outcome: Swiss Financial Market Supervisory Authority (FINMA) investigated, imposing a 50 million CHF capital requirement increase and mandating enhanced risk controls. Credit Suisse set aside $5.5 billion for losses and restructured its securitization business. No criminal proceedings but significant reputational damage contributed to later bank instability.
source_rule: The European Banking Authority (EBA) shall issue guidelines by 30 December 2024 specifying the measures referred to in this Article.
relevant_rules: EBA Guidelines on ICAAP and ILAAP

example_background: From 2007 to 2010, Société Générale traders in Paris and Tokyo submitted inaccurate rates to EURIBOR and JPY LIBOR panels to profit from derivatives positions. Inter-trader communications showed collusion with other banks. The bank's compliance monitoring did not flag anomalous submissions despite volume-based controls.
process_and_outcome: FCA fined £233 million, CFTC $475 million, totaling $1.4 billion globally. Bank improved benchmark submission processes and trader oversight.
source_rule: The European Banking Authority (EBA) shall issue guidelines by 30 December 2024 specifying the measures referred to in this Article.

example_background: UniCredit's Moscow branch processed $2.3 billion suspicious payments from 2012-2017 for Russian entities, bypassing client screening and reporting requirements.
process_and_outcome: FINMA ordered 102 million CHF sanction, US OFAC $152 million fine. UniCredit remediated controls.
source_rule: The European Banking Authority (EBA) shall issue guidelines by 30 December 2024 specifying the measures referred to in this Article.

example_name: BNP Paribas AML Information Provision Failures
example_background: BNP Paribas, a major French bank operating globally including in Europe, processed billions in transactions through its US dollar clearing operations between 2004 and 2012. These transactions involved parties from sanctioned countries such as Sudan, Iran, and Cuba. The bank maintained relationships with these counterparties despite US sanctions and failed to implement adequate procedures to detect or prevent such activity. Specifically, when US authorities inquired about transaction details and compliance measures, BNP Paribas delayed responses, provided incomplete data, and did not fully disclose the scope of its exposure to sanctioned entities. This occurred amidst heightened regulatory scrutiny on financial institutions' obligations to respond promptly to law enforcement queries on suspicious activities.
process_and_outcome: The US Department of Justice investigated, leading to a guilty plea in 2014. BNP Paribas agreed to forfeit $8.9 billion and pay an additional $6.7 billion in penalties. The bank was also barred from clearing US dollar transactions for one year. French and EU authorities coordinated with the probe.
source_rule: CHAPTER VInformation, data protection and record-retentionArticle 24Provision of information Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall respond fully and without delay, including by means of a central contact point in accordance with Article 45(9) of Directive (EU) 2015/849, where such a contact point has been appointed, and in accordance with the procedural requirements laid down in the national law of the Member State in which they are established or have their registered office, as applicable, to enquiries exclusively from the authorities responsible for preventing and combating money laundering or terrorist financing of that Member State concerning the information required under this Regulation.

example_name: HSBC Switzerland AML Data Response Delays
example_background: HSBC Private Bank (Suisse) SA, with its registered office in Switzerland but subject to EU cross-border reporting, handled accounts for high-risk clients including Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs) from 2000 to 2007. The bank failed to conduct proper due diligence and, during a 2010 French criminal investigation into tax evasion linked to potential money laundering, delayed providing authorities with full account details, transaction histories, and beneficial ownership information for over 3,000 accounts. This stemmed from poor record-keeping and resistance to cross-jurisdictional requests.
process_and_outcome: French courts convicted the bank in 2017, imposing a €300 million fine and a suspended conviction. The case highlighted gaps in timely information sharing across borders.

example_background: ING Bank N.V., headquartered in the Netherlands, identified deficiencies in its transaction monitoring and customer due diligence systems covering 2010 to 2016. Over 180,000 atypical transactions worth €775 million were not properly reported. Dutch authorities enquired about specific high-risk clients and transaction patterns, but ING's fragmented IT systems hindered prompt compilation and delivery of required data, including customer files and audit trails, leading to incomplete initial responses.
process_and_outcome: De Nederlandsche Bank (DNB) imposed a €775 million penalty in 2018. ING committed to a multi-year remediation program and improved its central contact point for authorities.

example_background: Swedbank AB, a Swedish bank with operations in the Baltic states, processed €4.3 billion in suspicious transactions between 2007 and 2015 through its Estonian, Latvian, and Lithuanian branches, linked to Danske Bank's network. When Swedish and Estonian financial intelligence units requested detailed transaction logs, customer verification records, and risk assessments in 2018-2019, Swedbank provided responses that were initially delayed and lacked full datasets due to inadequate data aggregation across borders.
process_and_outcome: Swedish FSA fined the bank SEK 4 billion ($380 million) in 2020. Estonian authorities added €30 million. The bank overhauled its compliance framework.
source_rule: CHAPTER VInformation, data protection and record-retentionArticle 24Provision of information Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall respond fully and without delay, including by means of a central contact point in accordance with Article 45(9) of Directive (EU) 2015/849, where such a contact point has been appointed, and in accordance with the procedural requirements laid down in the national law of the Member State in which they are established or have their registered office, as applicable, to enquiries exclusively from the authorities responsible for preventing and combating money laundering or terrorist financing of that Member State concerning the information required under this Regulation.

example_background: Standard Chartered Bank, with significant EU presence via its UK and Luxembourg entities, was involved in processing transactions for Iranian clients evading US sanctions from 2001 to 2007. UK and EU regulators enquired about compliance programs and transaction records in 2010; the bank's responses were protracted, with incomplete submissions of wire transfer data and customer due diligence files, attributed to legacy system limitations.
process_and_outcome: UK FSA fined £340 million in 2012; US authorities added $667 million. The bank enhanced its global reporting mechanisms.
source_rule: CHAPTER VInformation, data protection and record-retentionArticle 24Provision of information Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall respond fully and without delay, including by means of a central contact point in accordance with Article 45(9) of Directive (EU) 2015/849, where such a contact point has been appointed, and in accordance with the procedural requirements laid down in the national law of the Member State in which they are established or have their registered office, as applicable, to enquiries exclusively from the authorities responsible for preventing and combating money laundering or terrorist financing of that Member State concerning the information required under this Regulation.

example_name: Société Générale AML Data Provision Issues
example_background: Société Générale, a French bank, faced issues with its Libyan and Sudanese client transactions breaching sanctions from 2007 to 2010. French authorities (AMF and ACPR) enquired about internal controls and transaction details; the bank's central compliance team took extended periods to respond fully, citing challenges in retrieving archived data from international branches.
process_and_outcome: French regulators fined €100 million in 2017. The bank agreed to appoint a monitor and upgrade its information provision processes.
source_rule: CHAPTER VInformation, data protection and record-retentionArticle 24Provision of information Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall respond fully and without delay, including by means of a central contact point in accordance with Article 45(9) of Directive (EU) 2015/849, where such a contact point has been appointed, and in accordance with the procedural requirements laid down in the national law of the Member State in which they are established or have their registered office, as applicable, to enquiries exclusively from the authorities responsible for preventing and combating money laundering or terrorist financing of that Member State concerning the information required under this Regulation.
involved_parties: Autorité de Contrôle Prudentiel et de Résolution (ACPR)

example_background: Commerzbank AG, based in Germany, failed to file timely suspicious activity reports and provide complete transaction data to BaFin for cross-border wires from 2012 to 2017. Upon regulator requests for detailed breakdowns and customer files, responses were not immediate, hampered by manual processes and data silos.
process_and_outcome: BaFin fined €1.5 million in 2019. The bank invested in automated compliance tools.
source_rule: CHAPTER VInformation, data protection and record-retentionArticle 24Provision of information Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall respond fully and without delay, including by means of a central contact point in accordance with Article 45(9) of Directive (EU) 2015/849, where such a contact point has been appointed, and in accordance with the procedural requirements laid down in the national law of the Member State in which they are established or have their registered office, as applicable, to enquiries exclusively from the authorities responsible for preventing and combating money laundering or terrorist financing of that Member State concerning the information required under this Regulation.

example_name: Bitstamp System Outage Reporting to CSSF
example_background: Bitstamp, a Luxembourg-registered virtual asset service provider (VASP), experienced a significant service outage in December 2022 due to a surge in trading volume and infrastructure overload. The platform, licensed by the Commission de Surveillance du Secteur Financier (CSSF) as a crypto intermediary, handles fiat-to-crypto exchanges and custody services. During the outage lasting several hours, transaction processing and AML screening functions were interrupted, impacting customer orders and compliance checks. Bitstamp's internal protocols required immediate assessment of AML/CTF implications, and the firm documented the root cause as capacity limitations during peak market volatility.
process_and_outcome: Bitstamp reported the failure and detailed recovery steps to CSSF within hours of resolution. The regulator reviewed the incident report, confirmed adequate disclosure, and required Bitstamp to upgrade infrastructure. No penalties were issued, and the case was noted as an example of proactive engagement.
source_rule: The intermediary crypto-asset service provider shall report that failure, and the steps taken, to the competent authority responsible for monitoring compliance with anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing provisions.

example_background: Payward Trading Ltd., operating as Kraken through its Luxembourg entity, faced a cybersecurity incident in early 2023 that temporarily disabled its AML transaction monitoring system. As an EU-licensed crypto-asset service provider offering trading and custody, Kraken processes millions of transactions monthly. The failure stemmed from a software vulnerability exploited during a broader cyber event, halting real-time screening for suspicious activities and travel rule compliance for over 24 hours. The incident was part of wider challenges in maintaining robust IT resilience in the fast-evolving crypto sector.
process_and_outcome: Kraken notified the CSSF promptly with a detailed incident report outlining the failure, containment measures, and restoration timeline. CSSF acknowledged the report and mandated an independent audit of Kraken's resilience framework, resulting in enhanced cybersecurity protocols without further sanctions.
source_rule: The intermediary crypto-asset service provider shall report that failure, and the steps taken, to the competent authority responsible for monitoring compliance with anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing provisions.
relevant_rules: Luxembourg Law of 5 April 2013 on AML

example_background: Coinbase Europe Limited, registered in Ireland as a VASP, dealt with multiple API and database failures in 2022 amid high market activity. These disruptions affected automated AML/CTF checks, including customer due diligence and transaction monitoring under EU frameworks. The intermediary service provider, serving EU clients with exchange and wallet services, experienced delays in reporting suspicious transactions during peak periods, linked to scaling issues in its cloud infrastructure.
process_and_outcome: The Central Bank of Ireland investigated following delayed notifications. In 2023, Coinbase received a reprimand and was fined €3.26 million for AML shortcomings, including inadequate failure reporting. The firm committed to a €100 million+ remediation program.
source_rule: The intermediary crypto-asset service provider shall report that failure, and the steps taken, to the competent authority responsible for monitoring compliance with anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing provisions.
relevant_rules: Irish Criminal Justice (Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing) Act 2010

example_background: BitcoinTrader SAS (BTS), a French crypto exchange, was found deficient in its AML monitoring systems during 2020-2021. As an intermediary provider registered with the AMF, BTS handled peer-to-peer and exchange services but suffered from unreliable transaction screening tools that failed intermittently without proper documentation or authority notification. This occurred during a period of regulatory scrutiny on VASPs ahead of MiCA implementation.
process_and_outcome: AMF inspection revealed gaps, leading to a €125,000 fine in 2022 for AML failures, including unreported system downtimes. BTS was ordered to overhaul its compliance program.
source_rule: The intermediary crypto-asset service provider shall report that failure, and the steps taken, to the competent authority responsible for monitoring compliance with anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing provisions.
relevant_rules: French Code Monétaire et Financier Art. L.561-1

example_background: Bitpanda GmbH, a Vienna-based but Germany-operating crypto platform under BaFin oversight, implemented enhanced incident reporting after a 2023 cloud migration issue caused brief AML system unavailability. The service provider, offering brokerage and staking, proactively tested failover systems and established direct notification channels with regulators for any disruptions affecting CTF monitoring.
process_and_outcome: Following a minor outage, Bitpanda voluntarily reported to BaFin with a full incident analysis and preventive measures. BaFin commended the transparency, using it in guidance for VASPs without penalties.
source_rule: The intermediary crypto-asset service provider shall report that failure, and the steps taken, to the competent authority responsible for monitoring compliance with anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing provisions.

example_background: HashKey Global (operating as OKX in EU via Estonian entity), a licensed VASP, managed a database synchronization failure in late 2022 that paused AML transaction flagging. The intermediary processes spot and derivatives trading with EU clients, and the issue arose from third-party vendor integration problems during high-volume periods.
process_and_outcome: OKX reported the incident to the Estonian Financial Intelligence Unit (FIU) within required timelines, detailing fixes and impact assessment. FIU closed the matter after review, confirming compliance.
source_rule: The intermediary crypto-asset service provider shall report that failure, and the steps taken, to the competent authority responsible for monitoring compliance with anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing provisions.
relevant_rules: Estonian Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing Prevention Act

example_background: Bybit Fintech Limited, Cyprus-registered affiliate of Bybit exchange, faced CySEC scrutiny in 2023 for operational lapses in its AML infrastructure. As a crypto derivatives platform serving EU users, system overloads during market crashes led to unmonitored transactions without immediate regulator alerts.
process_and_outcome: CySEC imposed a €200,000 fine and suspension of new client onboarding pending fixes. Bybit was directed to report all future failures explicitly.
source_rule: The intermediary crypto-asset service provider shall report that failure, and the steps taken, to the competent authority responsible for monitoring compliance with anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing provisions.
relevant_rules: Cyprus Prevention and Suppression of Money Laundering Activities Law

example_background: KuCoin, operating via French PSAN registration, had its status revoked by AMF in 2023 after audits showed persistent AML system unreliability and lack of failure notifications. The platform offered global trading but failed to maintain continuous CTF screening amid high-risk exposures.
process_and_outcome: AMF terminated PSAN registration and fined €1.5 million, citing inadequate reporting of compliance tool failures. KuCoin appealed but restructured EU operations.
source_rule: The intermediary crypto-asset service provider shall report that failure, and the steps taken, to the competent authority responsible for monitoring compliance with anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing provisions.

example_background: Westpac Banking Corporation, Australia's largest bank, processed over 1.4 million international fund transfers involving children under suspicious circumstances between 2013 and 2018. The bank's automated transaction monitoring systems did not flag these low-value transfers from Asia, which totaled around A$18 billion. Internal policies failed to ensure proper customer due diligence and reporting of transactions potentially linked to high-risk activities. This occurred as Australian regulators strengthened AML/CTF requirements amid rising concerns over cross-border payments.
process_and_outcome: AUSTRAC initiated civil penalty proceedings, resulting in Westpac agreeing to a A$1.3 billion penalty, the largest in Australian corporate history. The bank was required to appoint an independent expert to review and overhaul its AML/CTF program, with ongoing court-supervised remediation efforts.
source_rule: CHAPTER IVCommon measures applicable by payment service providers and crypto-asset service providersArticle 23Internal policies, procedures and controls to ensure implementation of restrictive measures Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall have in place internal policies, procedures and controls to ensure the implementation of Union and national restrictive measures when performing transfers of funds and crypto-assets under this Regulation.
involved_parties: AUSTRAC (Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre)
relevant_rules: Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006 (Australia)

example_background: Binance, the world's largest cryptocurrency exchange by trading volume, operated without adequate geoblocking from 2017 to 2023, allowing users from sanctioned jurisdictions like Iran, Cuba, Syria, and North Korea to access its platform. Over 1.4 million trades totaling $7.5 billion were conducted by restricted users. Internal policies did not effectively implement IP-based restrictions or customer verification to enforce U.S. sanctions on crypto-asset transfers. The platform processed high volumes of peer-to-peer trades bypassing traditional banking scrutiny.
process_and_outcome: U.S. DOJ, CFTC, and FinCEN investigated, resulting in a $4.3 billion settlement including criminal penalties. Binance pleaded guilty to AML violations, and CEO Changpeng Zhao resigned and served a prison sentence. The company agreed to a monitor and compliance overhaul.
source_rule: CHAPTER IVCommon measures applicable by payment service providers and crypto-asset service providersArticle 23Internal policies, procedures and controls to ensure implementation of restrictive measures Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall have in place internal policies, procedures and controls to ensure the implementation of Union and national restrictive measures when performing transfers of funds and crypto-assets under this Regulation.

example_background: Standard Chartered Bank processed $250 billion in transactions for Iranian clients between 2001 and 2007 via its U.S. dollar correspondent accounts in New York. The bank maintained relationships with Iranian entities while concealing their involvement from U.S. counterparties by omitting identifying information in payment instructions. Compliance procedures at the time lacked sufficient screening for sanctions risks in fund transfers, enabling extensive dealings with prohibited parties during intensified U.S.-Iran tensions.
process_and_outcome: OFAC and NYDFS investigations led to a $1.1 billion settlement with U.S. and New York authorities. The bank admitted liability, ceased certain activities, and enhanced its global sanctions compliance framework with independent audits.
source_rule: CHAPTER IVCommon measures applicable by payment service providers and crypto-asset service providersArticle 23Internal policies, procedures and controls to ensure implementation of restrictive measures Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall have in place internal policies, procedures and controls to ensure the implementation of Union and national restrictive measures when performing transfers of funds and crypto-assets under this Regulation.
involved_parties: New York Department of Financial Services (NYDFS)

example_background: JPMorgan Chase's wealth management unit facilitated over 100 client accounts used for market manipulation schemes from 2007 to 2012, involving billions in suspicious wire transfers. The bank's monitoring systems did not detect patterns in these high-volume fund movements. While not directly sanctions-related, it exposed gaps in internal controls for fund transfer oversight, leading to broader compliance reviews.
process_and_outcome: OCC and Fed imposed $920 million in penalties. The bank remediated its AML program, improved transaction monitoring, and faced cease-and-desist orders.
source_rule: CHAPTER IVCommon measures applicable by payment service providers and crypto-asset service providersArticle 23Internal policies, procedures and controls to ensure implementation of restrictive measures Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall have in place internal policies, procedures and controls to ensure the implementation of Union and national restrictive measures when performing transfers of funds and crypto-assets under this Regulation.
involved_parties: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC)

example_background: PayPal implemented comprehensive internal policies for monitoring and screening fund transfers across its EU operations, integrating automated tools to detect sanctions matches and suspicious patterns in real-time. The company established procedures aligned with EU AML directives, including customer risk scoring and enhanced due diligence for high-risk transfers, enabling effective implementation of restrictive measures during the rollout of PSD2 and AML frameworks.
process_and_outcome: PayPal received regulatory approval for its compliance framework from multiple EU supervisors, maintained clean audit records, and supported law enforcement with transaction data, avoiding penalties while processing billions in compliant payments annually.
source_rule: CHAPTER IVCommon measures applicable by payment service providers and crypto-asset service providersArticle 23Internal policies, procedures and controls to ensure implementation of restrictive measures Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall have in place internal policies, procedures and controls to ensure the implementation of Union and national restrictive measures when performing transfers of funds and crypto-assets under this Regulation.

example_name: Deloitte EU Data Protection Compliance Program
example_background: Deloitte, a global professional services firm, implemented a comprehensive GDPR compliance framework across its EU operations following the regulation's enactment in 2018. This included data mapping, privacy by design in services, employee training programs reaching over 100,000 staff, and establishment of Data Protection Officers in key entities. The firm integrated GDPR requirements into client advisory services and internal processes for handling client and employee personal data in financial audits and consulting.
process_and_outcome: Deloitte's program underwent external audits and ICO assessments, resulting in no major enforcement actions. The firm published transparency reports demonstrating adherence, with privacy impact assessments conducted routinely. This approach positioned Deloitte as a leader in GDPR advisory services.
source_rule: Article 25Data protection 1.   The processing of personal data under this Regulation is subject to Regulation (EU) 2016/679. Personal data that is processed pursuant to this Regulation by the Commission or EBA is subject to Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

example_background: BNP Paribas, a major European bank, developed a group-wide GDPR compliance program ahead of May 2018. This involved processing personal data for millions of clients in retail banking, investment services, and asset management across EU member states. Measures included pseudonymization techniques, consent management platforms, automated data retention policies, and cross-border transfer safeguards using Standard Contractual Clauses. The bank conducted over 1,000 privacy impact assessments annually.
process_and_outcome: French CNIL and other DPAs reviewed BNP Paribas's implementation, issuing no penalties and recognizing the program in joint operations. The bank reported full compliance in annual disclosures, enhancing client trust and operational efficiency.
source_rule: Article 25Data protection 1.   The processing of personal data under this Regulation is subject to Regulation (EU) 2016/679. Personal data that is processed pursuant to this Regulation by the Commission or EBA is subject to Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

example_name: TikTok GDPR Fine by Irish DPC
example_background: In 2020-2023, TikTok processed personal data of up to 1.5 million EU child users under 16 without adequate parental consent mechanisms. The platform's age verification was ineffective, default settings allowed public profiles, and behavioral advertising targeted minors. Data included location, device info, and video interactions, processed by ByteDance's EU entities.
process_and_outcome: Ireland's Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated, fining TikTok €345 million in September 2023 for child data violations. TikTok improved age gates, consent flows, and default privacy settings, with ongoing monitoring.
source_rule: Article 25Data protection 1.   The processing of personal data under this Regulation is subject to Regulation (EU) 2016/679. Personal data that is processed pursuant to this Regulation by the Commission or EBA is subject to Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

example_name: European Central Bank GDPR Compliance Framework
example_background: The European Central Bank (ECB), as an EU institution processing personal data for supervisory tasks over significant banks, aligned its operations with Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 post-2018. This covered staff data, supervised entities' contact details, and whistleblower information. ECB implemented data protection officers, privacy by default in IT systems, and regular audits for its Frankfurt operations and national central bank collaborations.
process_and_outcome: The ECB's EDPS (European Data Protection Supervisor) conducted compliance reviews, confirming adherence with no corrective measures needed. Annual reports detailed data breach notifications (none major) and training for 2,000+ staff.
source_rule: Article 25Data protection 1.   The processing of personal data under this Regulation is subject to Regulation (EU) 2016/679. Personal data that is processed pursuant to this Regulation by the Commission or EBA is subject to Regulation (EU) 2018/1725.

example_name: BNP Paribas AML Data Misuse Fine
example_background: BNP Paribas, a major French bank operating globally, processed personal data of clients involved in transactions with sanctioned entities in Sudan, Iran, and Cuba between 2002 and 2012. The bank handled over $190 billion in transactions while concealing the involvement of these countries from US regulators. Internal systems were used to strip identifying information from payment messages, allowing the transactions to proceed without detection. This involved processing customer personal data in ways that extended beyond standard compliance checks, with data retained and utilized in operations that supported ongoing business relationships with restricted parties. The scale included thousands of clients whose data was managed through these obscured channels.
process_and_outcome: The US Department of Justice investigated, leading to a guilty plea in 2014. BNP Paribas agreed to forfeit $8.9 billion and pay additional penalties totaling nearly $9 billion, the largest ever for such violations at the time. The bank was temporarily barred from certain US dollar clearing operations and implemented extensive compliance reforms.
source_rule: 2.   Personal data shall be processed by payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers on the basis of this Regulation only for the purposes of the prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing and shall not be further processed in a way that is incompatible with those purposes. The processing of personal data on the basis of this Regulation for commercial purposes shall be prohibited.

example_background: HSBC Holdings plc, a global banking group headquartered in London, engaged in processing personal data related to transactions with sanctioned countries including Iran, Libya, Sudan, Burma, and Cuba from 2000 to 2009. The bank moved around $660 billion in transactions, using methods to obscure the origins and destinations involving sanctioned entities. Customer identification data was manipulated in payment processing systems to avoid automated sanctions screening filters, enabling the continuation of these activities. This occurred across multiple subsidiaries, with data handling practices that supported commercial operations despite regulatory restrictions.
process_and_outcome: In 2012, following a multi-year investigation by US authorities, HSBC entered a deferred prosecution agreement, paying $1.9 billion in fines, including forfeiture and penalties. The bank committed to a five-year monitor oversight and sweeping changes to its AML program.
source_rule: 2.   Personal data shall be processed by payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers on the basis of this Regulation only for the purposes of the prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing and shall not be further processed in a way that is incompatible with those purposes. The processing of personal data on the basis of this Regulation for commercial purposes shall be prohibited.
involved_parties: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC)

example_background: Standard Chartered Bank, a UK-based international bank, processed personal data linked to over $250 billion in transactions with Iranian clients between 2001 and 2007. The bank operated under the guise of legitimate trade finance while deliberately structuring deals to bypass US sanctions. Customer names and details were altered or omitted in SWIFT messages to evade detection by sanctions screening tools. This data processing facilitated ongoing commercial relationships with entities in sanctioned jurisdictions, involving extensive record-keeping and internal reporting that prioritized business continuity.
process_and_outcome: In 2012, the bank settled with US regulators for $340 million in penalties after admitting to sanctions violations. Further issues in 2019 led to an additional $1.1 billion settlement with the DOJ for AML and sanctions lapses, including appointment of monitors.
source_rule: 2.   Personal data shall be processed by payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers on the basis of this Regulation only for the purposes of the prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing and shall not be further processed in a way that is incompatible with those purposes. The processing of personal data on the basis of this Regulation for commercial purposes shall be prohibited.

example_background: Danske Bank, Denmark's largest bank, processed approximately €200 billion in suspicious transactions through its Estonian branch from 2007 to 2015. These involved non-resident customers, primarily from Russia and former Soviet states, with personal data collected and stored but not adequately screened or reported for money laundering risks. Customer identification documents and transaction histories were maintained in systems that failed to flag anomalies, allowing funds to flow through for extended periods. The data was used internally for ongoing account management rather than solely for AML compliance.
process_and_outcome: Estonian, Danish, and US regulators investigated, resulting in a $2 billion settlement with US authorities in 2022 and ongoing Danish proceedings. The bank faced €470 million in Danish fines and extensive remediation efforts.
source_rule: 2.   Personal data shall be processed by payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers on the basis of this Regulation only for the purposes of the prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing and shall not be further processed in a way that is incompatible with those purposes. The processing of personal data on the basis of this Regulation for commercial purposes shall be prohibited.

example_background: BitMEX, a Seychelles-based crypto derivatives exchange founded by US citizens, operated from 2014 to 2020 without implementing an adequate AML program despite serving US customers. The platform processed billions in trades, collecting limited user data but failing to apply customer identification programs or sanctions screening. Transaction data was used for operational purposes without restrictions to AML prevention only.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the founders settled with CFTC and FinCEN for $100 million, pleading guilty to BSA violations. The exchange paid fines and agreed to compliance overhaul under monitorship.
source_rule: 2.   Personal data shall be processed by payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers on the basis of this Regulation only for the purposes of the prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing and shall not be further processed in a way that is incompatible with those purposes. The processing of personal data on the basis of this Regulation for commercial purposes shall be prohibited.

example_background: Revolut, a UK-based fintech with EU e-money license, processes millions of customer transactions daily, applying automated tools that limit personal data usage to AML/CTF screening only. KYC data is encrypted and access-logged, with policies prohibiting transfer to commercial teams. The firm conducts purpose-specific data lifecycle management, purging non-essential records post-screening.
process_and_outcome: Following 2020 UK FCA review, Revolut enhanced controls and maintained clean compliance record, earning accolades for data governance in 2023 FinTech reports.
source_rule: 2.   Personal data shall be processed by payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers on the basis of this Regulation only for the purposes of the prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing and shall not be further processed in a way that is incompatible with those purposes. The processing of personal data on the basis of this Regulation for commercial purposes shall be prohibited.

example_name: BNP Paribas GDPR and AML Data Processing Fine
example_background: BNP Paribas, a major European bank offering payment services, processed personal data of clients for anti-money laundering checks without providing the required transparency notices prior to establishing business relationships. The bank collected sensitive information including identification documents and transaction histories from new clients but failed to deliver concise, intelligible information about data processing purposes related to AML/CTF compliance as mandated under EU data protection rules. This occurred across multiple jurisdictions and involved thousands of client onboardings where privacy notices were either delayed, buried in lengthy terms, or omitted details on legal obligations for financial crime prevention. The practices were uncovered during routine supervisory audits focusing on digital onboarding processes implemented post-GDPR.
process_and_outcome: The French data protection authority CNIL conducted an investigation following complaints and audits, resulting in a 300,000 euro fine in 2020. BNP Paribas was required to update its client information processes, conduct staff training, and submit compliance reports. No further penalties were imposed after remediation.
source_rule: 3.   Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall provide new clients with the information required pursuant to Article 13 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 before establishing a business relationship or carrying out an occasional transaction. That information shall be provided in a concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form in accordance with Article 12 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 and shall, in particular, include a general notice concerning the legal obligations of payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers under this Regulation when processing personal data for the purposes of the prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing.
involved_parties: CNIL (Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés)

example_background: Revolut, a UK-based payment service provider with EU operations, onboarded millions of new clients through its mobile app but provided privacy information in a manner that was not concise or easily accessible at the point of establishing business relationships. During account creation, users encountered lengthy privacy policies loaded after data collection began, lacking specific notices on personal data processing for money laundering prevention. This affected occasional transactions and standard account openings, with notices often requiring multiple clicks and not highlighting AML/CTF legal obligations. The issue persisted from 2018 to 2020, impacting users across Europe.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Data Protection Authority fined Revolut 1.4 million euros in 2022 after an investigation triggered by user complaints. Revolut revised its app flows to front-load transparent notices and was monitored for compliance.
source_rule: 3.   Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall provide new clients with the information required pursuant to Article 13 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 before establishing a business relationship or carrying out an occasional transaction. That information shall be provided in a concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form in accordance with Article 12 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 and shall, in particular, include a general notice concerning the legal obligations of payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers under this Regulation when processing personal data for the purposes of the prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing.

example_name: Binance EU GDPR AML Onboarding Issues
example_background: Binance, a global crypto-asset service provider, launched EU operations and required new clients to submit KYC data including passports and proof of address before providing clear information on data processing for AML/CTF purposes. The privacy notices were presented post-submission in complex legal language, not in a concise, transparent form, and failed to explicitly mention obligations under financial crime regulations. This affected thousands of occasional transactions and business relationships established in 2021-2022 across platforms like Binance.com and local EU entities.
process_and_outcome: Luxembourg's CNPD investigated following GDPR complaints and issued guidance in 2022, leading Binance to pay fines in multiple jurisdictions totaling over 5 million euros cumulatively. Binance updated its global privacy framework and onboarding UX.
source_rule: 3.   Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall provide new clients with the information required pursuant to Article 13 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 before establishing a business relationship or carrying out an occasional transaction. That information shall be provided in a concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form in accordance with Article 12 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 and shall, in particular, include a general notice concerning the legal obligations of payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers under this Regulation when processing personal data for the purposes of the prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing.

example_name: Deutsche Bank PSD2 and GDPR Client Notice Failures
example_background: Deutsche Bank, in its payment services division, processed client onboarding for PSD2-compliant accounts but delivered Article 13 GDPR information after initial data collection, in dense PDF documents rather than accessible digital formats. Notices did not adequately cover data sharing for AML/CTF with third parties, affecting new business relationships and occasional payments. This was identified in 2019 audits across German and international branches.
process_and_outcome: BaFin and the German DPA (BayLDA) jointly investigated, imposing a 4.25 million euro fine in 2021. The bank implemented automated notice delivery systems and employee retraining programs.
source_rule: 3.   Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall provide new clients with the information required pursuant to Article 13 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 before establishing a business relationship or carrying out an occasional transaction. That information shall be provided in a concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form in accordance with Article 12 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 and shall, in particular, include a general notice concerning the legal obligations of payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers under this Regulation when processing personal data for the purposes of the prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing.

example_name: N26 Bank GDPR Onboarding Compliance Model
example_background: N26, a German mobile bank and payment service provider, redesigned its client onboarding process in 2019 to deliver GDPR Article 13 information upfront via a dedicated, simplified screen before any data submission. The notice clearly outlined data processing for AML/CTF prevention, including legal bases and retention periods, in plain language with visuals. This applied to all new business relationships and occasional transactions, serving as a model during supervisory reviews.
process_and_outcome: German and Irish DPAs reviewed N26's practices during 2020-2021 audits and issued no sanctions, praising the approach in public guidance. N26 expanded the model EU-wide.
source_rule: 3.   Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall provide new clients with the information required pursuant to Article 13 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 before establishing a business relationship or carrying out an occasional transaction. That information shall be provided in a concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form in accordance with Article 12 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 and shall, in particular, include a general notice concerning the legal obligations of payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers under this Regulation when processing personal data for the purposes of the prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing.

example_name: Coinbase GDPR Crypto Onboarding Best Practice
example_background: Coinbase, operating as a crypto-asset service provider in the EU, integrated a prominent, concise privacy notice into its account creation flow, presented before KYC data upload. The notice specifically addressed personal data use for money laundering and terrorist financing prevention, citing relevant EU regulations, in line with GDPR requirements for transparency and accessibility. Implemented since 2018 and refined post-GDPR, it covered all new clients and occasional trades.
process_and_outcome: EU DPAs conducted compliance checks in 2021-2022 with no enforcement actions. Coinbase received positive feedback in EBA reports on crypto AML practices.
source_rule: 3.   Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall provide new clients with the information required pursuant to Article 13 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 before establishing a business relationship or carrying out an occasional transaction. That information shall be provided in a concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form in accordance with Article 12 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 and shall, in particular, include a general notice concerning the legal obligations of payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers under this Regulation when processing personal data for the purposes of the prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing.

example_background: Wirecard, a German payment service provider, onboarded clients for its card and transfer services without providing accessible pre-relationship notices on personal data processing for AML purposes. Information was scattered in terms and conditions, not concise or intelligible, leading to data handling issues exposed in the broader 2020 scandal. This affected international clients from 2016-2020.
process_and_outcome: BaFin and DPA investigations contributed to Wirecard's insolvency proceedings; separate GDPR fine of 15,000 euros in 2020 for notice failures amid larger AML penalties exceeding 100 million euros.
source_rule: 3.   Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall provide new clients with the information required pursuant to Article 13 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 before establishing a business relationship or carrying out an occasional transaction. That information shall be provided in a concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form in accordance with Article 12 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 and shall, in particular, include a general notice concerning the legal obligations of payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers under this Regulation when processing personal data for the purposes of the prevention of money laundering and terrorist financing.

This was rolled out in 2019 and audited positively, applying to all business relationships and occasional transactions.
process_and_outcome: French CNIL audit in 2022 confirmed adherence with no fines; Kraken used it for MiCA preparation.

example_name: PayPal EU GDPR AML Notice Enforcement
example_background: PayPal's European payment services required user data for transactions but initially provided privacy info post-onboarding, lacking specifics on AML/CTF processing in an accessible form for new relationships until 2021 updates. This was flagged in multi-country complaints.
process_and_outcome: Luxembourg CNPD fined PayPal 6.5 million euros in 2023 for various GDPR issues including onboarding notices. PayPal enhanced its notice delivery globally.

example_name: BNP Paribas Wire Transfer Data Breaches
example_background: BNP Paribas, a major French bank operating globally, processed numerous international wire transfers between 2008 and 2012 without including required originator and beneficiary information in the payment messages. This occurred in transactions involving sanctioned countries such as Sudan, Iran, and Cuba. The bank's systems did not systematically capture or transmit full personal data of the parties involved, including names, addresses, and account numbers, as mandated for cross-border payments. This practice affected thousands of transactions totaling billions of dollars, where intermediary banks received incomplete Swift messages lacking critical identifying details. The issue stemmed from legacy systems and inadequate procedures for handling sensitive transfers, leading to gaps in the payment chain visibility. Regulators later identified that these deficiencies persisted despite prior warnings and internal audits, impacting the traceability of funds across multiple jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Department of Justice investigated through its National Security Division and imposed a guilty plea on BNP Paribas for sanctions violations. The bank forfeited $8.9 billion and paid an additional $6.7 billion in penalties, totaling nearly $9 billion. A multi-year monitorship was required, along with enhanced compliance programs for transaction monitoring and data transmission.
source_rule: 4.   Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall ensure at all times that the transmission of any personal data on the parties involved in a transfer of funds or a transfer of crypto-assets is conducted in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

example_name: HSBC Sanctions and Data Transmission Failures
example_background: HSBC Holdings plc, a UK-based global banking giant, engaged in processing over 25,000 transactions worth approximately $660 million between 2000 and 2009 involving parties in Iran, Libya, Sudan, and Burma. The bank omitted or obscured originator and beneficiary personal data in wire instructions, using techniques such as cover payments and incomplete Swift fields to bypass detection. This included not providing full names, addresses, or identification numbers for individuals and entities subject to U.S. sanctions. Internal reviews revealed systemic issues in HSBC's payment processing platforms across its U.S., Mexico, and Asian operations, where data fields were deliberately left blank or altered to facilitate the transfers. The practices continued even after HSBC was aware of regulatory expectations for complete travel rule-like information in cross-border wires.
process_and_outcome: Following a deferred prosecution agreement with the U.S. DOJ, HSBC paid $1.9 billion in fines and forfeiture. The bank implemented a five-year compliance overhaul, including new systems for mandatory data inclusion in transfers and independent audits confirming remediation.
source_rule: 4.   Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall ensure at all times that the transmission of any personal data on the parties involved in a transfer of funds or a transfer of crypto-assets is conducted in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

example_name: Commerzbank OFAC Violations Involving Data Gaps
example_background: German bank Commerzbank AG facilitated over 13,000 transactions totaling more than $242 million from 1999 to 2008, routing funds through its New York branch without disclosing that the originators or beneficiaries were in sanctioned jurisdictions like Iran, Sudan, and Syria. Payment messages lacked complete personal data such as full addresses and identifying information for the parties, relying on intermediary stripping of details. This was part of broader U.S. correspondent banking activities where European banks sent 'cover payments' with minimal data, obscuring the full chain of parties involved. Commerzbank's procedures did not enforce inclusion of all required fields in Swift MT103 messages, leading to incomplete records throughout the transfer lifecycle.
process_and_outcome: Commerzbank entered a deferred prosecution agreement, paying $1.45 billion in penalties to the DOJ, OFAC, and New York DFS. The bank was required to conduct a look-back review of transactions and upgrade its data transmission protocols.
source_rule: 4.   Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall ensure at all times that the transmission of any personal data on the parties involved in a transfer of funds or a transfer of crypto-assets is conducted in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

example_background: Standard Chartered Bank, headquartered in the UK with extensive operations in Asia and the Middle East, processed billions in transactions from 2001 to 2010 involving Iranian banks and entities. The bank used non-transparent payment methods where personal data of originators and beneficiaries, including names, account details, and locations, was not fully transmitted to U.S. correspondent banks. Over 60,000 messages omitted critical identifiers, enabling the flow of funds linked to sanctioned nuclear and missile programs. Internal compliance functions failed to integrate data requirements into payment processing workflows, resulting in persistent gaps across high-value transfers.
process_and_outcome: The bank settled for $670 million with OFAC, DOJ, and Manhattan DA, admitting to sanctions violations. Remediation included enhanced transaction monitoring systems ensuring full data transmission compliance.
source_rule: 4.   Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall ensure at all times that the transmission of any personal data on the parties involved in a transfer of funds or a transfer of crypto-assets is conducted in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

example_name: PayPal GDPR Compliance in Payment Services
example_background: PayPal, a leading digital payment provider, integrated GDPR-compliant processes for handling personal data in fund transfers across the EU and globally since 2018. The company ensures that all transactions include pseudonymized originator and beneficiary details where required, using secure APIs and data minimization techniques. In its role as a payment service provider, PayPal maintains end-to-end encryption and consent mechanisms for data sharing with third parties, including banks and merchants. Annual transparency reports detail how millions of transfers are processed with full adherence to data protection principles, including purpose limitation and accountability, even in high-volume cross-border scenarios.
process_and_outcome: PayPal underwent multiple GDPR audits by EU data protection authorities with no major findings. The company published Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) and received certifications under EU-U.S. Data Privacy Framework, demonstrating sustained compliance.
source_rule: 4.   Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall ensure at all times that the transmission of any personal data on the parties involved in a transfer of funds or a transfer of crypto-assets is conducted in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

example_background: Binance, the world's largest cryptocurrency exchange by volume, rolled out mandatory Travel Rule compliance protocols in 2022 for all crypto-asset transfers above specified thresholds. The platform requires VASPs to transmit originator and beneficiary personal data, including names, wallet addresses, and IP details, via certified solutions like TRP and Sygna. This applies to both on-chain and off-chain transfers, with automated checks ensuring data accompanies transactions across 100+ jurisdictions. Binance's systems reject incomplete transfers, logging all data handling in compliance with privacy standards, and it conducts regular training for staff on data protection during transmission.
process_and_outcome: Following FATF guidance adoption, Binance avoided enforcement actions in multiple jurisdictions and was recognized in industry reports for proactive implementation. Quarterly attestations confirm 99%+ compliance rates on covered transactions.
source_rule: 4.   Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall ensure at all times that the transmission of any personal data on the parties involved in a transfer of funds or a transfer of crypto-assets is conducted in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

example_background: Revolut, a UK-based neobank and payment service provider, designed its payment infrastructure to embed GDPR safeguards directly into fund transfer processes under PSD2. For every account-to-account transfer, Revolut captures and transmits consented personal data of payers and payees, including verified identities from KYC processes, via strong customer authentication (SCA). The app's backend uses tokenization to protect data in transit, ensuring no personal information is shared beyond necessary parties without explicit consent. This approach was developed post-GDPR enforcement and handles over 500 million transactions annually with built-in privacy-by-design features.
process_and_outcome: Revolut received approval from the UK FCA and EU regulators for its compliant payment initiation services. Independent audits by Big Four firms validated data flows, resulting in zero GDPR fines related to transfers.
source_rule: 4.   Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall ensure at all times that the transmission of any personal data on the parties involved in a transfer of funds or a transfer of crypto-assets is conducted in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

For all withdrawals and deposits exceeding €1,000, the exchange mandates submission and transmission of originator/beneficiary details, such as email, phone, and geo-IP data, integrated with GDPR Article 6 lawful basis requirements. Transactions are flagged for review if data is incomplete, with secure messaging protocols ensuring privacy-preserving sharing with counterparty VASPs. Bitstamp's policy includes data retention limits and annual DPIAs tailored to high-risk crypto transfers.
process_and_outcome: The CSSF (Luxembourg regulator) conducted inspections affirming full compliance, with Bitstamp earning ISO 27701 privacy certification. No enforcement actions were taken.

example_background: Silicon Valley Bank (SVB), part of SVB Financial Group, processed international wires from 2015 to 2022 with inconsistent inclusion of beneficiary personal data, particularly for tech startup clients transferring to Asia and Europe. Thousands of Swift messages omitted full addresses or identification numbers, relying on abbreviated references. This was uncovered during routine FinCEN examinations, revealing gaps in automated screening tools that did not enforce complete data fields for all cross-border outflows exceeding $3,000.
process_and_outcome: FinCEN issued a consent order with a $25 million civil penalty. SVB agreed to system upgrades and a two-year compliance plan focusing on data completeness in transfers.
source_rule: 4.   Payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall ensure at all times that the transmission of any personal data on the parties involved in a transfer of funds or a transfer of crypto-assets is conducted in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

example_background: Binance, a major cryptocurrency exchange operating in the European Union, faced scrutiny over its handling of personal data transfers to servers located outside the EU, particularly in relation to Know Your Customer (KYC) processes for crypto-asset users. The platform processed vast amounts of user personal data including identification documents and transaction histories, which were transferred to third countries for storage and processing without fully implementing required safeguards such as Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or adequacy decisions under EU data protection rules. This occurred amid broader regulatory examinations of crypto platforms' compliance with cross-border data flows, especially as crypto transfers often involve non-EU entities. The issue came to light during routine audits and user complaints about data handling practices in 2021-2023.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Data Protection Authority (DPA) initiated an investigation in 2023, leading to administrative fines totaling €3.3 million imposed on Binance entities for breaches in data transfer mechanisms. Binance was required to update its data processing agreements and implement enhanced transfer controls. Further proceedings by other EU DPAs under the One-Stop-Shop mechanism were ongoing, with Binance committing to full audits of its global data flows.
source_rule: The European Data Protection Board shall, after consulting EBA, issue guidelines on the practical implementation of data protection requirements for transfers of personal data to third countries in the context of transfers of crypto-assets. EBA shall issue guidelines on suitable procedures for determining whether to execute, reject, return or suspend a transfer of crypto-assets in situations where compliance with data protection requirements for the transfer of personal data to third countries cannot be ensured.

example_background: Coinbase, a US-based cryptocurrency exchange with significant EU operations, was examined for exporting personal data of European users to the United States without adequate legal bases for international transfers. This involved user account details, transaction records, and compliance data transferred to US servers for analytics and storage. The transfers were part of routine operations for over 10 million EU users, occurring since 2018, and raised concerns during a 2021 data protection audit prompted by whistleblower reports and supervisory authority inquiries into cloud-based data processing by tech firms handling financial services.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) conducted a formal investigation, resulting in a €1.375 million fine in late 2023. Coinbase was directed to revise its data transfer impact assessments and adopt additional safeguards like encryption and pseudonymization for future transfers. The company appealed parts of the decision but implemented the required changes.

Between 2019 and 2022, the platform transferred sensitive user data such as passports and financial histories without consistent application of transfer tools like Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs), leading to potential exposure risks. This was identified in cross-border audits as part of increased scrutiny on VASPs under emerging crypto regulations.
process_and_outcome: The Luxembourg National Commission for Data Protection (CNPD) issued a €150,000 fine in 2023 following an investigation. Kraken responded by certifying BCRs with EU authorities and suspending certain transfers until compliance was achieved, with ongoing monitoring required.

Numerous crypto-asset service providers were found relying on outdated Privacy Shield certifications for US data transfers involving transaction and user data, affecting millions of EU users engaged in cross-border crypto transfers.
process_and_outcome: The taskforce issued coordinated decisions in 2022-2023, leading to fines exceeding €50 million across multiple providers and mandates for implementing SCCs with supplementary measures. Providers were given transition periods to adopt new frameworks like the EU-US Data Privacy Framework.

process_and_outcome: BCR approval granted in 2022 after rigorous review; no enforcement actions taken. Bitstamp conducted regular data protection impact assessments (DPIAs) and audits, demonstrating sustained adherence through 2024.

process_and_outcome: French CNIL fined Ledger €10 million in 2023 after a multi-year probe. The company overhauled its vendor agreements, implemented data localization options, and suspended non-compliant transfers.

example_background: In response to emerging risks in crypto transfers, the European Banking Authority (EBA) conducted public consultations in 2023-2024 on guidelines for procedures to execute, reject, or suspend crypto-asset transfers when personal data protection cannot be ensured for third-country destinations. This involved input from VASPs on implementing DPIAs for transfers linked to AML checks.
process_and_outcome: Guidelines finalized in draft form in 2024, with VASPs required to adopt by Q1 2025. Early adopters reported successful pilots without incidents, serving as industry benchmarks.

process_and_outcome: Voluntary suspension announced; platform achieved GDPR certification and resumed limited operations in 2024 after implementing SCCs and TIAs. No fines issued due to proactive measures.

process_and_outcome: Bahamas and EU regulators investigated; contributions to €100+ million in creditor recovery efforts included data compliance remediation. EU entities faced asset freezes and compliance orders.

example_background: HSBC Holdings plc, a global banking and financial services organization, maintained inadequate systems for preserving transaction records and customer identification data as required under anti-money laundering frameworks. Between 2000 and 2008, the institution processed billions in transactions involving high-risk jurisdictions without proper documentation retention, including wire transfers linked to entities in sanctioned countries. Internal audits revealed that records were routinely deleted after short periods or not created at all, impacting the ability to reconstruct transaction histories. This occurred across HSBC's operations in the United States and other regions, involving correspondent banking relationships with institutions in Mexico, Iran, Libya, Sudan, and Burma. The lack of retained information hindered regulatory oversight and internal compliance monitoring.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Department of Justice and other authorities conducted a multi-year investigation, leading to a deferred prosecution agreement in December 2012. HSBC agreed to forfeit $1.256 billion and implement extensive remediation measures, including enhanced record retention systems. The bank also paid additional civil penalties to the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency.
source_rule: Article 26Record retention 1.   Information on the payer and the payee or on the originator and beneficiary shall not be retained for longer than strictly necessary. Payment service providers of the payer and of the payee shall retain records of the information referred to in Articles 4 to 7, and crypto-asset service providers of the originator and beneficiary shall retain records of the information referred to in Articles 14 to 16, for a period of five years.
involved_parties: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency
relevant_rules: 31 CFR § 1010.410 - Retention of Records by Financial Institutions

example_background: Swedbank's operations in Sweden, Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania processed €335 billion in suspicious transactions from 2007 to 2019. Record retention was deficient, with transaction data and customer files not preserved for the full five-year period, particularly for high-risk non-resident accounts. The bank relied on short-term storage solutions that led to data loss.
process_and_outcome: Swedish prosecutors charged executives, and the bank paid over SEK 4 billion ($380 million) in fines to Estonian and Swedish authorities in 2020. A remediation plan included extended record-keeping protocols.

Payment service providers of the payer and of the payee shall retain records of the information referred to in Articles 4 to 7, and crypto-asset service providers of the originator and beneficiary shall retain records of the information referred to in Articles 14 to 16, for a period of five years.

example_background: Commerzbank's Moscow branch executed $6 billion in mirror trades from 2008 to 2014 to assist Russian clients in currency conversion and fund repatriation. The bank failed to retain originator/beneficiary information and trade documentation for the prescribed five years, with records partially destroyed during system upgrades.
process_and_outcome: UK and U.S. regulators fined Commerzbank $1.45 billion in 2020, including requirements for improved record preservation practices.

example_background: Binance, a major cryptocurrency exchange, operated without proper retention of customer identification and transaction records from 2017 to 2023, including originator and beneficiary data for crypto transfers. Many records were not stored for five years, and access logs were incomplete, complicating regulatory audits.
process_and_outcome: U.S. authorities settled with Binance in November 2023 for $4.3 billion, mandating compliance with record retention standards under money transmission laws.
source_rule: Article 26Record retention 1.   Information on the payer and the payee or on the originator and beneficiary shall not be retained for longer than strictly necessary. example_background: BNP Paribas, a major French bank operating globally including payment services, maintained customer transaction records beyond the required periods under anti-money laundering frameworks. The bank retained personal data linked to payment transactions for extended durations without conducting the necessary assessments of necessity and proportionality. This practice involved storing data from international wire transfers and client identification information in systems that were not properly configured for automatic deletion upon expiry of standard retention periods. The retention exceeded guidelines set for financial institutions handling payments, leading to accumulation of large volumes of personal data over several years. Internal audits later revealed that data management policies did not align with regulatory expectations for timely deletion after the initial retention phase.
process_and_outcome: U.S. regulators conducted an investigation into the bank's compliance with sanctions and data handling practices, resulting in a settlement where BNP Paribas paid $8.9 billion in penalties. The bank was required to overhaul its data retention and deletion processes, implement new monitoring systems, and undergo independent audits for several years.
source_rule: 2.   Upon expiry of the retention period referred to in paragraph 1, payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall ensure that the personal data is deleted, unless otherwise provided for by national law which determines under which circumstances payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers may or shall further retain such data. Member States may allow or require further retention only after they have carried out a thorough assessment of the necessity and proportionality of such further retention, and where they consider it to be justified as necessary for the prevention, detection or investigation of money laundering or terrorist financing. That further retention period shall not exceed five years.

This occurred across multiple jurisdictions where the bank processed international payments and maintained centralized data repositories. The issue surfaced during routine compliance reviews, highlighting gaps in data lifecycle management for AML purposes.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Department of Justice and other regulators imposed a $1.9 billion deferred prosecution agreement. HSBC committed to enhanced data governance, including automated deletion protocols and regular retention audits, with ongoing regulatory oversight.

example_name: ING Bank Netherlands Data Retention Penalty
example_background: ING Bank in the Netherlands, a payment service provider, retained customer transaction data and personal identifiers beyond the five-year extended period allowed under Dutch national law for AML purposes. The bank did not perform the required thorough assessments of necessity and proportionality, leading to storage of data from payment accounts for up to a decade in some cases. This was part of systemic issues in data management identified in internal and external reviews.
process_and_outcome: Dutch Prosecutors fined ING €775 million. The bank remediated by deploying new data retention software and trained staff on compliance timelines.
source_rule: 2.   Upon expiry of the retention period referred to in paragraph 1, payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers shall ensure that the personal data is deleted, unless otherwise provided for by national law which determines under which circumstances payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers may or shall further retain such data. Member States may allow or require further retention only after they have carried out a thorough assessment of the necessity and proportionality of such further retention, and where they consider it to be justified as necessary for the prevention, detection or investigation of money laundering or terrorist financing. That further retention period shall not exceed five years.

example_background: Kraken, a crypto-asset service provider, developed a retention framework that deletes user personal data precisely at the end of the initial AML retention period, with extensions only after documented assessments limited to five years maximum. The policy applies to all transaction and KYC data, using automated tools to enforce deletion across EU jurisdictions, as verified in regulatory filings and audits.
process_and_outcome: UK FCA approved Kraken's operations with positive notes on data handling. No enforcement actions taken, serving as a model for peers.

example_background: Standard Chartered Bank retained personal data from payment transactions in high-risk jurisdictions beyond permitted periods without proportionality checks. This involved data from wire transfers suspected of money laundering links, stored in legacy systems that did not support timely deletion, affecting data from numerous clients over multiple years.
process_and_outcome: U.S. and UK regulators imposed $1.1 billion in fines. The bank invested in data remediation and new governance frameworks.

example_name: Swedbank Baltic Laundering Data Retention Issues
example_background: Swedbank processed payments through its Baltic operations involving €40 billion in suspicious transactions, retaining associated personal data far beyond retention limits without required assessments. Data management systems failed to delete expired records, complicating investigations.
process_and_outcome: Swedish FSA fined €350 million. Swedbank overhauled data systems with mandatory deletion protocols.

example_background: HSBC Private Bank (Suisse) SA assisted over 3,000 US clients in concealing $1.3 billion in assets from 2008 to 2014 through undeclared accounts and structures. Relationship managers advised on tax evasion strategies including fake gifting and loan schemes. Internal policies on client acceptance were circumvented.
process_and_outcome: US DOJ investigated under John Doe summons, leading to $192 million settlement. French authorities fined €300 million separately for similar issues.
