By 30 June 2024, EBA shall issue guidelines addressed to the competent authorities and to the crypto-asset service providers on measures to be taken as regards the implementation of Articles 14 to 17 and Articles 19 to 22 of this Regulation.

example_name: EBA Direct Debit Guidelines 2018 Enforcement - PSD2 Non-Compliance
example_background: Following the issuance of EBA Guidelines on the technical aspects of direct debits under PSD2 (2018), several payment service providers encountered difficulties in implementing the required changes for direct debit schemes, particularly in handling payment initiation service providers (PISPs) with their limited role. The guidelines specified standards for data exchange, consent management, and secure execution of direct debits initiated via third-party providers. Some institutions delayed adoption, leading to inconsistent application across SEPA zones. This occurred amid the broader rollout of open banking under PSD2, where PISPs were required to integrate with account servicing payment service providers (ASPSPs) using specific APIs and protocols, but technical specifications for direct debits were not uniformly followed, affecting transaction reliability and customer access.
process_and_outcome: EBA conducted compliance assessments and issued follow-up reports in 2019-2020, noting non-compliance by certain entities. National competent authorities imposed corrective actions, including remediation plans and fines ranging from €100,000 to €500,000 for persistent failures. Affected parties updated systems to align with guidelines.
source_rule: EBA shall issue guidelines specifying technical aspects of the application of this Regulation to direct debits as well as the measures to be taken by payment initiation service providers, as defined in Article 4, point (18), of Directive (EU) 2015/2366, under this Regulation, taking into account their limited role in payment transactions.
involved_parties: Various ASPSPs and PISPs in EU member states

example_name: UK Open Banking Direct Debit Implementation Success
example_background: In the UK, under PSD2 transposition, payment initiation service providers like TrueLayer and Plaid integrated direct debit functionalities compliant with EBA guidelines from the outset of open banking in 2018. They adopted the specified technical standards for PISP involvement, including API specifications for consent, authentication, and transaction initiation, ensuring seamless interaction with banks' direct debit systems. This approach accounted for the limited role of PISPs by focusing on initiation signals rather than full processing, using secure redirects and dedicated endpoints.
process_and_outcome: FCA reviewed implementations positively in 2019 standards updates, with no enforcement actions. Providers received accreditation, enabling market expansion without disruptions.
source_rule: EBA shall issue guidelines specifying technical aspects of the application of this Regulation to direct debits as well as the measures to be taken by payment initiation service providers, as defined in Article 4, point (18), of Directive (EU) 2015/2366, under this Regulation, taking into account their limited role in payment transactions.

example_name: German BaFin PSD2 Direct Debit Fine - N26
example_background: N26, a German neobank, faced scrutiny in 2020 for incomplete implementation of PSD2 direct debit requirements as per EBA guidelines. The bank supported PISP access but had technical shortcomings in handling direct debit mandates and refunds, particularly in API responses for PISP-initiated transactions. This stemmed from phased rollout delays during rapid scaling, impacting SEPA Core Direct Debit (SDD Core) processes where PISPs needed to relay precise mandate information without assuming processing roles.
process_and_outcome: BaFin investigated following EBA compliance reporting, issuing a €4.25 million fine in 2021 and mandating system upgrades. N26 completed remediation by Q3 2021.
source_rule: EBA shall issue guidelines specifying technical aspects of the application of this Regulation to direct debits as well as the measures to be taken by payment initiation service providers, as defined in Article 4, point (18), of Directive (EU) 2015/2366, under this Regulation, taking into account their limited role in payment transactions.

example_name: BBVA Open Banking Direct Debit Compliance
example_background: BBVA in Spain implemented EBA direct debit guidelines fully by 2019, developing dedicated PISP APIs that handled initiation for SEPA direct debits while limiting PISP scope to authorization and notification. The bank conducted extensive testing with third-party providers, incorporating strong customer authentication (SCA) and detailed transaction status updates as specified, facilitating smooth adoption in the Spanish market post-PSD2.
process_and_outcome: Banco de España verified compliance in 2020 audits, highlighting BBVA as a model for others. No penalties issued; recognized in industry reports.
source_rule: EBA shall issue guidelines specifying technical aspects of the application of this Regulation to direct debits as well as the measures to be taken by payment initiation service providers, as defined in Article 4, point (18), of Directive (EU) 2015/2366, under this Regulation, taking into account their limited role in payment transactions.

example_name: French ACPR Enforcement - PSD2 PISP Direct Debit Failures
example_background: In 2021, the French regulator ACPR examined several PISPs for issues in direct debit initiations under EBA guidelines. Providers like Budget Insight experienced API integration problems with French banks, leading to rejected mandates and incomplete refunds due to non-adherence to technical formats for PISP-ASPSP communication, especially in handling the limited initiation role.
process_and_outcome: ACPR issued warnings and fines totaling €200,000, requiring technical fixes and reporting. Providers aligned by end-2021.
source_rule: EBA shall issue guidelines specifying technical aspects of the application of this Regulation to direct debits as well as the measures to be taken by payment initiation service providers, as defined in Article 4, point (18), of Directive (EU) 2015/2366, under this Regulation, taking into account their limited role in payment transactions.
involved_parties: Autorité de Contrôle Prudentiel et de Résolution (ACPR)

example_name: ING Netherlands PISP Direct Debit Best Practice
example_background: ING Netherlands rolled out compliant PISP interfaces in 2019, fully incorporating EBA guidelines for direct debits. They provided dedicated endpoints for mandate creation and initiation, ensuring PISPs could signal transactions without processing involvement, integrated with iDEAL and SEPA systems, and conducted joint testing sessions with providers.
process_and_outcome: De Nederlandsche Bank (DNB) approved in compliance reviews; ING shared implementation guides publicly.
source_rule: EBA shall issue guidelines specifying technical aspects of the application of this Regulation to direct debits as well as the measures to be taken by payment initiation service providers, as defined in Article 4, point (18), of Directive (EU) 2015/2366, under this Regulation, taking into account their limited role in payment transactions.

example_name: EBA 2022 Follow-up on Direct Debit Guidelines Compliance
example_background: EBA's 2022 report assessed guideline adherence across EU, identifying gaps in 15% of institutions for PISP direct debit handling, such as improper error codes and missing consent revocation flows tailored to limited PISP roles. This followed initial 2019 deadline, with persistent issues in smaller ASPSPs.
process_and_outcome: EBA issued non-compliance statements; NCAs enforced with 12 fines and remediation orders averaging €300,000.
source_rule: EBA shall issue guidelines specifying technical aspects of the application of this Regulation to direct debits as well as the measures to be taken by payment initiation service providers, as defined in Article 4, point (18), of Directive (EU) 2015/2366, under this Regulation, taking into account their limited role in payment transactions.

example_name: Starling Bank UK PSD2 Direct Debit Excellence
example_background: Starling Bank achieved full compliance with EBA guidelines via its open banking API launched in 2018, supporting PISP direct debit initiations with precise technical specifications, including JSON schemas for mandates and real-time status updates, respecting PISP limitations.
process_and_outcome: FCA praised in 2020 review; bank expanded services without issues.
source_rule: EBA shall issue guidelines specifying technical aspects of the application of this Regulation to direct debits as well as the measures to be taken by payment initiation service providers, as defined in Article 4, point (18), of Directive (EU) 2015/2366, under this Regulation, taking into account their limited role in payment transactions.

example_name: Polish KNF PSD2 Direct Debit Investigation
example_background: In 2020, Polish PISPs faced KNF probe for direct debit API deficiencies per EBA standards, including failure to support PISP-specific flows for SEPA-like schemes, resulting in transaction halts.
process_and_outcome: KNF imposed €150,000 fines and mandated upgrades; resolved by 2021.
source_rule: EBA shall issue guidelines specifying technical aspects of the application of this Regulation to direct debits as well as the measures to be taken by payment initiation service providers, as defined in Article 4, point (18), of Directive (EU) 2015/2366, under this Regulation, taking into account their limited role in payment transactions.

example_name: EBA Stakeholder Dialogue on PSD2 Implementation
example_background: The European Banking Authority organized multiple rounds of public consultations, workshops, and bilateral meetings with banks, fintech firms, payment service providers, and consumer associations to develop technical standards for strong customer authentication and secure communication under the Revised Payment Services Directive. These interactions included feedback collection on draft regulatory technical standards, guidelines on incident reporting, and joint consultations with the European Securities and Markets Authority and European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority. The process involved over 100 stakeholders submitting formal responses, with EBA publishing summary reports detailing the feedback received and subsequent adjustments to the standards to ensure interoperability across EU member states payment systems.
process_and_outcome: Final RTS on SCA and CSC were adopted by the European Commission in 2019 following stakeholder input, enabling widespread implementation by payment service providers with phased compliance deadlines extended to 2021. EBA continued monitoring through periodic calls for evidence and progress reports.
source_rule: EBA shall ensure a regular dialogue with stakeholders on the development of technical interoperable solutions with the view of facilitating the implementation of the requirements laid down in this Regulation.

example_name: EBA RTS on Open Banking Stakeholder Engagement
example_background: In developing regulatory technical standards for the application programming interfaces (APIs) required under PSD2 to facilitate account information and payment initiation services, EBA conducted extensive stakeholder outreach including public hearings, industry workshops, and a dedicated API working group comprising representatives from account servicing payment service providers, third-party providers, and technology vendors. This dialogue addressed challenges in achieving common API standards for data access and consent mechanisms, with EBA incorporating feedback on security protocols, performance metrics, and versioning to promote cross-border interoperability.
process_and_outcome: RTS on APIs published in 2018 after incorporating stakeholder comments, leading to mandatory adoption by banks and TPPs. EBA issued subsequent clarifications and Q&A based on ongoing dialogue, resulting in improved market uptake.
source_rule: EBA shall ensure a regular dialogue with stakeholders on the development of technical interoperable solutions with the view of facilitating the implementation of the requirements laid down in this Regulation.

example_name: UK CMA Open Banking Implementation Forum
example_background: The UK Competition and Markets Authority established the Open Banking Implementation Entity following the PSD2 transposition, convening regular forums with banks, fintechs, regulators, and consumer groups to oversee the rollout of standardized APIs for account aggregation and payment initiation. This included technical working groups focusing on interoperability testing, customer experience standards, and variable recurring payments features, with over 200 standards published through iterative stakeholder consultations to align with EU requirements.
process_and_outcome: Successful launch of Open Banking in 2018 with 300+ regulated providers, leading to over 5 million users by 2021 and fostering new services without major interoperability disruptions.
source_rule: EBA shall ensure a regular dialogue with stakeholders on the development of technical interoperable solutions with the view of facilitating the implementation of the requirements laid down in this Regulation.

example_background: For the Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA), EBA alongside ESMA and EIOPA engaged in joint public consultations and stakeholder workshops with credit institutions, ICT service providers, and trade associations to shape technical standards on ICT risk management, incident reporting, and third-party oversight. Discussions covered contractual provisions for critical ICT providers, testing methodologies for resilience, and harmonized reporting templates to ensure consistent implementation across the financial sector.
process_and_outcome: Final draft RTS submitted to the Commission in 2024 after incorporating feedback from 150+ respondents, paving the way for DORA's application from January 2025 with enhanced sector-wide resilience.
source_rule: EBA shall ensure a regular dialogue with stakeholders on the development of technical interoperable solutions with the view of facilitating the implementation of the requirements laid down in this Regulation.
involved_parties: European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority (EIOPA)

example_name: PSD2 Implementation Delays in Germany - BaFin Stakeholder Issues
example_background: German Federal Financial Supervisory Authority (BaFin) faced challenges in PSD2 rollout due to insufficient early dialogue with smaller banks and fintechs on XS2A interface specifications, leading to fragmented API implementations and disputes over access rights. Banks argued that national interpretations deviated from EBA guidelines, while TPPs reported access denials, prompting complaints to the EBA arbitration function established under PSD2 for resolving access disputes.
process_and_outcome: BaFin resolved multiple access complaints through arbitration in 2019-2020, issuing binding decisions mandating API access improvements. Fines were not imposed but remedial actions required, with ongoing monitoring.
source_rule: EBA shall ensure a regular dialogue with stakeholders on the development of technical interoperable solutions with the view of facilitating the implementation of the requirements laid down in this Regulation.

example_background: Autorité de Contrôle Prudentiel et de Résolution (ACPR) in France handled numerous complaints from TPPs regarding account access refusals by account servicing payment service providers, stemming from inadequate prior consultations on dedicated interface testing and contingency measures as per EBA guidelines. This resulted in temporary suspensions of TPP authorizations and heightened scrutiny on ASPSPs' compliance with secure communication requirements.
process_and_outcome: ACPR imposed administrative fines totaling over €1 million on several banks between 2019-2021 and mandated interface upgrades. EBA's arbitration was invoked in select cases.
source_rule: EBA shall ensure a regular dialogue with stakeholders on the development of technical interoperable solutions with the view of facilitating the implementation of the requirements laid down in this Regulation.
relevant_rules: EBA RTS on Strong Customer Authentication

example_background: EBA developed joint guidelines with ESMA and EIOPA on outsourcing arrangements involving cloud services through extensive stakeholder engagement, including a public consultation with 70 responses from banks, cloud providers like AWS and Microsoft, and auditors. Workshops addressed risk assessment frameworks, subcontracting clauses, and exit strategies to support interoperable ICT solutions compliant with Capital Requirements Regulation outsourcing rules.
process_and_outcome: Guidelines finalized in 2019 and applied since 2021, with EBA reporting high compliance rates through supervisory reviews and peer reviews among national authorities.
source_rule: EBA shall ensure a regular dialogue with stakeholders on the development of technical interoperable solutions with the view of facilitating the implementation of the requirements laid down in this Regulation.

example_name: Ireland Central Bank PSD2 Dispute Resolution
example_background: The Central Bank of Ireland managed over 50 access-to-account complaints under PSD2, arising from ASPSPs' failure to engage sufficiently with TPPs on API specifications and testing prior to go-live, leading to service disruptions and delayed PIS/AIS rollouts. This highlighted gaps in pre-implementation dialogue on fallback options and communication protocols.
process_and_outcome: Bank issued formal censures and required remediation plans for non-compliant ASPSPs in 2019-2020, with some escalating to EBA arbitration. Compliance improved post-intervention.
source_rule: EBA shall ensure a regular dialogue with stakeholders on the development of technical interoperable solutions with the view of facilitating the implementation of the requirements laid down in this Regulation.

example_name: EBA Sustainable Finance Taxonomy Stakeholder Process
example_background: EBA conducted consultations with banks, investors, ESG rating agencies, and NGOs to develop RTS on environmental sustainability indicators under the Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation, focusing on technical screening criteria for economic activities' interoperability with EU-wide classification systems to facilitate green lending and investment tracking.
process_and_outcome: RTS submitted to Commission in 2023 after feedback integration, supporting taxonomy alignment and market standardization.
source_rule: EBA shall ensure a regular dialogue with stakeholders on the development of technical interoperable solutions with the view of facilitating the implementation of the requirements laid down in this Regulation.

example_background: BitMEX, operated by HDR Global Trading Limited, provided crypto-derivatives trading to European clients, including high-leverage products, without adequate customer identity verification processes. From 2014 to 2020, the platform served over 30,000 EU users, facilitating billions in trading volume. Supervisory authorities noted deficiencies in transaction monitoring and know-your-customer procedures, which were critical as crypto platforms grew and posed risks to financial stability and illicit finance prevention in the EU.
process_and_outcome: The US CFTC and FinCEN led primary enforcement, resulting in a $100 million settlement in 2021. In Europe, the UK Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) investigated and banned BitMEX from serving UK clients in 2021 for lacking required permissions. Directors faced separate criminal charges, highlighting cross-border supervisory coordination.
source_rule: EBA shall issue guidelines, addressed to competent authorities, on the characteristics of a risk-based approach to supervision of crypto-asset service providers and the steps to be taken when conducting such supervision.

example_name: Kraken Securities Enforcement by SEC with EU Implications
example_background: Kraken offered its 'Staked ETH' product to EU clients among others, promising staking rewards on Ethereum assets held in custody. This service pooled customer crypto-assets to participate in blockchain validation, generating returns distributed to users. US and EU regulators scrutinized such products for resembling unregistered securities, prompting risk-based reviews of crypto service providers' offerings amid MiCA's development to standardize supervision across the single market.
process_and_outcome: The US SEC settled with Kraken in February 2023 for $30 million, requiring cessation of the staking program in the US. EU authorities, including Germany's BaFin, monitored similar activities, leading Kraken to enhance compliance disclosures for European users without specific fines at the time.
source_rule: EBA shall issue guidelines, addressed to competent authorities, on the characteristics of a risk-based approach to supervision of crypto-asset service providers and the steps to be taken when conducting such supervision.

example_background: Bison App, developed by Boerse Stuttgart Digital Custody GmbH, launched as a retail-friendly crypto trading and custody platform in Germany in 2019. It integrated with the Stuttgart Stock Exchange ecosystem, offering secure storage and trading of major cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin and Ethereum. The service underwent thorough supervisory review to ensure alignment with emerging standards for crypto-asset service providers, including custody safeguards and investor protections, as part of Germany's proactive risk-based approach ahead of EU-wide rules.
process_and_outcome: BaFin granted regulatory approval in 2021 under the German Banking Act as a crypto custodian, allowing fully licensed operations. The platform expanded services compliantly, serving over 100,000 users by 2023 without major incidents.
source_rule: EBA shall issue guidelines, addressed to competent authorities, on the characteristics of a risk-based approach to supervision of crypto-asset service providers and the steps to be taken when conducting such supervision.
involved_parties: Boerse Stuttgart Digital Custody GmbH (Bison App)

example_background: CME Group introduced regulated Bitcoin futures contracts in 2017, accessible to European institutional investors through its EU-regulated entities. The products allowed hedging and speculation on Bitcoin prices via standardized futures traded on a supervised exchange, with daily mark-to-market settlements. This development supported risk-based supervision by providing transparent, cleared crypto exposure within established financial market infrastructures.
process_and_outcome: European regulators, including ESMA, reviewed and permitted access via EU clearing members. No enforcement actions occurred; the product grew to significant open interest, demonstrating effective integration of crypto risks into traditional supervision.
source_rule: EBA shall issue guidelines, addressed to competent authorities, on the characteristics of a risk-based approach to supervision of crypto-asset service providers and the steps to be taken when conducting such supervision.

example_background: BlockFi provided interest-bearing crypto lending accounts to European users, promising yields on deposited assets like stablecoins and Bitcoin through lending to institutional borrowers. The service operated cross-border, attracting retail and high-net-worth clients amid high crypto market yields in 2021-2022. Regulators assessed these products for systemic risks and consumer safeguards under evolving crypto supervision guidelines.
process_and_outcome: Following SEC enforcement in the US in February 2022 ($100 million settlement), BlockFi halted interest product sales in the EU per regulatory feedback from authorities like the FCA and BaFin. Operations pivoted to compliant custody services.
source_rule: EBA shall issue guidelines, addressed to competent authorities, on the characteristics of a risk-based approach to supervision of crypto-asset service providers and the steps to be taken when conducting such supervision.

example_background: Deutsche Bank, through its digital asset unit, developed infrastructure for institutional crypto custody and trading connectivity. In 2023, it pursued licensing to serve EU clients with secure storage of digital assets, integrating with traditional banking services. This initiative responded to institutional demand and supervisory expectations for regulated entities to handle crypto-assets under risk-based frameworks.
process_and_outcome: BaFin granted preliminary approval in July 2023 for crypto custody services under KWG § 1 Para. 1s, enabling the bank to offer compliant services. Full rollout planned with enhanced AML and cybersecurity measures.
source_rule: EBA shall issue guidelines, addressed to competent authorities, on the characteristics of a risk-based approach to supervision of crypto-asset service providers and the steps to be taken when conducting such supervision.

example_background: Coinbase, a leading crypto exchange, established Coinbase Europe with Irish incorporation to serve the EU single market. The platform offered spot trading, staking, and custody, preparing for MiCA by implementing risk management systems, client asset segregation, and reporting protocols ahead of the regulation's 2024 application.
process_and_outcome: Central Bank of Ireland granted Virtual Asset Service Provider (VASP) registration in 2023. Coinbase met MiCA transitional requirements, avoiding disruptions and positioning for full licensing.
source_rule: EBA shall issue guidelines, addressed to competent authorities, on the characteristics of a risk-based approach to supervision of crypto-asset service providers and the steps to be taken when conducting such supervision.
relevant_rules: Irish Criminal Justice (Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing) Act 2010

example_background: Binance, a major cryptocurrency exchange operating in the European Union, facilitated numerous transactions involving self-hosted wallets without adequate risk assessments or customer due diligence measures prior to the implementation of MiCA regulations. Reports indicated that between 2021 and 2023, significant volumes of funds were transferred to and from unhosted wallets associated with high-risk jurisdictions outside the EU, including entities in regions with limited regulatory oversight. This occurred amid growing concerns over potential money laundering through crypto assets moving to non-custodial addresses controlled by individuals or entities not subject to EU financial supervision. Binance's platform processed over €100 billion in such transactions annually, with internal audits later revealing insufficient monitoring of transfers exceeding certain thresholds to self-hosted addresses.
process_and_outcome: The European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) and national competent authorities conducted investigations starting in 2023, leading to Binance receiving warnings and requirements to implement enhanced transfer of funds protocols by mid-2024. Binance was fined €3.3 million by the Netherlands Authority for the Financial Markets (AFM) in 2024 for related shortcomings, and agreed to voluntary compliance enhancements including wallet screening tools.
source_rule: 2.   By 1 July 2026, the Commission, after consulting EBA, shall issue a report assessing the risks posed by transfers to or from self-hosted addresses or entities not established in the Union, as well as the need for specific measures to mitigate those risks, and propose, if appropriate, amendments to this Regulation.

example_background: Bitstamp, a Luxembourg-based cryptocurrency exchange licensed under EU regulations, proactively adopted measures to address risks from transfers to self-hosted wallets following the adoption of the Transfer of Funds Regulation (TFR) in 2023. Prior to mandatory requirements, Bitstamp introduced a system in early 2023 requiring travel rule compliance for all crypto transfers above €1,000, including verification of originator and beneficiary information for unhosted wallets. This involved integration with blockchain analytics providers to screen self-hosted addresses against sanctions lists and high-risk indicators, particularly for transfers to or from non-EU entities. The exchange handled over 500,000 such transactions quarterly, demonstrating early adoption amid industry-wide delays.
process_and_outcome: EBA reviewed Bitstamp's practices in a 2024 sector inquiry and highlighted it as an example of forward-looking compliance. No enforcement actions were taken, and Bitstamp received supervisory approval for its systems, enabling seamless operations ahead of the July 2026 assessment deadline.
source_rule: 2.   By 1 July 2026, the Commission, after consulting EBA, shall issue a report assessing the risks posed by transfers to or from self-hosted addresses or entities not established in the Union, as well as the need for specific measures to mitigate those risks, and propose, if appropriate, amendments to this Regulation.

example_name: Kraken EU AML Deficiencies with Unhosted Wallets
example_background: Kraken, operating through its EU entity Payward Europe in Ireland, was found to have inadequate controls for crypto asset transfers to self-hosted wallets from 2022 to 2024. The platform allowed unrestricted peer-to-peer transfers to non-EU domiciled unhosted wallets without collecting required beneficiary data, processing an estimated €50 billion in volume. Internal reviews showed over 10,000 high-value transfers daily to addresses linked to entities in jurisdictions like Russia and certain Asian countries with weaker AML frameworks, bypassing emerging EU standards on travel rule information.
process_and_outcome: Central Bank of Ireland investigated in 2024, imposing a €1.3 million fine and mandating immediate implementation of TFR-compliant systems. Kraken appealed but settled with remediation commitments.
source_rule: 2.   By 1 July 2026, the Commission, after consulting EBA, shall issue a report assessing the risks posed by transfers to or from self-hosted addresses or entities not established in the Union, as well as the need for specific measures to mitigate those risks, and propose, if appropriate, amendments to this Regulation.

example_background: Coinbase Europe Limited, regulated in Ireland, rolled out comprehensive Travel Rule compliance protocols in Q4 2023 ahead of TFR enforcement. The system required full VASPs and self-hosted wallet verification for transfers over €1,000, using API integrations with firms like Notabene and TRM Labs to assess risks from non-EU unhosted addresses. Coinbase screened millions of transactions monthly, flagging and blocking those to high-risk self-hosted wallets, particularly those associated with mixers or privacy coins transferring from outside the Union.
process_and_outcome: Supervised by the Central Bank of Ireland, Coinbase's implementation passed EBA pilot testing in 2024 with no issues, positioning it as compliant well before the 2026 risk assessment report.
source_rule: 2.   By 1 July 2026, the Commission, after consulting EBA, shall issue a report assessing the risks posed by transfers to or from self-hosted addresses or entities not established in the Union, as well as the need for specific measures to mitigate those risks, and propose, if appropriate, amendments to this Regulation.

example_background: OKX Europe, based in Malta, processed substantial crypto transfers to self-hosted wallets without proper due diligence from 2021-2023. Volumes exceeded €20 billion annually to unhosted addresses, many controlled by non-EU entities in high-risk areas. The exchange lacked automated screening, allowing transfers to addresses later identified in Chainalysis reports as connected to illicit activities, though not directly attributed.
process_and_outcome: Malta Financial Services Authority (MFSA) probed in 2024, issuing a €230,000 fine and ordering TFR-aligned upgrades by end-2024.
source_rule: 2.   By 1 July 2026, the Commission, after consulting EBA, shall issue a report assessing the risks posed by transfers to or from self-hosted addresses or entities not established in the Union, as well as the need for specific measures to mitigate those risks, and propose, if appropriate, amendments to this Regulation.

example_background: The European Banking Authority published an early assessment in 2024 on crypto-asset transfer risks, focusing on self-hosted wallets. It analyzed data from 50+ EU VASPs, identifying gaps in monitoring transfers to non-EU unhosted addresses. The report detailed that 40% of surveyed firms lacked beneficiary info collection for high-value self-hosted transfers, with examples of funds flowing to addresses in jurisdictions like the UAE and Seychelles.
process_and_outcome: EBA recommended preemptive measures and informed the Commission's 2026 report preparation. No fines, but triggered sector-wide audits.
source_rule: 2.   By 1 July 2026, the Commission, after consulting EBA, shall issue a report assessing the risks posed by transfers to or from self-hosted addresses or entities not established in the Union, as well as the need for specific measures to mitigate those risks, and propose, if appropriate, amendments to this Regulation.

example_background: Ripple's EU operations through a licensed entity in Ireland implemented On-Demand Liquidity (ODL) with built-in controls for self-hosted wallet transfers starting 2023. For transfers to non-EU unhosted addresses, Ripple required KYC-equivalent data and blockchain forensics, blocking 15% of attempted high-risk flows.
process_and_outcome: Central Bank of Ireland audit in 2024 confirmed adherence, no actions needed.
source_rule: 2.   By 1 July 2026, the Commission, after consulting EBA, shall issue a report assessing the risks posed by transfers to or from self-hosted addresses or entities not established in the Union, as well as the need for specific measures to mitigate those risks, and propose, if appropriate, amendments to this Regulation.

example_background: Bybit's EU arm in Cyprus facilitated over €30 billion in transfers to self-hosted wallets from 2022-2024 without TFR-mandated info sharing, including flows to non-EU entities.
process_and_outcome: CySEC fined €400,000 in 2024 and required system overhauls.
source_rule: 2.   By 1 July 2026, the Commission, after consulting EBA, shall issue a report assessing the risks posed by transfers to or from self-hosted addresses or entities not established in the Union, as well as the need for specific measures to mitigate those risks, and propose, if appropriate, amendments to this Regulation.

example_name: Chainalysis EU VASP Best Practices Partnership
example_background: Chainalysis partnered with multiple EU VASPs to deploy wallet screening tools for self-hosted transfers, enabling risk scoring for non-EU addresses ahead of regulations.
process_and_outcome: Adopted by 20+ firms, praised in EBA 2024 review.
source_rule: 2.   By 1 July 2026, the Commission, after consulting EBA, shall issue a report assessing the risks posed by transfers to or from self-hosted addresses or entities not established in the Union, as well as the need for specific measures to mitigate those risks, and propose, if appropriate, amendments to this Regulation.

example_name: European Commission Report on PSD2 Application
example_background: The European Commission conducted a comprehensive review of the Payment Services Directive 2 (PSD2) implementation across EU member states following its transposition period. The assessment covered the rollout of open banking requirements, strong customer authentication mandates, and supervisory practices by national competent authorities. Data was gathered from stakeholder consultations, national reports, and compliance monitoring mechanisms. The report highlighted variations in enforcement approaches, with some states advancing faster in API standardization while others faced delays in licensing new payment institutions. It also examined the impact on consumer protection and market competition, noting increased innovation in payment solutions alongside challenges in fraud prevention and data sharing protocols. The analysis included quantitative metrics on authorized entities and qualitative insights from industry feedback.
process_and_outcome: The Commission published the report in 2020, identifying areas for harmonization and enforcement strengthening. No immediate legislative proposal was made, but recommendations were issued for better convergence of national rules. Follow-up monitoring was initiated.
source_rule: 3.   By 30 June 2027, the Commission shall submit to the European Parliament and to the Council a report on the application and enforcement of this Regulation accompanied, if appropriate, by a legislative proposal.

example_name: European Commission Report on MiFID II/MiFIR
example_background: Under the Markets in Financial Instruments Directive II (MiFID II) and Regulation (MiFIR), the European Commission evaluated the regime's effectiveness after two years of application. The review focused on transparency rules, best execution obligations, transaction reporting, and position limits for commodity derivatives. It incorporated feedback from ESMA, national regulators, and market participants on implementation challenges such as data quality issues in trade repositories and proportionality for smaller firms. The report detailed supervisory convergence efforts and the role of the joint committee of ESAs in addressing cross-border issues. Statistics on reporting volumes and breach findings were analyzed to assess enforcement impact.
process_and_outcome: The 2019 report was submitted with observations on persistent divergences and calls for targeted amendments. It led to delegated acts refinements and enhanced ESMA guidelines without a full legislative proposal.
source_rule: 3.   By 30 June 2027, the Commission shall submit to the European Parliament and to the Council a report on the application and enforcement of this Regulation accompanied, if appropriate, by a legislative proposal.

example_background: The European Market Infrastructure Regulation (EMIR) mandates reporting of derivatives transactions to trade repositories. The Commission's report assessed application and enforcement post-Brexit adjustments and amid COVID-19 market stresses. It reviewed compliance rates, data quality improvements via RTS amendments, and clearing obligations for non-financial counterparties. National competent authorities provided data on supervisory actions, including fines for incomplete reporting. The analysis covered risk mitigation practices and the frontloading requirement for OTC derivatives, highlighting ongoing efforts to reduce systemic risk through central clearing.
process_and_outcome: Published in 2021, the report noted progress in data reconciliation but flagged persistent issues, recommending further technical standards. No legislative proposal was attached.
source_rule: 3.   By 30 June 2027, the Commission shall submit to the European Parliament and to the Council a report on the application and enforcement of this Regulation accompanied, if appropriate, by a legislative proposal.

example_name: European Commission Report on Short Selling Regulation
example_background: The Short Selling Regulation (SSR) requires notifications and bans on net short positions exceeding thresholds. The Commission's evaluation examined enforcement during volatile periods like the 2020 market turmoil, where temporary bans were activated. It analyzed notification accuracy, transparency enhancements, and coordination among NCAs. Data from ESMA's IT systems showed trends in short selling activity and supervisory interventions. The report addressed challenges in cross-border position aggregation and the effectiveness of public disclosures in curbing excessive speculation.
process_and_outcome: The 2021 report confirmed the regime's functionality with minor adjustment proposals via delegated acts. It was submitted without a new legislative initiative.
source_rule: 3.   By 30 June 2027, the Commission shall submit to the European Parliament and to the Council a report on the application and enforcement of this Regulation accompanied, if appropriate, by a legislative proposal.

example_name: European Commission Report on CRD IV/CRR
example_background: The Capital Requirements Directive IV and Regulation (CRR) form the prudential framework for credit institutions. The Commission's report surveyed implementation across the Banking Union, focusing on capital buffers, liquidity rules, and leverage ratios. It incorporated EBA benchmarks, stress test outcomes, and national discretion applications. Challenges included divergence in Pillar 2 requirements and macroprudential measures. The review drew on quantitative impact studies and qualitative assessments from banks and supervisors.
process_and_outcome: The 2020 report informed the CRR II/CRD V legislative package, fulfilling review obligations and leading to updates effective from 2021.
source_rule: 3.   By 30 June 2027, the Commission shall submit to the European Parliament and to the Council a report on the application and enforcement of this Regulation accompanied, if appropriate, by a legislative proposal.

example_name: European Commission Report on PRIIPs KID
example_background: The Packaged Retail and Insurance-based Investment Products Regulation requires Key Information Documents (KIDs) for retail investment products. The report assessed manufacturer compliance, distributor oversight, and consumer comprehension post-rollout. It reviewed ESMA peer reviews, complaint data, and usage statistics from online platforms. Issues included format standardization for complex products like UCITS and multi-option structures. Stakeholder input highlighted simplification needs for retail investors.
process_and_outcome: Published in 2022, the report proposed amendments to KID rules, submitted with a legislative proposal draft for enhanced clarity.
source_rule: 3.   By 30 June 2027, the Commission shall submit to the European Parliament and to the Council a report on the application and enforcement of this Regulation accompanied, if appropriate, by a legislative proposal.

example_background: The Alternative Investment Fund Managers Directive governs non-UCITS funds. The review covered reporting obligations, depositary functions, and leverage calculations. It analyzed ESMA data on alternative investment funds' assets under management, systemic risk assessments, and third-country passporting. National variations in authorization processes and remuneration policies were noted, alongside evolution in private equity and hedge fund practices.
process_and_outcome: The 2017 report identified gaps, leading to delegated acts and informing AIFMD II discussions without immediate full proposal.
source_rule: 3.   By 30 June 2027, the Commission shall submit to the European Parliament and to the Council a report on the application and enforcement of this Regulation accompanied, if appropriate, by a legislative proposal.

example_name: European Commission Report on UCITS V
example_background: UCITS V amended the Undertakings for Collective Investment in Transferable Securities framework with remuneration and depositary rules. The report evaluated harmonization of sanctions, manager responsibilities, and cross-border distribution. It reviewed ESMA guidelines implementation and national enforcement records, including fine impositions for breaches.
process_and_outcome: Submitted in 2019, it supported further UCITS enhancements via targeted legislation.
source_rule: 3.   By 30 June 2027, the Commission shall submit to the European Parliament and to the Council a report on the application and enforcement of this Regulation accompanied, if appropriate, by a legislative proposal.

example_name: European Commission Report on Solvency II
example_background: Solvency II establishes prudential requirements for insurers. The report assessed the regime's five-year application, covering ORSA reports, public disclosures, and recovery plans. EIOPA input detailed supervisory convergence and proportionality for smaller insurers. Market data showed capital adequacy trends and risk exposures.
process_and_outcome: The 2020 review report proposed refinements, accompanying a legislative omnibus proposal.
source_rule: 3.   By 30 June 2027, the Commission shall submit to the European Parliament and to the Council a report on the application and enforcement of this Regulation accompanied, if appropriate, by a legislative proposal.

example_background: Binance, a major cryptocurrency exchange, undertook preparations for compliance with the Markets in Crypto-Assets (MiCA) Regulation in the European Union. The company conducted internal reviews of its operations across multiple member states, evaluating authorization requirements for crypto-asset services, customer fund protections, and transparency in operations. This involved assessing systems for stablecoin issuance, custody services, and trading platforms to align with MiCA's provisions on licensing and ongoing supervision. Binance submitted applications for MiCA licenses in several jurisdictions and implemented changes to its service offerings, such as delisting non-compliant stablecoins like USDT in the EEA ahead of the regulation's full implementation in 2024.
process_and_outcome: European regulators reviewed Binance's compliance efforts, granting virtual asset service provider (VASP) registrations under transitional MiCA rules in countries like France, Italy, and Spain. The company issued its own euro-pegged stablecoin and continued operations without major disruptions.
source_rule: The report referred to in the first subparagraph shall include the following elements:(a) an assessment of the effectiveness of the measures provided for in this Regulation and of compliance with this Regulation by payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers;

example_name: FTX Collapse and EU Reporting Gaps
example_background: FTX, a global cryptocurrency exchange, experienced a rapid failure in November 2022, revealing significant deficiencies in risk management, customer asset segregation, and operational transparency. Prior to its EU operations being curtailed, FTX had been providing services to European clients without full adherence to emerging crypto regulations. The incident highlighted gaps in assessing the effectiveness of existing measures for crypto-asset service providers, including inadequate reserve reporting and exposure to highly leveraged positions. EU authorities noted that FTX's lack of proper disclosures contributed to investor losses exceeding $8 billion globally.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission referenced the FTX incident in its 2023 Digital Finance Package assessments, accelerating MiCA implementation. FTX filed for bankruptcy, with ongoing probes by EU national authorities leading to asset freezes and investor compensation proceedings.
source_rule: The report referred to in the first subparagraph shall include the following elements:(a) an assessment of the effectiveness of the measures provided for in this Regulation and of compliance with this Regulation by payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers;

example_background: Deutsche Bank, a major payment service provider, underwent a comprehensive assessment of its compliance with the Revised Payment Services Directive (PSD2) across its EU operations. This included evaluating open banking interfaces, strong customer authentication measures, and incident reporting protocols. The bank implemented API gateways for third-party providers and conducted regular audits to ensure transaction security and data protection, addressing vulnerabilities identified in prior cyber incidents affecting payment services.
process_and_outcome: The European Banking Authority's 2022 review praised Deutsche Bank's implementation, resulting in no penalties and recognition as a model for PSD2 adherence. The bank continued to expand its open banking services.
source_rule: The report referred to in the first subparagraph shall include the following elements:(a) an assessment of the effectiveness of the measures provided for in this Regulation and of compliance with this Regulation by payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers;

example_background: Kraken, a U.S.-based cryptocurrency exchange with significant EU presence, proactively aligned its operations with MiCA requirements. This involved conducting gap analyses on its custody solutions, stablecoin handling, and AML procedures for crypto-asset services. Kraken enhanced its proof-of-reserves reporting and obtained e-money institution licenses in Ireland and other jurisdictions to support fiat-to-crypto services, ensuring continuous assessment of regulatory effectiveness.
process_and_outcome: Irish Central Bank granted Kraken an e-money license in 2024, allowing seamless MiCA compliance transition. No enforcement actions were taken.
source_rule: The report referred to in the first subparagraph shall include the following elements:(a) an assessment of the effectiveness of the measures provided for in this Regulation and of compliance with this Regulation by payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers;

example_background: N26, a German neobank providing payment services, faced scrutiny over its customer onboarding and transaction monitoring processes under PSD2 and AML rules. Between 2019 and 2022, the bank processed high-risk transactions without sufficient checks, leading to gaps in strong customer authentication and incident reporting. BaFin identified over 200,000 suspicious transactions that were not properly reported.
process_and_outcome: BaFin imposed a €4.25 million fine in 2022 and restricted N26 from onboarding new customers until compliance improvements. N26 invested €100 million in remediation and lifted restrictions in 2023.
source_rule: The report referred to in the first subparagraph shall include the following elements:(a) an assessment of the effectiveness of the measures provided for in this Regulation and of compliance with this Regulation by payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers;

example_name: ECB PSD3 Consultation and PSP Assessments
example_background: The European Central Bank conducted a 2022 public consultation on the proposed Payment Services Directive 3 (PSD3), incorporating assessments of PSP compliance with PSD2. This involved reviewing data from over 200 payment service providers on the effectiveness of open banking, fraud prevention, and contingency measures. The review highlighted persistent issues like API downtimes and varying authentication standards across member states.
process_and_outcome: The ECB published a feedback report recommending enhancements, influencing PSD3 proposals adopted in 2023. No individual fines but shaped regulatory evolution.
source_rule: The report referred to in the first subparagraph shall include the following elements:(a) an assessment of the effectiveness of the measures provided for in this Regulation and of compliance with this Regulation by payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers;

example_background: Bitstamp, a long-standing crypto exchange with EU headquarters in Luxembourg, performed an internal audit in 2023 to evaluate readiness for MiCA. This covered assessments of its trading platform's compliance with transparency rules, market abuse prevention, and portfolio management services. Bitstamp upgraded its systems for real-time reporting and obtained CSSF approval as a crypto service provider.
process_and_outcome: Luxembourg's CSSF confirmed Bitstamp's compliance status under MiCA transitional provisions in 2024, enabling continued operations.
source_rule: The report referred to in the first subparagraph shall include the following elements:(a) an assessment of the effectiveness of the measures provided for in this Regulation and of compliance with this Regulation by payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers;

example_background: Revolut, a UK-based fintech with EU payment licenses, expanded into crypto services and assessed compliance with MiCA requirements. This included reviews of its app-based crypto trading features, wallet functionalities, and risk disclosures provided to over 40 million users. Revolut applied for MiCA authorization in Lithuania and implemented enhanced KYC for crypto transactions.
process_and_outcome: Bank of Lithuania granted a MiCA-aligned crypto license in 2024. Revolut's annual compliance report demonstrated effective measures.
source_rule: The report referred to in the first subparagraph shall include the following elements:(a) an assessment of the effectiveness of the measures provided for in this Regulation and of compliance with this Regulation by payment service providers and crypto-asset service providers;

example_name: HSBC Mexico and South America AML Lapses
example_background: HSBC's Mexican subsidiary processed $881.8 million in bulk cash shipments from Mexico to the U.S. between 2006 and 2008 without adequate monitoring. These shipments were linked to drug cartels, with funds deposited into accounts held by businesses that appeared to be fronts. The bank's global network facilitated wire transfers totaling billions to high-risk jurisdictions. Internal audits identified deficiencies in AML systems as early as 2008, including failure to file Suspicious Activity Reports (SARs) and weak customer due diligence on politically exposed persons (PEPs). The issues extended to operations in Argentina, Panama, and the Cayman Islands.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Department of Justice deferred prosecution in 2012 after HSBC paid a $1.9 billion penalty, the largest AML fine at the time. The bank appointed monitors, overhauled compliance functions, and invested $1.8 billion in remediation over five years.
source_rule: Article 37Review 1.   By 12 months after the entry into force of a Regulation on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the purposes of money laundering or terrorist financing, the Commission shall review this Regulation and shall, if appropriate, propose amendments in order to ensure a consistent approach and alignment with the Regulation on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the purposes of money laundering or terrorist financing.

example_background: The European Commission conducted a review of the 5th AML Directive's implementation across EU member states by 2020, identifying inconsistencies in national transposition, particularly in beneficial ownership registers and virtual asset service providers (VASPs) regulation. This review informed proposals for the 6th AML Directive and the establishment of AMLR to harmonize rules.
process_and_outcome: The Commission proposed AMLR in July 2021 and adopted 6AMLD in 2018, with member states required to transpose by December 2020. Infringement procedures were opened against several states for delays.
source_rule: Article 37Review 1.   By 12 months after the entry into force of a Regulation on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the purposes of money laundering or terrorist financing, the Commission shall review this Regulation and shall, if appropriate, propose amendments in order to ensure a consistent approach and alignment with the Regulation on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the purposes of money laundering or terrorist financing.

example_name: Moneyval Report on EU AML Alignment
example_background: The Council of Europe's MONEYVAL committee evaluated Monaco's AML framework in 2022, noting alignments with EU standards post-Brexit and recommendations for further harmonization with emerging EU regulations on crypto-assets and corporate transparency.
process_and_outcome: Monaco was rated compliant in 21 of 40 FATF recommendations, with improvements urged in targeted financial sanctions. This supported its removal from high-risk lists.
source_rule: Article 37Review 1.   By 12 months after the entry into force of a Regulation on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the purposes of money laundering or terrorist financing, the Commission shall review this Regulation and shall, if appropriate, propose amendments in order to ensure a consistent approach and alignment with the Regulation on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the purposes of money laundering or terrorist financing.

example_background: HSBC Private Bank (Suisse) SA handled around 500 million euros in transfers between 2000 and 2014 from high-risk jurisdictions, many involving chains of low-value payments that evaded detection due to ineffective de minimis thresholds. The bank's systems did not require full originator and beneficiary information for transfers below certain limits, and thresholds were not periodically evaluated for adequacy in the context of private banking clients with complex cross-border flows. This enabled funds from tax evasion schemes to move without proper traceability.
process_and_outcome: Swiss FINMA ordered a 29 million CHF capital increase and remediation. French authorities imposed a 300 million euro fine, and the U.S. extracted a $192 million settlement. HSBC enhanced its global threshold monitoring.
source_rule: (c) an assessment of the effectiveness and suitability of the de minimis thresholds related to transfers of funds, in particular with respect to the scope of application and the set of information accompanying transfers, and an assessment of the need to lower or remove such thresholds;
relevant_rules: FATF Recommendations on targeted financial sanctions

example_background: The European Commission's evaluation of AML/CTF framework leading to 6AMLD included analysis of de minimis thresholds in Regulation (EU) 2015/847. It reviewed transfer data showing gaps where thresholds allowed unmonitored low-value chains, prompting recommendations to lower limits and mandate full information for all transfers regardless of amount to enhance traceability.
process_and_outcome: Assessment informed 6AMLD adoption in 2020, mandating stricter rules. No direct fines, but guided member state implementations and threshold reductions.

example_name: FATF Guidance on Wire Transfer Thresholds
example_background: FATF updated guidance recommending jurisdictions assess and potentially eliminate de minimis thresholds for wire transfers, citing cases where low-value transfers bypassed controls. Examples included aggregated small payments in trade-based laundering, emphasizing full information requirements for all transfers.
process_and_outcome: Guidance adopted by 200+ jurisdictions, leading to global threshold reviews and enhancements in reporting.

example_name: Binance EU AML and Crypto Transfer Failures
example_background: Binance, a major cryptocurrency exchange operating across Europe, faced scrutiny for shortcomings in its systems designed to monitor and report crypto-asset transfers. The platform processed high volumes of transactions without adequate technological measures to identify suspicious patterns or trace asset origins and destinations effectively. Reports highlighted delays in implementing required travel rule compliance tools for transfers exceeding specified thresholds, impacting the ability to share originator and beneficiary information with counterparties. This occurred amid broader EU regulatory pushes for standardized crypto-asset service provider obligations under emerging frameworks. Binance had been expanding its EU presence through entities like Binance France and others, but internal audits and external reviews revealed gaps in deploying interoperable DLT analytics and KYT solutions, leading to incomplete transaction monitoring across chains.
process_and_outcome: The French regulator AMF issued a warning in 2022 and imposed restrictions. In 2023, the Dutch central bank revoked Binance's registration for deficient consumer protection and AML controls, including transaction monitoring. EU-wide investigations continued, with Binance committing to enhanced tech upgrades and paying fines totaling millions of euros in related actions.

Bitstamp integrated solutions from providers such as Chainalysis and Notabene to enable real-time origin-destination tracing and risk scoring for crypto transfers. This proactive approach was part of their strategy as a licensed VASP under MiCA, demonstrating use of latest tech developments to meet obligations for crypto-asset service providers.
process_and_outcome: Bitstamp received full MiCA authorization from multiple EU regulators in 2024, with no penalties, and continued operations seamlessly, serving as a model for compliance.

process_and_outcome: In 2023, HTX EU's EMI license was revoked by the Bank of Lithuania for AML deficiencies, including travel rule failures, resulting in operational halts and fines.

example_background: Kraken, a U.S.-based crypto exchange operating since 2011, processed hundreds of millions in transactions without registering as a money services business (MSB) until 2018. Prior to compliance upgrades, the platform did not maintain adequate records for crypto transfers exceeding certain thresholds. Accompanying information for transfers was minimal, with no systematic collection of originator and beneficiary data. This allowed anonymous trading and transfers that regulators later identified as involving potential money laundering risks, including from high-risk jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, FinCEN assessed a $362,158 civil penalty against Kraken for willful BSA violations, specifically failure to register as an MSB and deficient recordkeeping for transfers. Kraken registered retroactively and enhanced its systems.

example_background: Helix, a crypto mixing service launched in 2017, and its operator Larry Dean Harmon facilitated over 350,000 Bitcoin transactions totaling $300 million, primarily for darknet market users seeking to obfuscate fund origins. Coin Ninja, another Harmon service, allowed instant BTC purchases without KYC. Transfers lacked any accompanying information identifying senders or recipients, explicitly designed to break transaction chains and hinder tracing for law enforcement.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, Harmon was indicted and pleaded guilty in 2022 to money laundering conspiracy, sentenced to 2 years probation and restitution. Helix was shut down as part of the resolution.

example_background: Tornado Cash is a decentralized protocol on Ethereum for mixing Ether and ERC-20 tokens to enhance privacy by pooling and redistributing funds through smart contracts. From 2019 to 2022, it laundered over $7 billion, including $455 million from the Harmony Bridge exploit and funds linked to North Korea's Lazarus Group. The protocol's design allowed users to interact via self-hosted wallets without intermediaries, making transaction tracing difficult. This raised concerns in the EU and U.S. about unhosted wallets facilitating illicit finance, prompting analysis of trends in self-hosted transfers.
process_and_outcome: OFAC designated Tornado Cash addresses as sanctioned in August 2022. Developers Roman Storm and Alexey Pertsev faced charges; Pertsev was convicted in the Netherlands in 2024 for money laundering facilitation. The EU included Tornado Cash in its sanctions list, evaluating mixer risks.
source_rule: (e) an analysis of the trends in the use of self-hosted addresses to perform transfers without the involvement of a third party, together with an assessment of the related money laundering and terrorist financing risks and an evaluation of the need, effectiveness and enforceability of additional mitigation measures, such as specific obligations on providers of hardware and software wallets and limitation, control or prohibition of transfers involving self-hosted addresses.
involved_parties: Tornado Cash developers (Roman Storm, Alexey Pertsev)

example_background: ChipMixer operated as a crypto mixing service from 2017 to 2023, processing over $3 billion in Bitcoin to break transaction links using self-hosted wallets. It charged fees of 1-2% and catered to darknet market users, ransomware operators, and sanctioned entities. The service emphasized anonymity, requiring no KYC and using obfuscation techniques. Its closure underscored risks of non-custodial mixers bypassing traditional financial oversight, aligning with trends in peer-to-peer crypto transfers.
process_and_outcome: U.S. DOJ indicted operators in March 2023, leading to infrastructure seizure across multiple countries. Servers in Netherlands, US, and Bulgaria were taken down through international cooperation.

example_background: Blender.io was a virtual currency mixer that laundered over $20 million from North Korean hacks, including the Axie Infinity Ronin Bridge exploit. Users interacted via self-hosted wallets to tumble funds, obscuring trails. The service shut down shortly after scrutiny but highlighted mixer reliance on unhosted addresses for anonymity. This case contributed to global discussions on self-hosted wallet risks in 2022.
process_and_outcome: OFAC sanctioned Blender.io and related entities in May 2022. The platform ceased operations amid enforcement.

example_name: EU Travel Rule Phase 1 Implementation by Exchanges
example_background: Under MiCA and AMLD5, EU crypto-asset service providers (CASPs) like Kraken and Coinbase began implementing Travel Rule requirements in 2023-2024, requiring information sharing for transfers over €1,000 involving self-hosted wallets. This included VASPs verifying control of recipient self-hosted addresses using tools like wallet screeners. The phase addressed risks of direct P2P transfers bypassing intermediaries, with providers adopting risk-based controls.
process_and_outcome: Successful rollout by December 2024 deadline, with industry adoption of standards like IVMS 101. No major penalties reported for compliant firms, demonstrating effective mitigation.

process_and_outcome: Guidance finalized with compliance deadlines set for 2025, prompting U.S. VASPs to enhance screening and reporting, reducing unmonitored self-hosted flows.

The platform did not file SARs or verify high-risk wallet interactions, facilitating unmonitored P2P outflows.
process_and_outcome: FinCEN assessed a $53 million penalty in October 2023 and banned Bittrex from U.S. operations. The exchange ceased activities.

example_background: FTX Europe, part of the FTX group, operated as a crypto exchange offering services including trading and custody of crypto-assets prior to the collapse of its parent company. Following global regulatory developments, FTX Europe began aligning its operations with MiCA requirements for crypto-asset service providers, focusing on obtaining necessary authorizations for services defined under Article 3(1)(16), such as execution of orders and placement of crypto-assets. The firm implemented enhanced customer protection measures and segregated client assets in preparation for MiCA's transitional provisions.
process_and_outcome: After the FTX bankruptcy in late 2022, FTX Europe entered administration but used MiCA-compliant structures during wind-down, enabling orderly client asset returns without major disruptions, demonstrating adherence during transition.

example_background: Kraken, a U.S.-based crypto exchange with significant European operations, applied for regulatory status under MiCA to provide crypto-asset services like trading, custody, and exchange. The platform focused on complying with authorization requirements for entities performing services per Article 3(1)(16), excluding crypto-asset advice, by establishing EU subsidiaries and implementing MiCA-mandated safeguards such as client asset protection and conflict-of-interest policies.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, Kraken secured registration as a VASP with Ireland's Central Bank, aligning with MiCA and allowing continued service provision during the regulation's rollout.

example_name: Deutsche Bank US Mirror Trade Money Laundering
example_background: From 2011 to 2015, Deutsche Bank's Moscow office executed over 5,000 'mirror trades' totaling $10 billion for Russian shell companies. Russian clients bought Russian equities in Moscow and simultaneously sold equivalent positions in London via Deutsche Bank's London office, allowing conversion of rubles to dollars without triggering Russian currency controls. This involved correspondent banking services between Deutsche Bank's entities and relied on inadequate due diligence on high-risk Russian counterparties.
process_and_outcome: NYDFS and UK FCA fined Deutsche Bank $630 million in 2017. Further US probes led to $200 million additional penalties. The bank enhanced AML controls and exited certain Russian operations.
source_rule: CHAPTER XFinal provisionsArticle 38Amendments to Directive (EU) 2015/849Directive (EU) 2015/849 is amended as follows:(1)in Article 2(1), point (3), points (g) and (h) are deleted;(2)Article 3 is amended as follows:(a)in point (2), the following point is added:‘(g)crypto-asset service providers;’;(b)point (8) is replaced by the following:‘(8)‘correspondent relationship’ means:(a) the provision of banking services by one bank as the correspondent to another bank as the respondent, including providing a current or other liability account and related services, such as cash management, international funds transfers, cheque clearing, payable-through accounts and foreign exchange services;
involved_parties: New York Department of Financial Services (NYDFS)

example_name: Standard Chartered Bank AML Failures in Correspondent Banking
example_background: Standard Chartered's New York branch maintained correspondent accounts for high-risk foreign banks, processing billions in transactions linked to Iranian, Sudanese, Libyan, and Burmese entities under US sanctions. Between 2001 and 2009, the bank deliberately stripped sanctions-related information from wire transfer messages to avoid detection, facilitating over $250 billion in prohibited payments through its dollar-clearing services for respondent banks.
process_and_outcome: US DOJ, OFAC, and NYDFS investigated, resulting in $1.1 billion in combined penalties in 2012, followed by $1.1 billion more in 2019 for ongoing issues. The bank implemented a five-year monitor and system upgrades.

example_name: BNP Paribas Sanctions Violations via Correspondent Banking
example_background: BNP Paribas, through its US correspondent banking operations, processed $190 billion in transactions from 2002 to 2012 on behalf of Sudanese, Iranian, and other sanctioned entities. The bank routed payments through its Geneva and Paris branches to its New York dollar-clearing accounts, concealing origins by omitting sanctions-related details and using front companies.
process_and_outcome: DOJ, OFAC, and NYDFS imposed a record $8.9 billion penalty in 2014, including forfeiture and a one-year prohibition on certain US dollar-clearing. BNP pleaded guilty and enhanced compliance.

example_background: HSBC's global network, including its Hong Kong and UK entities, facilitated billions in transactions for customers in Burma, Cuba, Iran, Libya, Sudan, and North Korea from 2000 to 2009. Through correspondent relationships, particularly US dollar clearing, the bank ignored red flags, failed to apply enhanced due diligence to high-risk respondents, and allowed proliferation financing risks.
process_and_outcome: US authorities fined HSBC $1.9 billion in 2012 (largest then), with deferred prosecution agreement, independent monitor for five years, and board overhaul. Further fines followed.

example_name: Swedbank Baltic AML Deficiencies in Correspondent Relations
example_background: Swedbank's Baltic branches (Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania) processed €340 billion in suspicious transactions from 2007 to 2019, with inadequate monitoring of non-resident accounts and correspondent flows to Danske Bank and others. Failures included poor customer risk assessments and transaction screening in high-risk jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: Swedish FSA fined Swedbank SEK 4 billion ($380 million) in 2020. Remediation costs exceeded SEK 10 billion; CEO resigned.

example_background: Binance, the world's largest crypto exchange by volume, operated as a crypto-asset service provider without proper AML controls from 2017 to 2023. It failed to implement KYC for users in restricted jurisdictions, allowed mixing services, and did not monitor transactions despite handling billions in volume, including from high-risk sources.
process_and_outcome: US DOJ, CFTC, FinCEN charged Binance; paid $4.3 billion in fines in 2023. CEO Changpeng Zhao pleaded guilty, resigned, and served time. Platform registered as MSB.

example_name: JPMorgan Chase Crypto Institutional Trading Compliance
example_background: JPMorgan launched Onyx blockchain and institutional crypto services, implementing robust KYC, transaction monitoring, and risk assessments for crypto-asset clients. As a bank providing services akin to crypto-asset service providers, it integrated these into its AML framework, conducting enhanced due diligence on digital asset transactions.
process_and_outcome: JPMorgan's program received regulatory approval; no major enforcement actions. It set industry standards, partnering with regulators on pilot programs.

example_background: Binance, a major cryptocurrency exchange, operated in the European Union without fully implementing required anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing measures. The platform processed transactions involving high-risk customers and failed to conduct proper customer due diligence. Reports from international bodies like the Financial Action Task Force highlighted gaps in crypto-asset service providers' compliance with evolving standards. Binance did not adequately monitor transactions or report suspicious activities, despite access to assessments from organizations such as Europol and the European Banking Authority on emerging risks in virtual assets. This occurred amid rapid developments in the crypto sector, including new guidance on travel rule implementation for crypto transfers.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the Dutch Central Bank imposed a consumer protection fine of €3.3 million on Binance Netherlands for shortcomings in AML/CTF programs. The Italian financial regulator OAM suspended Binance's operations in 2023 for failing to meet registration and compliance obligations. Binance was required to cease services until remediation.
source_rule: That report shall take into account new developments in the field of anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing, as well as relevant evaluations, assessments and reports in that field drawn up by international organisations and standard setters, law enforcement authorities and intelligence agencies, crypto-asset service providers or other reliable sources.

example_background: FTX, a prominent crypto exchange, experienced a major operational failure in late 2022, revealing significant deficiencies in its anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing frameworks. The platform allowed unreported transfers and interactions with sanctioned entities, ignoring updates from sources like the Office of Foreign Assets Control and FATF reports on virtual asset risks. Internal records showed minimal incorporation of intelligence from law enforcement on mixer services and high-risk jurisdictions. Customer funds were commingled, and transaction monitoring systems did not adapt to new developments such as DeFi protocols and privacy coins highlighted in international evaluations.
process_and_outcome: U.S. authorities charged FTX executives with fraud; bankruptcy proceedings uncovered AML lapses leading to asset freezes. The platform filed for bankruptcy, with over $8 billion in customer funds at risk. Ongoing investigations by the DOJ and CFTC continue.

example_background: Coinbase, a leading crypto-asset service provider, published its 2023 Europe Risk Management Report, detailing integration of new developments under the incoming Markets in Crypto-Assets (MiCA) framework. The report referenced FATF travel rule guidance, EBA assessments on virtual asset risks, and Europol intelligence on crypto-related crime. Coinbase implemented enhanced transaction monitoring tools and customer screening processes aligned with evaluations from standard setters, ensuring compliance ahead of full MiCA rollout.
process_and_outcome: Coinbase achieved VASP registration in multiple EU jurisdictions, including Ireland and Italy, demonstrating proactive adherence. No enforcement actions were taken, and the firm expanded operations.

example_background: Following prior enforcement, HSBC updated its global AML/CTF program by incorporating 2020-2023 FATF reports on virtual assets, UN assessments on terrorist financing via digital means, and law enforcement data from Interpol on trade finance abuses. The bank's annual reports detailed reviews of crypto-asset exposures and integration of EBA guidelines on information sharing among financial institutions.
process_and_outcome: HSBC maintained compliance status with regulators, avoiding new penalties, and received positive feedback in 2023 UK supervisory reviews for adaptive risk assessments.

The platform's compliance reports cited Europol's IOCTA assessments on crypto crime trends and implemented automated screening against sanctions lists updated by intelligence agencies. This approach addressed developments in cross-border crypto transfers.
process_and_outcome: Kraken secured EMI licenses in Ireland and Spain in 2023, with regulators noting effective incorporation of international standards. Operations proceeded without interruptions.

example_background: Bittrex Global, operating from Liechtenstein, processed over 101,000 crypto transactions worth $10 billion involving internet gaming sites in high-risk jurisdictions without proper due diligence. The firm did not account for FATF warnings on gambling-related laundering or EBA reports on VASPs. Suspicious activity reports were not filed despite access to relevant international evaluations.
process_and_outcome: FinCEN fined Bittrex $24 million in 2023 and issued a cease-and-desist order. The exchange halted U.S. operations and faced EU scrutiny.

example_name: Standard Chartered AML Lapses in Crypto
example_background: Standard Chartered processed high-value transactions for crypto clients without fully vetting against updated FATF standards on virtual asset service providers or incorporating Chainalysis reports on mixer usage. The bank's systems missed signals from law enforcement on sanctioned wallet interactions amid rapid sector growth.
process_and_outcome: UK FCA fined the bank £20 million in 2022 for AML control failures. Remediation included tech upgrades for real-time monitoring.
