example_name: Deutsche Bank Danske Bank Correspondent AML Failures
example_background: Deutsche Bank acted as the U.S. correspondent bank for Danske Bank's Estonia branch from 2007 to 2015. During this period, the Estonia branch processed approximately €200 billion in suspicious transactions through Deutsche Bank's New York branch. These transactions involved non-resident customers, primarily from Russia, Latvia, and Estonia, using shell companies. Deutsche Bank conducted initial due diligence but failed to perform adequate ongoing monitoring or reassess risks as red flags emerged, such as unusual transaction patterns and high volumes from high-risk jurisdictions. Internal controls did not effectively identify or report suspicious activities, and senior management approvals for the relationship were not properly documented or refreshed amid escalating risks.
process_and_outcome: U.S. Department of Justice investigated alongside other regulators, leading to Deutsche Bank paying $20 million in forfeiture and disgorgement in 2021 for Bank Secrecy Act violations. The bank also faced $425 million in fines from New York State in 2017. Danske Bank was fined €470 million by Danish authorities and faced ongoing probes.
source_rule: (c)assess the respondent entity’s AML/CFT controls;(d)obtain approval from senior management before establishing new correspondent relationships;(e)document the respective responsibilities of each party to the correspondent relationship;(f) with respect to payable-through crypto-asset accounts, be satisfied that the respondent entity has verified the identity of, and performed ongoing due diligence on, the customers having direct access to accounts of the correspondent entity, and that it is able to provide relevant customer due diligence data to the correspondent entity, upon request.
involved_parties: New York Department of Financial Services
relevant_rules: Anti-Money Laundering regulations under 31 CFR Chapter X

example_name: HSBC Bangladesh Bank Cyber Heist Correspondent Lapses
example_background: In 2016, hackers stole $81 million from Bangladesh Bank's account at the Federal Reserve Bank of New York via the SWIFT network. The funds were routed through Rizal Commercial Banking Corporation (RCBC) in the Philippines, a correspondent of Bangladesh Bank. RCBC opened accounts for fictitious entities using stolen identities without proper customer due diligence. Bangladesh Bank had not adequately assessed RCBC's AML/CFT controls prior to or during the relationship, nor obtained documentation of responsibilities. Ongoing monitoring failed to detect the anomalous transfers exceeding $1 billion attempted.
process_and_outcome: U.S. regulators fined RCBC affiliates and pursued criminal charges. Bangladesh Bank enhanced controls post-incident. No direct fine on Bangladesh Bank, but global scrutiny led to SWIFT governance reforms and industry-wide correspondent reviews.
source_rule: (c)assess the respondent entity’s AML/CFT controls;(d)obtain approval from senior management before establishing new correspondent relationships;(e)document the respective responsibilities of each party to the correspondent relationship;(f) with respect to payable-through crypto-asset accounts, be satisfied that the respondent entity has verified the identity of, and performed ongoing due diligence on, the customers having direct access to accounts of the correspondent entity, and that it is able to provide relevant customer due diligence data to the correspondent entity, upon request.
involved_parties: Federal Reserve Bank of New York
relevant_rules: EU AML Directive correspondent banking requirements

example_name: Westpac New Zealand AML Correspondent Deficiencies
example_background: Westpac New Zealand maintained numerous correspondent relationships but lacked systematic assessments of foreign banks' AML/CFT controls. From 2010-2018, the bank did not consistently obtain senior management approval for new relationships, failed to document shared responsibilities, and performed inadequate due diligence on respondents' customer verification processes. This exposed Westpac to risks from high-risk jurisdictions, including transactions linked to drug trafficking and sanctions evasion.
process_and_outcome: Reserve Bank of New Zealand and Department of Internal Affairs investigated, resulting in a NZ$6.7 million penalty in 2021 and a court-enforceable undertaking for remediation. Westpac invested over NZ$300 million in compliance upgrades.
source_rule: (c)assess the respondent entity’s AML/CFT controls;(d)obtain approval from senior management before establishing new correspondent relationships;(e)document the respective responsibilities of each party to the correspondent relationship;(f) with respect to payable-through crypto-asset accounts, be satisfied that the respondent entity has verified the identity of, and performed ongoing due diligence on, the customers having direct access to accounts of the correspondent entity, and that it is able to provide relevant customer due diligence data to the correspondent entity, upon request.
relevant_rules: Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2009 (New Zealand)

example_name: Standard Chartered UAE Correspondent AML Gaps
example_background: Standard Chartered Bank's Dubai branch handled $157 million in transactions for Iranian clients via U.S. correspondent accounts from 2008-2013. The bank did not sufficiently assess the AML/CFT controls of its respondent entities, neglected senior management approvals for high-risk relationships, and failed to document responsibilities. It also could not provide complete customer identification data to U.S. counterparts upon request.
process_and_outcome: Office of Foreign Assets Control and other U.S. regulators imposed $300 million in penalties in 2019 as part of a broader $1.1 billion sanctions settlement. The bank remediated its correspondent banking program.
source_rule: (c)assess the respondent entity’s AML/CFT controls;(d)obtain approval from senior management before establishing new correspondent relationships;(e)document the respective responsibilities of each party to the correspondent relationship;(f) with respect to payable-through crypto-asset accounts, be satisfied that the respondent entity has verified the identity of, and performed ongoing due diligence on, the customers having direct access to accounts of the correspondent entity, and that it is able to provide relevant customer due diligence data to the correspondent entity, upon request.
involved_parties: U.S. Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC)

example_background: Commerzbank AG processed over $1.45 billion in payments linked to sanctioned Iranian entities through its New York branch's correspondent accounts from 2009-2012. The bank failed to implement adequate due diligence on respondent institutions, did not secure senior approvals for risky relationships, and lacked documentation of party responsibilities. Controls did not ensure respondent verification of ultimate customers or data provision.
process_and_outcome: Manhattan District Attorney and OFAC fined the bank $1.45 billion in 2015. Commerzbank admitted liability and overhauled its compliance framework.
source_rule: (c)assess the respondent entity’s AML/CFT controls;(d)obtain approval from senior management before establishing new correspondent relationships;(e)document the respective responsibilities of each party to the correspondent relationship;(f) with respect to payable-through crypto-asset accounts, be satisfied that the respondent entity has verified the identity of, and performed ongoing due diligence on, the customers having direct access to accounts of the correspondent entity, and that it is able to provide relevant customer due diligence data to the correspondent entity, upon request.
involved_parties: U.S. Office of Foreign Assets Control

example_name: JPMorgan Chase Correspondent Banking Compliance Overhaul
example_background: Following multiple incidents, JPMorgan enhanced its correspondent banking program by implementing rigorous assessments of respondent entities' AML/CFT controls, requiring senior executive approvals for all new relationships, and maintaining detailed documentation of responsibilities. For payable-through accounts, the bank verifies respondents' customer due diligence processes and tests their ability to supply data on demand, including through annual audits and shared risk assessments.
process_and_outcome: Post-2015 enhancements led to no major correspondent-related fines; recognized in regulatory exams for strong controls. The program served as a model in industry reports.
source_rule: (c)assess the respondent entity’s AML/CFT controls;(d)obtain approval from senior management before establishing new correspondent relationships;(e)document the respective responsibilities of each party to the correspondent relationship;(f) with respect to payable-through crypto-asset accounts, be satisfied that the respondent entity has verified the identity of, and performed ongoing due diligence on, the customers having direct access to accounts of the correspondent entity, and that it is able to provide relevant customer due diligence data to the correspondent entity, upon request.
involved_parties: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC)
relevant_rules: Bank Secrecy Act/AML Manual of Examination Procedures

example_name: Credit Suisse Mirror Trade Sanctions Evasion
example_background: From 2014, Credit Suisse Moscow facilitated $2.5 billion in 'mirror trades' routing Russian rubles for FX trades through its London branch and U.S. correspondents, benefiting sanctioned entities. The bank did not assess respondent controls adequately, skipped senior approvals, and failed to document duties or ensure customer data sharing for direct account access.
process_and_outcome: U.S. and UK regulators fined Credit Suisse $265 million in 2017. The bank exited Russian operations and improved global AML systems.
source_rule: (c)assess the respondent entity’s AML/CFT controls;(d)obtain approval from senior management before establishing new correspondent relationships;(e)document the respective responsibilities of each party to the correspondent relationship;(f) with respect to payable-through crypto-asset accounts, be satisfied that the respondent entity has verified the identity of, and performed ongoing due diligence on, the customers having direct access to accounts of the correspondent entity, and that it is able to provide relevant customer due diligence data to the correspondent entity, upon request.
involved_parties: New York Department of Financial Services

example_name: ING Bank Netherlands AML Correspondent Shortcomings
example_background: ING Netherlands processed €1.8 billion in unusual transactions from 2010-2016, including through correspondent channels with inadequate respondent assessments. The bank neglected ongoing due diligence, senior management sign-offs for new ties, responsibility documentation, and verification of respondents' customer ID processes for payable-through accounts.
process_and_outcome: Dutch Prosecutors fined ING €775 million in 2018, the largest ever in the Netherlands. ING committed to a multi-year remediation plan costing hundreds of millions.
source_rule: (c)assess the respondent entity’s AML/CFT controls;(d)obtain approval from senior management before establishing new correspondent relationships;(e)document the respective responsibilities of each party to the correspondent relationship;(f) with respect to payable-through crypto-asset accounts, be satisfied that the respondent entity has verified the identity of, and performed ongoing due diligence on, the customers having direct access to accounts of the correspondent entity, and that it is able to provide relevant customer due diligence data to the correspondent entity, upon request.
relevant_rules: Dutch Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing (Prevention) Act

example_name: Deutsche Bank EU Money Laundering Mirror Trades
example_background: Deutsche Bank facilitated a series of foreign exchange trades known as mirror trades between 2011 and 2015 involving entities in Moscow and London. These transactions, totaling approximately 6 billion euros, were executed through Deutsche Bank's offices. The bank conducted initial due diligence on the counterparties but did not perform ongoing monitoring or gather comprehensive information about the nature of the businesses involved, particularly the reputation and supervisory quality of the Russian entities. Publicly available information indicated concerns about the Russian financial sector's oversight during that period, yet the bank proceeded without sufficient verification. This occurred within the European regulatory framework where financial institutions are required to assess licensing, business nature, reputation, and supervision quality before engaging in significant transactions.
process_and_outcome: The European Central Bank conducted an investigation starting in 2017, leading to a settlement in 2021. Deutsche Bank was fined 12.6 million euros by the ECB and an additional amount by Dutch authorities. The bank implemented enhanced due diligence procedures and remediation measures as part of the settlement.
source_rule: (a)determine if the respondent entity is licensed or registered;(b) gather sufficient information about the respondent entity to understand fully the nature of the respondent’s business and to determine from publicly available information the reputation of the entity and the quality of supervision;
relevant_rules: ECB Supervisory Review and Evaluation Process (SREP)

example_background: Swedbank's operations in Sweden, Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania from 2007 to 2019 involved handling transactions worth around 345 billion Swedish kronor linked to high-risk non-resident customers from Russia and former Soviet states. The bank's customer due diligence processes did not adequately verify the licensing status of these clients, fully comprehend the nature of their businesses, or assess reputation and supervisory quality using publicly available sources. Internal reviews later revealed that transaction monitoring systems failed to flag suspicious patterns despite available public reports on sanctions risks and weak oversight in certain jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: Swedish Financial Supervisory Authority (FSA) investigated from 2018, imposing a fine of 4 billion Swedish kronor in 2020. Estonian authorities added 30 million euros. Swedbank undertook extensive remediation, including system upgrades and customer file reviews.
source_rule: (a)determine if the respondent entity is licensed or registered;(b) gather sufficient information about the respondent entity to understand fully the nature of the respondent’s business and to determine from publicly available information the reputation of the entity and the quality of supervision;

example_name: Danske Bank Estonia Branch AML Failures
example_background: Between 2007 and 2015, Danske Bank's Estonian branch processed approximately 200 billion euros in suspicious transactions through shell companies, primarily from Russian and Eastern European sources. The branch did not properly determine if these respondent entities were licensed or registered in their jurisdictions, failed to gather detailed information on their business activities, and overlooked publicly available data on their reputation and the quality of supervision in high-risk areas. Parent bank oversight was limited, with inadequate aggregation of customer risk profiles.
process_and_outcome: Danish FSA investigated from 2017, leading to a 4 billion Danish kroner fine in 2021 after criminal proceedings. US authorities imposed a 2 billion USD penalty. The bank exited the non-resident portfolio and enhanced compliance programs.
source_rule: (a)determine if the respondent entity is licensed or registered;(b) gather sufficient information about the respondent entity to understand fully the nature of the respondent’s business and to determine from publicly available information the reputation of the entity and the quality of supervision;

example_name: UniCredit Bank Austria Russian Clients Case
example_background: UniCredit Bank Austria handled payments for Russian customers from 2013 to 2020, including those under sanctions lists, without fully checking licensing status, business nature, or public reputation and supervision quality. Public sources showed risks in the Russian sanctions environment, but due diligence was insufficient.
process_and_outcome: Austrian Financial Market Authority (FMA) investigated, imposing a 300 million euro fine in 2021. UniCredit enhanced its sanctions screening and customer onboarding processes.
source_rule: (a)determine if the respondent entity is licensed or registered;(b) gather sufficient information about the respondent entity to understand fully the nature of the respondent’s business and to determine from publicly available information the reputation of the entity and the quality of supervision;
relevant_rules: Austrian Financial Markets Anti-Money Laundering Act (FM-GwG)

example_name: Standard Chartered Paris Branch AML Issues
example_background: Standard Chartered's Paris branch from 2008 to 2014 onboarded high-risk clients without proper licensing verification, business understanding, or checks on public reputation and supervision quality, facilitating transactions linked to sanctioned entities.
process_and_outcome: French regulator ACPR fined 20 million euros in 2023 following investigation. The bank improved its compliance framework globally.
source_rule: (a)determine if the respondent entity is licensed or registered;(b) gather sufficient information about the respondent entity to understand fully the nature of the respondent’s business and to determine from publicly available information the reputation of the entity and the quality of supervision;
involved_parties: Autorité de Contrôle Prudentiel et de Résolution (ACPR)

example_background: Commerzbank AG's Moscow subsidiary between 2012 and 2019 did not adequately assess respondent entities' licensing, business nature, or public reputation and supervision quality, processing payments for high-risk Russian clients amid known sector risks.
process_and_outcome: BaFin and ECB investigated, leading to a 1.45 million euro fine in 2021. Commerzbank sold the subsidiary and bolstered group-wide controls.
source_rule: (a)determine if the respondent entity is licensed or registered;(b) gather sufficient information about the respondent entity to understand fully the nature of the respondent’s business and to determine from publicly available information the reputation of the entity and the quality of supervision;

example_name: Binance EU AML Policy Termination Documentation
example_background: In 2023, Binance, a major cryptocurrency exchange operating in the European Union, conducted a review of its correspondent banking relationships as part of its ongoing anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing program. Following assessments of risk profiles, transaction patterns, and compliance with enhanced due diligence requirements, the company decided to terminate relationships with several banking partners that exhibited elevated risks related to crypto-asset transfers. The decision-making process involved internal risk committees evaluating factors such as geographic exposure, customer onboarding practices, and historical suspicious activity reports. Binance maintained detailed records of these evaluations, including risk scoring models, meeting minutes from decision panels, and correspondence with the affected institutions, ensuring traceability and auditability of each termination action. This approach was implemented across multiple jurisdictions within the EU to align with harmonized regulatory expectations for crypto-asset service providers.
process_and_outcome: The documented terminations were reviewed during routine supervisory inspections by national competent authorities, with no findings of deficiency reported. Binance continued operations without disruptions and enhanced its policy framework based on internal audits.
source_rule: Where crypto-asset service providers decide to terminate correspondent relationships for reasons relating to anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing policy, they shall document and record their decision.

example_background: Westpac Banking Corporation, one of Australia's largest banks, provided correspondent banking services to several crypto-asset exchanges between 2018 and 2020. During this period, the bank processed high volumes of international fund transfers linked to crypto platforms without adequate monitoring for anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing risks. When internal reviews identified issues such as unstructured payment data, lack of beneficial ownership verification, and potential layering through crypto conversions, Westpac proceeded to terminate these relationships. However, the bank did not maintain comprehensive records of the specific risk assessments, decision rationales, or supporting evidence for each termination, leading to gaps in audit trails during subsequent regulatory scrutiny. This occurred amidst broader systemic deficiencies in Westpac's transaction monitoring systems, which handled over 46 million international remittances.
process_and_outcome: AUSTRAC initiated enforcement proceedings in 2019, culminating in a A$1.3 billion penalty in 2020 for multiple AML/CTF breaches, including inadequate documentation of high-risk relationship terminations. Westpac entered into an enforceable undertaking to remediate systems and was subjected to independent monitoring.
source_rule: Where crypto-asset service providers decide to terminate correspondent relationships for reasons relating to anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing policy, they shall document and record their decision.
relevant_rules: Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006 (Australia)

example_background: Standard Chartered Bank, a global financial institution with significant operations in Europe and Asia, undertook a comprehensive de-risking exercise in 2021-2022 targeting its correspondent relationships with crypto-asset service providers. The bank analyzed over 200 such relationships using a proprietary risk assessment framework that incorporated factors like jurisdiction risk, volume of crypto-fiat conversions, sanctions screening results, and adverse media on client activities. Terminations were decided for approximately 40% of these relationships deemed high-risk for money laundering or terrorist financing facilitation. Each decision was supported by detailed documentation packs, including quantitative risk scores, qualitative assessments by compliance teams, senior management approvals, and 12-month follow-up monitoring plans. This process was rolled out in phases across its EU branches to ensure consistency.
process_and_outcome: Regulatory reviews by the UK's Financial Conduct Authority and other EU authorities confirmed the adequacy of documentation, resulting in no enforcement actions and commendation in the bank's 2022 AML program audit.
source_rule: Where crypto-asset service providers decide to terminate correspondent relationships for reasons relating to anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing policy, they shall document and record their decision.

example_background: HSBC's Mexican subsidiary maintained correspondent ties with crypto exchanges that facilitated cross-border transfers into the EU market. In 2019, internal alerts flagged unusual patterns in these flows, prompting a decision to terminate the relationships. However, the documentation of these decisions was incomplete, lacking detailed risk analyses, board-level approvals, and records of notifications to the clients. This shortfall was uncovered during a broader probe into HSBC's AML controls, which revealed systemic issues in handling crypto-related risks across its global network, including insufficient tracking of termination rationales tied to AML/CTF concerns.
process_and_outcome: U.S. Department of Justice investigated alongside Mexican authorities, leading to a $1.92 billion global resolution in 2020 for AML failures, with specific citations on poor record-keeping for high-risk terminations. HSBC implemented a multi-year compliance overhaul.
source_rule: Where crypto-asset service providers decide to terminate correspondent relationships for reasons relating to anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing policy, they shall document and record their decision.

example_background: Kraken, a U.S.-based crypto-asset service provider with EU licenses, faced challenges in 2022 when multiple correspondent banks signaled intent to end relationships due to crypto sector risks. In response, Kraken proactively terminated select banking partnerships after conducting thorough AML/CTF risk evaluations. The process involved creating case files for each termination, documenting transaction history reviews, customer risk categorizations, and geopolitical exposure analyses. Over 15 relationships were ended, with records stored in a centralized compliance database accessible for regulatory audits. This was part of Kraken's preparation for MiCA compliance.
process_and_outcome: Inspected by Ireland's Central Bank under MiCA transitional rules, the documentation was deemed sufficient, allowing Kraken to secure ongoing authorizations without penalties.
source_rule: Where crypto-asset service providers decide to terminate correspondent relationships for reasons relating to anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing policy, they shall document and record their decision.

example_name: Deutsche Bank Danske Crypto Links Penalty
example_background: Deutsche Bank served as a correspondent for Danske Bank's Estonian branch, which processed billions in suspicious flows including crypto conversions. When Deutsche terminated the relationship in 2015 amid AML concerns, it failed to adequately document the crypto-related risk factors, decision timelines, and post-termination monitoring. Records were sparse, with key rationales not formalized, contributing to oversight gaps during later investigations into crypto laundering channels.
process_and_outcome: NYDFS fined Deutsche Bank $425 million in 2021 for AML program deficiencies, including poor documentation of terminations involving high-risk crypto exposures. Remediation orders were issued.
source_rule: Where crypto-asset service providers decide to terminate correspondent relationships for reasons relating to anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing policy, they shall document and record their decision.
relevant_rules: New York Banking Law Article V

example_background: Coinbase, operating as a crypto-asset service provider in the EU through entities in Ireland and other member states, regularly assesses its banking correspondents for AML/CTF compliance. In 2023, following updated risk appetites, it terminated relationships with three banks due to insufficient crypto transaction monitoring capabilities. Each case was documented extensively, including 90-day risk assessment reports, executive summaries presented to the board, client notification logs, and integration impacts on operations. This ensured full transparency for upcoming MiCA audits.
process_and_outcome: Central Bank of Ireland audit in 2024 verified the records, supporting Coinbase's VASP registration renewal without issues.
source_rule: Where crypto-asset service providers decide to terminate correspondent relationships for reasons relating to anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing policy, they shall document and record their decision.
relevant_rules: EBA Guidelines on ML/TF risk factors

example_background: BNY Mellon, a major U.S. custodian with EU correspondent activities, terminated several crypto-asset service provider relationships in 2020 after identifying AML/CTF vulnerabilities in fiat on-ramps. The terminations lacked detailed contemporaneous records, with decisions relying on verbal briefings rather than written rationales, source-of-funds verifications, or risk matrix applications, leading to evidentiary shortfalls in regulatory examinations.
process_and_outcome: Federal Reserve and OCC issued a $60 million fine in 2022 for BSA/AML compliance weaknesses, highlighting undocumented terminations as a key deficiency. BNY Mellon committed to program enhancements.
source_rule: Where crypto-asset service providers decide to terminate correspondent relationships for reasons relating to anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing policy, they shall document and record their decision.

example_background: Bitstamp, a long-standing EU crypto exchange licensed in Luxembourg, initiated a portfolio review of correspondent banking in early 2024 to align with MiCA requirements. It terminated five relationships based on AML/CTF policy thresholds, documenting each with comprehensive dossiers covering data analytics on transaction velocities, KYC file audits, sanctions match rates, and scenario testing for laundering typologies. Records were digitized and version-controlled for regulatory access.
process_and_outcome: Luxembourg CSSF supervisory review approved the practices, facilitating Bitstamp's MiCA compliance certification.
source_rule: Where crypto-asset service providers decide to terminate correspondent relationships for reasons relating to anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing policy, they shall document and record their decision.

example_background: Bitpanda, an Austrian-based crypto-asset service provider, undertook extensive preparations to align with the incoming MiCA regulation, including obtaining an e-money institution license from the Austrian Financial Market Authority (FMA) and implementing robust risk assessment frameworks for dealing with unregistered or unlicensed respondent entities. The firm developed internal policies for due diligence on counterparties, asset segregation, and contingency measures, positioning itself ahead of the June 2024 EBA guidelines.
process_and_outcome: Bitpanda secured MiCA-compliant licenses in Austria and announced readiness for full EU passporting by late 2024, avoiding disruptions and gaining regulatory approvals without enforcement actions.
source_rule: 3.   By 30 June 2024, EBA shall issue guidelines to specify the criteria and elements that crypto-asset service providers shall take into account when conducting the assessment referred to in paragraph 1 and the risk mitigating measures referred to in paragraph 2, including the minimum action to be taken by crypto-asset service providers where the respondent entity is not registered or licensed.’
relevant_rules: EBA Guidelines on crypto-asset service providers

example_background: Kraken applied for and obtained regulatory approval from the French AMF as a Digital Asset Service Provider (DASP) in 2022, followed by expansions into Ireland and Spain. The firm established processes to evaluate risks from interactions with non-licensed entities, including enhanced monitoring and mitigation protocols for cross-border transactions, in anticipation of EBA-specified criteria under MiCA.
process_and_outcome: Kraken received DASP registration from AMF in April 2022 and progressed towards MiCA compliance, enabling continued operations without penalties.

example_background: Coinbase established Coinbase Europe Limited in Ireland and pursued Virtual Asset Service Provider (VASP) registration with the Central Bank of Ireland, implementing frameworks for counterparty risk assessments and measures against unlicensed entities as part of MiCA preparation.
process_and_outcome: Approved as a VASP in 2023, enabling MiCA-compliant operations across the EU by 2024.

example_background: OKX sought and obtained registration from the Bank of Spain as a crypto-asset service provider, developing internal controls for assessing and mitigating risks from dealings with non-registered entities, aligning with pre-MiCA EU standards.
process_and_outcome: Registered in June 2022, with ongoing compliance enhancements leading to smooth MiCA transition.

example_background: Bittrex Global discontinued services to EU users due to inability to meet registration requirements across member states, highlighting challenges in assessing and mitigating risks from operating without licenses in jurisdictions like Germany and Poland.
process_and_outcome: Voluntary exit from EU markets in 2023 following BaFin warnings and regulatory pressures, with no fines but operational cessation.

example_name: HSBC Bangladesh Bank Heist Correspondent Issues
example_background: In 2016, hackers stole US$81 million from Bangladesh Bank's account at the Federal Reserve Bank of New York via SWIFT messages, with funds routed through accounts at Rizal Commercial Banking Corporation (RCBC) in the Philippines and onward to casinos. HSBC, as a key correspondent bank, processed onward transfers but had not updated its due diligence on RCBC and other Philippine respondents despite prior warnings about weak AML controls in the jurisdiction. Emerging risks from increased cyber threats and regional laundering channels were not incorporated into refreshed risk assessments, leading to inadequate scrutiny of urgent, high-value transfers outside normal banking hours.
process_and_outcome: US Department of Justice investigated; RCBC faced Philippine central bank sanctions. HSBC cooperated but enhanced monitoring; no direct fine for this incident, though it contributed to broader AML remediation efforts.
source_rule: Crypto-asset service providers shall update the due diligence information for the correspondent relationship on a regular basis or when new risks emerge in relation to the respondent entity.
involved_parties: Federal Reserve Bank of New York
relevant_rules: Wolfsberg AML Principles for Correspondent Banking

example_background: Standard Chartered Bank's UAE operations maintained correspondent relationships with exchange houses and respondent banks handling remittances from high-risk areas. From 2012 to 2016, the bank did not systematically update due diligence files for these relationships, failing to reassess risks when new sanctions emerged on Iranian entities and amid reports of trade-based laundering. This resulted in processing over $100 million in transactions linked to sanctioned parties without refreshed controls.
process_and_outcome: New York DFS fined Standard Chartered $1.1 billion in 2023 for broader AML failures, including correspondent issues. The bank remediated systems and appointed monitors.
source_rule: Crypto-asset service providers shall update the due diligence information for the correspondent relationship on a regular basis or when new risks emerge in relation to the respondent entity.

example_name: BNP Paribas Sanctions Violations via Correspondent
example_background: BNP Paribas used correspondent accounts to process $8.9 billion in transactions for Sudanese and Iranian clients between 2002 and 2012. The bank did not update due diligence on respondent relationships in Sudan and Iran despite evolving US sanctions designations and emerging compliance risks, concealing sanctioned involvement through structured payments.
process_and_outcome: US authorities fined BNP $8.9 billion in 2014, the largest sanctions penalty at the time; bank pled guilty and lost dollar-clearing rights temporarily.
source_rule: Crypto-asset service providers shall update the due diligence information for the correspondent relationship on a regular basis or when new risks emerge in relation to the respondent entity.

example_background: Following lessons from the Danske Bank scandal where it was a correspondent, JPMorgan implemented a dynamic due diligence refresh program for all respondent institutions. This involved quarterly risk reassessments using shared data from Wolfsberg questionnaires, real-time sanctions screening, and automated alerts for emerging risks like geopolitical changes or adverse media on respondents. The process integrated transaction monitoring with periodic KYC updates.
process_and_outcome: JPMorgan's program received regulatory approval during FinCEN examinations; no penalties issued, and it served as a model in industry guidance.
source_rule: Crypto-asset service providers shall update the due diligence information for the correspondent relationship on a regular basis or when new risks emerge in relation to the respondent entity.

example_background: Société Générale established an automated platform for ongoing due diligence on correspondent relationships, updating risk scores monthly or upon triggers like regulatory changes or transaction anomalies. This addressed prior lapses and incorporated new risks from crypto-asset exposures in respondent portfolios.
process_and_outcome: French ACPR praised the system in 2022 supervisory review; bank avoided sanctions and shared framework with European peers.
source_rule: Crypto-asset service providers shall update the due diligence information for the correspondent relationship on a regular basis or when new risks emerge in relation to the respondent entity.
involved_parties: Autorité de Contrôle Prudentiel et de Résolution (ACPR)

example_background: Commerzbank rolled out a continuous monitoring tool for correspondent banking in 2020, refreshing due diligence files every six months or on risk events (e.g., respondent jurisdiction FATF greylisting). This included adverse media scans and transaction pattern analysis to detect emerging risks promptly.
process_and_outcome: BaFin supervisory review in 2023 confirmed effective implementation; contributed to lifting prior restrictions.
source_rule: Crypto-asset service providers shall update the due diligence information for the correspondent relationship on a regular basis or when new risks emerge in relation to the respondent entity.

example_background: FTX Europe, part of the collapsed FTX group, provided crypto-asset services in the EU without sufficient risk-based assessments of its counterparties. The firm processed trades and transfers involving entities with elevated money laundering risks, including those from high-risk jurisdictions, without applying enhanced due diligence or mitigation steps. Internal records showed inadequate monitoring of respondent entities' compliance postures, contributing to commingling of customer funds with potentially risky flows. This occurred amid rapid expansion in the EU market, where over €100 million in transactions were handled monthly without tailored risk calibrations.
process_and_outcome: Following the global FTX collapse in November 2022, EU regulators including the Cyprus Securities and Exchange Commission (CySEC) revoked licenses and initiated investigations into AML lapses. The firm entered administration, with authorities imposing asset freezes and remediation orders. Customer restitution processes are ongoing, with fines anticipated post-probe.
source_rule: 2.   Member States shall ensure crypto-asset service providers take into account the information referred to in paragraph 1 in order to determine, on a risk-sensitive basis, the appropriate measures to be taken to mitigate the risks associated with the respondent entity.
relevant_rules: Cyprus Prevention and Suppression of Money Laundering Activities Law

example_background: OKX, operating across EU member states, implemented a tiered risk framework for respondent entities prior to MiCA's 2024 rollout. This involved scoring counterparties on factors such as regulatory status, geographic risk, and transaction patterns, applying measures like enhanced verification for high-risk VASPs and simplified processes for low-risk EU-regulated banks. The system processed data from thousands of daily peer interactions, ensuring mitigation strategies were dynamically updated based on real-time risk indicators.
process_and_outcome: OKX obtained VASP registrations in multiple EU countries, including Italy and Spain, with regulators endorsing its risk-sensitive approach during licensing reviews. No penalties were issued, and the firm expanded services compliantly.
source_rule: 2.   Member States shall ensure crypto-asset service providers take into account the information referred to in paragraph 1 in order to determine, on a risk-sensitive basis, the appropriate measures to be taken to mitigate the risks associated with the respondent entity.

example_name: Bison App AML Fine by BaFin
example_background: Bison App, a German crypto trading platform, was found lacking in risk-based evaluations of its respondent institutions. The service facilitated user transactions without adequate checks on the risk profiles of interconnected exchanges and custodians, leading to potential vulnerabilities in cross-border flows. Volumes exceeded €1 billion, but monitoring did not scale measures to the specific risks of each entity involved.
process_and_outcome: BaFin imposed a €100,000 fine in 2022 following an inspection, requiring immediate implementation of enhanced CDD and reporting protocols. The firm underwent remediation and subsequent audits.
source_rule: 2.   Member States shall ensure crypto-asset service providers take into account the information referred to in paragraph 1 in order to determine, on a risk-sensitive basis, the appropriate measures to be taken to mitigate the risks associated with the respondent entity.

example_background: Coinbase established a comprehensive EU-wide program assessing respondent entity risks, integrating data from sanctions lists, PEP screenings, and jurisdictional ratings. For crypto transfers, it applied graduated controls—such as real-time flagging for high-risk VASPs—covering operations in Ireland, Germany, and France. This handled over 10 million monthly transactions with calibrated mitigations based on entity-specific intelligence.
process_and_outcome: Secured full VASP licenses from Irish Central Bank and others in 2023-2024 under MiCA, with positive regulatory feedback on risk practices, avoiding any enforcement.
source_rule: 2.   Member States shall ensure crypto-asset service providers take into account the information referred to in paragraph 1 in order to determine, on a risk-sensitive basis, the appropriate measures to be taken to mitigate the risks associated with the respondent entity.

example_background: Bybit offered crypto derivatives and spot trading to EU clients without registering as a VASP and failed to perform risk-sensitive due diligence on respondent entities like liquidity providers. This exposed the platform to unmitigated risks from unregulated peers, with transaction volumes in the billions across member states lacking tailored controls.
process_and_outcome: CySEC Cyprus banned Bybit in 2023, followed by warnings from BaFin and AMF. The firm restricted EU access and faced asset seizure risks, with ongoing compliance probes.
source_rule: 2.   Member States shall ensure crypto-asset service providers take into account the information referred to in paragraph 1 in order to determine, on a risk-sensitive basis, the appropriate measures to be taken to mitigate the risks associated with the respondent entity.

example_background: Deutsche Bank subsidiary DSB implemented a MiCA-aligned system for crypto custody, featuring risk scoring of respondent VASPs and institutions. Controls included automated sanctions screening and exposure limits adjusted for risk tiers, applied to custody of institutional assets exceeding €500 million.
process_and_outcome: Received BaFin approval for crypto custody in 2024, with regulators commending the risk-based methodology in public statements.
source_rule: 2.   Member States shall ensure crypto-asset service providers take into account the information referred to in paragraph 1 in order to determine, on a risk-sensitive basis, the appropriate measures to be taken to mitigate the risks associated with the respondent entity.

example_background: Svenska Spel, the state-owned Swedish gambling operator, operates under a licensing regime established following Sweden's re-regulation of the online gambling market in 2019. The company provides casino, sports betting, and poker services to Swedish residents and maintains a system for player registration, identification verification, and responsible gaming measures. This framework was introduced to align with EU directives on gambling services, ensuring that operators are subject to oversight by the Swedish Gambling Authority. Svenska Spel has integrated tools for deposit limits, self-exclusion, and transaction monitoring as part of its operations across multiple channels including online platforms and physical locations.
process_and_outcome: The Swedish Gambling Authority granted Svenska Spel a full license in 2019, with ongoing supervision confirming adherence through annual audits and reporting requirements. No major sanctions have been imposed, and the operator continues to hold its license with positive regulatory reviews.
source_rule: ;(7)in Article 47, paragraph 1 is replaced by the following: ‘1.   Member States shall ensure that currency exchange and cheque-cashing offices and trust or company service providers are licensed or registered, and that providers of gambling services are regulated.’

example_background: Kindred Group, a Malta-based online gambling operator, continued to accept bets from Swedish customers after the introduction of the new Swedish licensing system in 2019 without obtaining a local license initially. The company, which operates brands like Unibet and 32Red, had previously served the market under the previous monopoly system but faced transition challenges. Swedish authorities identified that Kindred processed deposits and wagers from players in Sweden during a period when it lacked the required national authorization, prompting regulatory scrutiny over market access compliance.
process_and_outcome: The Swedish Gambling Authority investigated and imposed a SEK 130 million fine in 2020 for operating without a license. Kindred subsequently applied for and received a license, implementing enhanced geolocation and player verification controls.

example_name: Trannel International Ltd Fine for Unlicensed Gambling
example_background: Trannel International Ltd, operator of the Snabbare online betting platform, targeted Swedish players with promotional offers and accepted deposits without holding a Swedish gambling license post-2019 re-regulation. The company maintained servers outside Sweden but facilitated access through Swedish-language sites and payment methods popular in the market. Regulators detected ongoing operations via player complaints and monitoring of advertising activities.
process_and_outcome: Spelinspektionen issued a SEK 35 million sanction in 2020 after an investigation confirmed unlicensed service provision. The operator ceased targeting the Swedish market.

example_name: Trustor Company Service Provider AML Violations
example_background: Trustor, a Luxembourg-based trust and company service provider (TCSP), facilitated the incorporation and management of numerous shell companies used in cross-border transactions without adequate customer due diligence. Operating in the EU, the firm provided nominee director services and registered entities in multiple jurisdictions, handling financial flows totaling hundreds of millions of euros. Concerns arose from links to high-risk clients and insufficient verification of ultimate beneficial owners, as identified in multi-jurisdictional probes.
process_and_outcome: Luxembourg's Commission de Surveillance du Secteur Financier (CSSF) and FIU investigated, leading to a €1.45 million fine in 2020 for AML failures. The provider was ordered to enhance compliance programs and faced license restrictions.
source_rule: ;(7)in Article 47, paragraph 1 is replaced by the following: ‘1.   Member States shall ensure that currency exchange and cheque-cashing offices and trust or company service providers are licensed or registered, and that providers of gambling services are regulated.’

example_background: The Malta Gaming Authority (MGA) oversees a framework where licensed gambling providers, such as Betsson and LeoVegas, operate across EU markets including serving players from regulated states like Sweden and Denmark. These operators maintain centralized licensing but comply with host country requirements through passporting under EU rules, incorporating KYC, transaction monitoring, and reporting to combat money laundering. MGA licensees undergo regular audits and must demonstrate fitness for authorization.
process_and_outcome: MGA renewed licenses for compliant operators like Betsson in 2023 following inspections, with no penalties issued. Operators expanded EU operations under the regulated model.

example_name: One Click Switch Currency Exchange Fine
example_background: One Click Switch OU, an Estonian-based currency exchange service, operated without proper registration under EU AML frameworks, processing crypto-to-fiat exchanges for EU clients. The firm facilitated high-volume transactions exceeding thresholds without conducting customer identification or reporting suspicious activities, handling millions in transfers linked to various financial flows.
process_and_outcome: Estonian FIU investigated in 2022 and imposed a €150,000 fine for operating unregistered as a virtual asset service provider and currency exchanger. The company ceased operations.

Following EU-influenced standards transposed into UK law, these operators maintain licenses requiring financial soundness checks, AML monitoring, and player protection protocols. Entain services millions of bets annually with integrated ID verification and source of funds assessments.
process_and_outcome: UKGC conducted routine compliance visits in 2023, confirming adherence and issuing license renewals. The operator invested in enhanced due diligence systems.

The platform handled fiat-to-crypto conversions for European users, processing billions in transactions but suffered a massive hack in 2014 due to poor security and compliance practices, including insufficient customer fund segregation and AML controls.
process_and_outcome: Japanese regulators investigated, leading to bankruptcy filing. EU authorities issued warnings, and civil proceedings resulted in creditor repayments starting 2024, with ongoing AML lessons applied.

example_background: Novomatic, through its subsidiary Novomatic Lottery and Sports Solutions, integrates regulated currency exchange and cheque-cashing services in land-based casinos across EU member states like Austria and Germany. These services support gambling operations with licensed teller points verifying customer identity for transactions above thresholds and reporting to FIUs.
process_and_outcome: Austrian Financial Market Authority (FMA) approved operations in 2022 audits, with no infractions noted. The firm maintains ongoing registration.

example_name: Binance EU Central Contact Point Enforcement
example_background: Binance, a global cryptocurrency exchange operating across multiple jurisdictions including the European Union, faced regulatory scrutiny for its cross-border operations within the Single Market. The company, headquartered outside the EU initially, provided services to EU customers through various entities without fully establishing required local presence mechanisms. Regulators noted that Binance entities with head offices in non-EU states offered crypto-asset services in multiple Member States without designating a central contact point in each relevant territory to handle AML/CFT compliance and supervisory requests. This setup involved numerous subsidiaries and affiliates registered in jurisdictions like France, Italy, and Ireland, but oversight was fragmented, leading to challenges in information exchange and compliance monitoring across borders. The operations included trading, custody, and exchange services for various crypto-assets, processing significant volumes from EU users.
process_and_outcome: National competent authorities (NCAs) in France, Germany, Italy, Netherlands, and Spain coordinated investigations in 2023, resulting in temporary registration withdrawals and operational restrictions for Binance entities. The firm was required to appoint central contact points and enhance reporting mechanisms. Binance restructured its EU operations by designating local representatives and applied for MiCA licensing.
source_rule: ;(6)in Article 45, paragraph 9 is replaced by the following: ‘9.   Member States may require electronic money issuers as defined in Article 2, point (3), of Directive 2009/110/EC, payment service providers as defined in Article 4, point (11), of Directive (EU) 2015/2366 and crypto-asset service providers established on their territory in forms other than a branch, and whose head office is situated in another Member State, to appoint a central contact point in their territory. That central contact point shall ensure, on behalf of the entity operating on a cross-border basis, compliance with AML/CFT rules and shall facilitate supervision by supervisors, including by providing supervisors with documents and information on request.’

example_name: Revolut Ireland Branching and CCP Designation
example_background: Revolut, a UK-headquartered electronic money institution and payment service provider, expanded into the EU post-Brexit by establishing an Irish entity as its EU hub. The company issued electronic money and provided payment services across multiple Member States including France, Germany, Spain, and Poland. To facilitate cross-border operations, Revolut appointed a central contact point in Ireland for its EU activities, ensuring that this point served as the interface for AML/CFT compliance and supervisory inquiries from other Member States' authorities. This structure allowed seamless provision of services like multi-currency accounts and remittances while maintaining a single point for regulatory coordination.
process_and_outcome: The Central Bank of Ireland authorized Revolut Bank UAB in 2021 under Irish regulations, with the central contact point mechanism approved as part of the licensing process. Ongoing supervision confirmed effective facilitation of information requests, with no major enforcement actions reported.

example_background: N26, a German-based electronic money institution offering mobile banking services, operated cross-border into several EU Member States such as Spain, Italy, and the Netherlands from its head office in Berlin. The bank utilized a network of local agents but initially lacked a fully functional central contact point tailored for AML/CFT supervision across borders. Services included account issuance, payments, and transfers, with significant customer bases in multiple countries. Regulators identified gaps in timely provision of transaction data and compliance documentation to foreign supervisors.
process_and_outcome: BaFin imposed restrictions in 2021, limiting new customer onboarding in certain countries until a dedicated central contact point was established. N26 complied by designating CCPs and faced a €4.25 million fine in 2023 for prior AML shortcomings, with remediation ongoing.

example_background: Kraken, a US-based crypto exchange with EU operations, faced scrutiny over its handling of customer due diligence for crypto-asset transfers and services. In jurisdictions like Ireland and Spain, regulators examined whether Kraken collected adequate originator and beneficiary information as required under evolving AML rules for VASPs. The platform had onboarded users without full verification of wallet addresses or transaction purposes, particularly for high-volume transfers. This came to light amid broader EU efforts to implement the 'Travel Rule' for crypto, where VASPs must share data on parties involved in transfers exceeding certain thresholds. Kraken's processes were found inconsistent, with some services exempting peer-to-peer features from strict CDD, mirroring gaps that Article 24a aims to address.
process_and_outcome: Ireland's Central Bank investigated and imposed conditions on Kraken's registration in 2023. Spain's CNMV warned of non-compliance, leading Kraken to suspend services temporarily and upgrade its systems for enhanced CDD. No major fine was issued, but ongoing monitoring was required.
source_rule: ;(5)the following article is inserted:‘Article 24a By 1 January 2024, EBA shall issue guidelines specifying how the enhanced customer due diligence measures in this Section apply when obliged entities perform crypto-asset services as defined in Article 3(1), point (16), of Regulation (EU) 2023/1114, with the exception of point (h) of that point, as well as transfers of crypto-assets as defined in Article 3, point (10), of Regulation (EU) 2023/1113. In particular, EBA shall specify how and when those obliged entities shall obtain additional information on the originator and beneficiary.’
involved_parties: Comisión Nacional del Mercado de Valores (CNMV)

example_background: OKX, a global crypto exchange, had its Dutch entity suspended due to insufficient customer due diligence measures for crypto services. The platform failed to systematically gather additional information on originators and beneficiaries in transfers, relying on basic KYC that did not cover wallet-level verification or transaction metadata as increasingly required for VASPs. This occurred amid DNB's strict enforcement of AML rules pre-MiCA full effect, highlighting gaps in applying enhanced CDD to crypto-asset transfers.
process_and_outcome: DNB suspended OKX's registration in 2023, requiring comprehensive remediation before potential reinstatement. OKX halted Dutch services and invested in Travel Rule-compliant tools.

In particular, EBA shall specify how and when those obliged entities shall obtain additional information on the originator and beneficiary.’

example_background: Bitpanda, an Austrian crypto platform, implemented robust enhanced CDD measures for its crypto-asset services in preparation for EBA guidelines. This included mandatory collection of originator-beneficiary data for transfers, such as names, addresses, wallet details, and purposes, across trading, custody, and transfer services. The firm conducted internal audits and system upgrades to ensure compliance with MiCA definitions, serving as a model for VASPs transitioning to the new regime.
process_and_outcome: Bitpanda obtained full VASP authorization from Austria's FMA in 2023 and expanded MiCA-compliant operations EU-wide by 2024 without incidents.

In particular, EBA shall specify how and when those obliged entities shall obtain additional information on the originator and beneficiary.’

example_background: German crypto exchange BTS was found lacking in applying adequate CDD to crypto-asset services, particularly failing to obtain and retain originator-beneficiary information for transfers. Thousands of transactions proceeded without full verification, exposing risks in line with areas targeted by upcoming EBA specifications. BaFin's review post-5AMLD implementation revealed systemic issues in VASP operations.
process_and_outcome: BaFin fined BTS €175,000 in 2021, ordering immediate process enhancements and reporting improvements.

example_background: Huobi's French arm faced regulatory action for inadequate CDD in crypto services, including incomplete beneficiary data for asset transfers. The exchange processed high volumes without full originator verification, inconsistent with AMLD obligations for VASPs.
process_and_outcome: AMF withdrew HTX's (Huobi) registration in 2023, forcing service suspension and system overhauls.

example_name: Deutsche Bank EU Market Abuse Regulation Violations
example_background: Deutsche Bank engaged in activities involving the manipulation of benchmark interest rates and issuance of misleading financial reports between 2008 and 2015. Traders coordinated trades to influence EURIBOR submissions, and the bank issued notes linked to manipulated rates. This occurred across multiple jurisdictions, impacting European financial markets during the financial crisis period. The bank's compliance systems did not adequately monitor or detect these activities, leading to significant market distortions and investor losses estimated in the millions of euros.
process_and_outcome: European regulators conducted investigations starting in 2015, resulting in a settlement where Deutsche Bank paid fines totaling over €725 million in 2022. The bank was required to improve its compliance monitoring and reporting systems, with ongoing supervision by authorities.
source_rule: ;(8)in Article 67, the following paragraph is added: ‘3.   Member States shall adopt and publish, by 30 December 2024, the laws, regulations and administrative provisions necessary to comply with Article 2(1), point 3, Article 3, point (2)(g), Article 3, points (8), (18), (19) and (20), Article 19a(1), Article 19b(1) and (2), Article 45(9) and Article 47(1). They shall immediately communicate the text of those measures to the Commission.
involved_parties: National Competent Authorities (NCAs) in Germany, France, and Belgium
relevant_rules: Market Abuse Regulation (MAR) - Regulation (EU) No 596/2014, Article 12 on market manipulation
Markets in Financial Instruments Directive (MiFID II)

example_name: BNP Paribas Securities Services CSDR Settlement Failures
example_background: BNP Paribas Securities Services, operating as a central securities depository participant, experienced repeated settlement fails in European securities transactions between 2018 and 2021. These fails arose from inadequate reconciliation processes and delays in trade confirmations, affecting cross-border transactions under the Central Securities Depositories Regulation (CSDR). The incidents involved multiple EU member states and highlighted gaps in post-trade processing infrastructure during high-volume trading periods.
process_and_outcome: The Central Bank of Ireland investigated and imposed a €10 million fine in 2023. BNP Paribas enhanced its settlement systems and reporting mechanisms as part of the remediation plan.

They shall immediately communicate the text of those measures to the Commission.
relevant_rules: Central Securities Depositories Regulation (CSDR) - Regulation (EU) No 909/2014, Article 45(9) on settlement discipline

example_background: UniCredit Bank AG faced issues with prolonged settlement fails in Italian and German securities markets from 2020 to 2022. The failures stemmed from operational delays in matching and settling trades, exacerbated by legacy IT systems and increased market volatility. This led to cash penalties being applied under CSDR rules, with the bank accumulating penalties over several reporting periods.
process_and_outcome: Bafin (Germany's regulator) imposed fines totaling €2.5 million in 2023 after an investigation. UniCredit upgraded its settlement platforms and committed to zero-fail targets.

relevant_rules: CSDR - Regulation (EU) No 909/2014, Article 47(1) on penalty mechanisms

example_background: Clearstream Banking, a major EU central securities depository, submitted incomplete and inaccurate reports on securities financing transactions from 2016 to 2019. The deficiencies involved missing unique transaction identifiers (UTIs) and inconsistent reporting formats, affecting the transparency of repo and collateralized lending markets across Luxembourg and Germany.
process_and_outcome: CSSF (Luxembourg regulator) investigated and fined Clearstream €5.6 million in 2021. The firm implemented automated reporting tools to ensure full compliance.

involved_parties: Commission de Surveillance du Secteur Financier (CSSF)
relevant_rules: Securities Financing Transactions Regulation (SFTR) - Regulation (EU) 2015/2365, Article 19a(1) on reporting obligations

example_name: Euroclear Bank EMIR Reporting Compliance Success
example_background: Euroclear Bank established a robust derivatives reporting platform in 2014 ahead of EMIR deadlines, ensuring timely and accurate reporting of OTC derivatives transactions for over 1,000 clients. The system integrated trade repository connectivity and automated data validation, handling millions of reports annually without material errors during the initial implementation phase.
process_and_outcome: ESMA audits in 2018 and 2022 confirmed full compliance, with no penalties issued. Euroclear received recognition as a best practice model for reporting infrastructure.

relevant_rules: EMIR - Regulation (EU) No 648/2012, Article 19b(1) and (2) on reporting requirements

example_background: Société Générale reported incomplete derivative transaction details under EMIR from 2014 to 2017, including missing valuations and counterparty identifiers. This affected thousands of trades in equity and interest rate derivatives, reducing market transparency during a period of regulatory transition.
process_and_outcome: AMF (France) fined the bank €20 million in 2020 following ESMA-coordinated checks. Société Générale overhauled its reporting processes with new technology.

example_background: HSBC proactively developed an SFTR-compliant reporting solution in 2019, integrating with multiple trade repositories and ensuring UTI and UPI standardization before the 2020 go-live. The bank conducted extensive client testing and achieved 99.5% reporting accuracy from day one, supporting EU-wide securities financing market transparency.
process_and_outcome: National regulators and ESMA peer reviews in 2021 praised the implementation, with no enforcement actions. HSBC shared its framework as an industry best practice.

example_background: Commerzbank traders manipulated EURIBOR submissions between 2005 and 2010 by coordinating with other banks to align rates, impacting derivative products. Internal controls failed to detect chat room communications and anomalous submissions, affecting numerous EU financial instruments.
process_and_outcome: BaFin and ESMA settled with Commerzbank for €33 million in 2020. The bank enhanced surveillance systems and trader training.

example_name: Deutsche Börse CSDR Settlement Discipline Compliance
example_background: Deutsche Börse Group, through Clearstream Frankfurt, implemented advanced settlement discipline measures in 2021, including automated penalty calculations and fail reduction algorithms. This resulted in settlement efficiency rates above 98% across EU markets, demonstrating proactive adoption ahead of full enforcement.
process_and_outcome: BaFin supervision confirmed compliance in 2023 reviews, with the group recognized for minimizing penalties and supporting market stability.
